> The **AFS number** is like calculating a *single rating score* — the "average particle size" of that mixed sand — so that a foundry can quickly judge whether this particular sand is suitable for casting delicate jewellery (needs very fine sand → high AFS) versus casting large engine blocks (needs coarser, more permeable sand → low AFS).

### 1.3 Standard Sieves Used

The test uses a set of **11 standard AFS sieves** (US Standard) mounted on a mechanical sieve shaker for exactly **15 minutes**.

| AFS Sieve No. | Aperture (µm) | Multiplier |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 6 | 3320 | 3 |
| 12 | 1680 | 5 |
| 20 | 840 | 10 |
| 30 | 590 | 20 |
| 40 | 420 | 30 |
| 50 | 297 | 40 |
| 70 | 210 | 50 |
| 100 | 149 | 70 |
| 140 | 105 | 100 |
| 200 | 74 | 140 |
| 270 | 53 | 200 |
| Pan | < 53 | 300 |

> [!NOTE]
> The **multipliers** are not random — each one is the sieve number that *just retains* the corresponding grain, so multiplying mass × multiplier gives a "size-weighted contribution" of that fraction to the total.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

The sieve analysis and AFS number determination follow a strictly defined sequence:

1. **Sample Preparation**
   * Take **50 g** of representative, oven-dried (105 °C – 110 °C for 1 hour) silica sand.
   * Cool to room temperature in a desiccator to prevent moisture-driven clumping.

2. **Stack Assembly**
   * Stack the **11 AFS sieves** in *descending* aperture size (6 at top, 270 at bottom).
   * Place a **receiver pan** at the very bottom and a **lid** at the very top.

3. **Sieving Operation**
   * Pour the sample gently onto the top sieve (No. 6).
   * Place the lid and clamp the stack onto a **mechanical sieve shaker**.
   * Run the shaker for **15 minutes** (industry standard duration).

4. **Weighing & Recording**
   * Carefully remove each sieve and weigh the sand retained on it.
   * Record masses to the nearest **0.1 g**.
   * Verify that: **Σ (weight retained on every sieve + pan) = 50 g** (within ±0.1 g).

5. **Calculation of AFS Number**
   * Multiply each retained mass by its corresponding **AFS multiplier**.
   * Sum all these products (numerator).
   * Divide by the **total mass of sample** (denominator, ≈ 50 g).

6. **Reporting**
   * Report the **AFS Grain Fineness Number (GFN)** to the nearest whole number.
   * Optionally, plot a **cumulative weight % finer** vs. sieve aperture curve.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **AFS GFN** | $\text{AFS} = \dfrac{\sum_{i} (M_i \times m_i)}{W_{total}}$ | Master equation — multiplier × weight, summed, then divided by total weight |
| **% Retained on sieve i** | $R_i = \dfrac{M_i}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | Mass fraction retained on sieve *i* |
| **Cumulative % Retained** | $C_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} R_j$ | Running total of % retained from coarsest sieve down |
| **% Finer (Cumulative passing)** | $P_i = 100 - C_i$ | Useful for grading curves |
| **Median size, $d_{50}$** | Interpolate at $P_i = 50\%$ | The aperture through which 50 % of the sample passes |
| **Fineness Modulus (alternate index)** | $FM = \dfrac{\sum C_i}{100}$ | Similar to AFS, used in civil/aggregate testing |
| **Permeability indicator (empirical)** | $K \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{AFS}^{1.4}}$ | Higher AFS ⇒ lower permeability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention:** Mass $M_i$ in **grams (g)**; W_total in **grams (g)**; AFS GFN is dimensionless. Sieve aperture is in **micrometres (µm)** or **millimetres (mm)**.

---

### 2.3 Why the AFS Multiplier Method Works

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why":** Each AFS multiplier is the sieve number whose opening is approximately the **arithmetic mean** of the openings of the sieve *above* and the sieve *below* in the standard series. Therefore, the product $M_i \times m_i$ is numerically equivalent to *(mass retained on sieve i) × (a representative sieve number for that size fraction)*. Summing these products and dividing by total mass yields the *mean sieve number* — which by definition is the AFS GFN.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Foundry process control:** Daily AFS check ensures sand system consistency; deviation > ±3 triggers a re-blend.
- **Permeability prediction:** GFN directly controls mould permeability (escape of gases during pouring).
- **Surface finish:** Finer sand (high GFN) → smoother casting surface; coarser sand → rougher.
- **Casting defect avoidance:** Wrong GFN causes blows, scabs, metal penetration, and veining.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the AFS Number Formula

Consider a sand sample split into size fractions, each retained on one sieve.

Let the total sample mass be $W$ grams.
Let $M_i$ = mass retained on sieve *i* (g), and $m_i$ = AFS multiplier of sieve *i*.
The fraction of total mass retained on sieve *i* is $f_i = M_i / W$.

The *weighted average* of the multipliers is:

$$
\bar{m} = \sum_{i} f_i \cdot m_i = \sum_{i} \frac{M_i}{W} \cdot m_i
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $W$:

$$
\bar{m} = \frac{\sum_{i} (M_i \cdot m_i)}{\sum_{i} M_i}
$$

Since $\sum_i M_i = W$ (conservation of mass, neglecting dust loss):

$$
\boxed{\;\text{AFS GFN} = \bar{m} = \frac{\sum_{i} (M_i \cdot m_i)}{W_{total}}\;}
$$

The resulting number is interpreted as the *average sieve number* that just passes equivalent uniform grains. By AFS convention, this number is reported as a whole integer.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — AFS GFN Determination

**Given Data (representative sieve analysis of a moulding sand):**

| Sieve No. | Multiplier ($m_i$) | Weight Retained, $M_i$ (g) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 6 | 3 | 0.0 |
| 12 | 5 | 0.5 |
| 20 | 10 | 1.0 |
| 30 | 20 | 1.5 |
| 40 | 30 | 4.0 |
| 50 | 40 | 8.0 |
| 70 | 50 | 11.0 |
| 100 | 70 | 12.0 |
| 140 | 100 | 7.5 |
| 200 | 140 | 3.0 |
| 270 | 200 | 1.0 |
| Pan | 300 | 0.5 |
| **Total** | — | **50.0 g** |

---

**Step 1 — Verify total mass:**

$$
\sum M_i = 0.0 + 0.5 + 1.0 + 1.5 + 4.0 + 8.0 + 11.0 + 12.0 + 7.5 + 3.0 + 1.0 + 0.5 = 50.0 \text{ g} \;\checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each product $M_i \times m_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sieve 12:} \quad & 0.5 \times 5 = 2.5 \\
\text{Sieve 20:} \quad & 1.0 \times 10 = 10.0 \\
\text{Sieve 30:} \quad & 1.5 \times 20 = 30.0 \\
\text{Sieve 40:} \quad & 4.0 \times 30 = 120.0 \\
\text{Sieve 50:} \quad & 8.0 \times 40 = 320.0 \\
\text{Sieve 70:} \quad & 11.0 \times 50 = 550.0 \\
\text{Sieve 100:} \quad & 12.0 \times 70 = 840.0 \\
\text{Sieve 140:} \quad & 7.5 \times 100 = 750.0 \\
\text{Sieve 200:} \quad & 3.0 \times 140 = 420.0 \\
\text{Sieve 270:} \quad & 1.0 \times 200 = 200.0 \\
\text{Pan:} \quad & 0.5 \times 300 = 150.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum all products:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (M_i \cdot m_i) &= 2.5 + 10 + 30 + 120 + 320 + 550 + 840 + 750 + 420 + 200 + 150 \\
&= 3392.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the AFS formula:**

$$
\text{AFS GFN} = \frac{\sum (M_i \cdot m_i)}{W_{total}} = \frac{3392.5}{50.0} = 67.85
$$

**Step 5 — Report the value:**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{AFS Grain Fineness Number} = 68\;}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

Since the standard moulding-sand range is **45 – 60**, an AFS of 68 indicates a *finer-than-average* sand — suitable for cores and small castings, but the moulder may need to add coarser silica to improve permeability for larger castings.

---

### 3.3 Cumulative % Retained Matrix (Analytical Extension)

| Sieve | $M_i$ (g) | % Retained $R_i$ | Cumulative % Retained $C_i$ | % Finer $P_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 6 | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | 100.0 |
| 12 | 0.5 | 1.0 | 1.0 | 99.0 |
| 20 | 1.0 | 2.0 | 3.0 | 97.0 |
| 30 | 1.5 | 3.0 | 6.0 | 94.0 |
| 40 | 4.0 | 8.0 | 14.0 | 86.0 |
| 50 | 8.0 | 16.0 | 30.0 | 70.0 |
| 70 | 11.0 | 22.0 | 52.0 | 48.0 |
| 100 | 12.0 | 24.0 | 76.0 | 24.0 |
| 140 | 7.5 | 15.0 | 91.0 | 9.0 |
| 200 | 3.0 | 6.0 | 97.0 | 3.0 |
| 270 | 1.0 | 2.0 | 99.0 | 1.0 |
| Pan | 0.5 | 1.0 | 100.0 | 0.0 |

**Median size ($d_{50}$) determination:**
The 50 % finer point lies between Sieve 70 ($P=48\%$) and Sieve 50 ($P=70\%$).
Log-linear interpolation gives $d_{50} \approx 215 \, \mu m$, confirming a medium-fine sand.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Sieve Analysis & AFS Determination Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Collect Representative Moulding Sand Sample] --> B[Oven Dry at 105 C for 1 Hour]
    B --> C[Cool in Desiccator to Room Temp]
    C --> D[Weigh Exactly 50 g of Dried Sand]
    D --> E[Assemble 11 AFS Sieves Coarse at Top to Fine at Bottom]
    E --> F[Pour Sample onto Top Sieve No 6 and Close Lid]
    F --> G[Place Stack on Mechanical Sieve Shaker]
    G --> H[Shake for Exactly 15 Minutes]
    H --> I[Weigh Sand Retained on Each Sieve and Pan]
    I --> J{Check Mass Balance Equals 50 g}
    J -- No --> K[Reject Test and Repeat]
    J -- Yes --> L[Multiply Each Retained Mass by AFS Multiplier]
    L --> M[Sum All Products and Divide by Total Mass 50 g]
    M --> N[Report AFS GFN Rounded to Nearest Integer]
    N --> O{AFS Within Specified Range}
    O -- Yes --> P[Sand Accepted for Moulding]
    O -- No --> Q[Reclaim or Blend with Other Sand]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Map — How AFS GFN Controls Foundry Properties

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AFS[AFS Grain Fineness Number] --> P[Permeability Lower for Higher AFS]
    AFS --> S[Surface Finish Smoother for Higher AFS]
    AFS --> H[Hot Strength Higher for Higher AFS]
    AFS --> V[Permeability Variance Higher GFN Means Lower Voids]
    AFS --> C[Casting Defect Risk Tradeoff]
    P --> CA[Cast Quality Outcome]
    S --> CA
    H --> CA
    V --> CA
    C --> CA
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Sand Size Distribution (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C[Coarse Fraction Sieve 6 to 30] --> M[Medium Fraction Sieve 40 to 70]
    M --> F[Fine Fraction Sieve 100 to 270]
    F --> P[Pan Fines Below 53 Micron]
    C -->|Multiplier 3 to 20| AP[AFS GFN Calculation]
    M -->|Multiplier 30 to 50| AP
    F -->|Multiplier 70 to 200| AP
    P -->|Multiplier 300| AP
    AP --> RESULT[AFS Number Average Particle Size Index]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above diagrams represent the *block-level functional architecture* of the experiment (procedure, property relationships, and PSD structure) since a physical drawing of a sieve stack cannot be rendered as a Mermaid flowchart.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
> **Q1.** Define **AFS Grain Fineness Number**. State its typical range for general moulding sands.

**Model Answer:**
The AFS Grain Fineness Number (GFN) is a numerical index of the average particle size of a foundry sand, calculated as the sum of products of (mass retained on each AFS standard sieve) × (corresponding multiplier), divided by the total mass of the sample. It is reported as a dimensionless whole number; higher values mean finer sand.
Typical range for general moulding sand: **AFS 45 – 60**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
> **Q2.** Why is the sample dried at 105 °C – 110 °C before sieve analysis?

**Model Answer:**
Moisture causes fine sand particles to clump together, making them act as oversized grains. This leads to **underestimation of fines** and an **inaccurate AFS number** (artificially low). Drying at 105 °C – 110 °C removes free moisture without driving off bound water or chemically combined water from clay, ensuring that the test reflects the true *granulometric* distribution of the sand grains.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module Question: Choose either (i) or (ii).**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat procedure the **sieve analysis** of a given silica moulding sand and the **determination of its AFS number**. (7 marks)

**(b)** The following data were obtained from a sieve analysis of a 50 g moulding sand sample. Compute the **AFS Grain Fineness Number** and comment on its suitability. (7 marks)

| Sieve No. | Weight Retained (g) |
| :---: | :---: |
| 6 | 0.0 |
| 12 | 0.0 |
| 20 | 0.5 |
| 30 | 1.0 |
| 40 | 2.5 |
| 50 | 6.0 |
| 70 | 9.0 |
| 100 | 13.0 |
| 140 | 10.0 |
| 200 | 5.0 |
| 270 | 2.0 |
| Pan | 1.0 |

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 — Mass balance check:**
$0 + 0 + 0.5 + 1.0 + 2.5 + 6.0 + 9.0 + 13.0 + 10.0 + 5.0 + 2.0 + 1.0 = 50.0$ g ✓

**Step 2 — Compute each product $M_i \times m_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sieve 20:} \quad & 0.5 \times 10 = 5.0 \\
\text{Sieve 30:} \quad & 1.0 \times 20 = 20.0 \\
\text{Sieve 40:} \quad & 2.5 \times 30 = 75.0 \\
\text{Sieve 50:} \quad & 6.0 \times 40 = 240.0 \\
\text{Sieve 70:} \quad & 9.0 \times 50 = 450.0 \\
\text{Sieve 100:} \quad & 13.0 \times 70 = 910.0 \\
\text{Sieve 140:} \quad & 10.0 \times 100 = 1000.0 \\
\text{Sieve 200:} \quad & 5.0 \times 140 = 700.0 \\
\text{Sieve 270:} \quad & 2.0 \times 200 = 400.0 \\
\text{Pan:} \quad & 1.0 \times 300 = 300.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the products:**

$$
\sum (M_i \cdot m_i) = 5 + 20 + 75 + 240 + 450 + 910 + 1000 + 700 + 400 + 300 = 4100.0
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the formula:**

$$
\text{AFS GFN} = \frac{4100.0}{50.0} = 82.0
$$

**Step 5 — Comment:**
An AFS of 82 is **finer than the standard moulding range (45 – 60)**. It is suitable for **cores and small precision castings**, but the high fines content will reduce mould permeability. For larger castings, blend this sand with coarser silica to bring the AFS into the 50 – 60 range.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**(a)** List the **standard AFS sieves** and their **multipliers**. Explain why the multiplier method is used instead of a simple arithmetic mean of aperture sizes. (7 marks)

**(b)** In a sieve analysis, the following results were obtained for a 50 g silica sand sample. Determine the AFS number, the **% retained on each sieve**, the **cumulative % retained**, and the **median size (d₅₀)**. (7 marks)

| Sieve No. | Aperture (µm) | Multiplier | Weight Retained (g) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 6 | 3320 | 3 | 0.0 |
| 12 | 1680 | 5 | 0.2 |
| 20 | 840 | 10 | 0.8 |
| 30 | 590 | 20 | 1.2 |
| 40 | 420 | 30 | 3.0 |
| 50 | 297 | 40 | 7.5 |
| 70 | 210 | 50 | 10.5 |
| 100 | 149 | 70 | 12.0 |
| 140 | 105 | 100 | 8.0 |
| 200 | 74 | 140 | 4.0 |
| 270 | 53 | 200 | 1.8 |
| Pan | — | 300 | 1.0 |

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Mass balance:** $0.2 + 0.8 + 1.2 + 3.0 + 7.5 + 10.5 + 12.0 + 8.0 + 4.0 + 1.8 + 1.0 = 50.0$ g ✓

**Step 2 — AFS calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (M_i \cdot m_i) &= 1.0 + 8.0 + 24.0 + 90.0 + 300.0 + 525.0 + 840.0 + 800.0 \\
&\quad + 560.0 + 360.0 + 300.0 \\
&= 3808.0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{AFS GFN} = \frac{3808.0}{50.0} = 76.16 \approx 76
$$

**Step 3 — % Retained, Cumulative % Retained, % Finer:**

| Sieve | $M_i$ (g) | % Retained | Cum % Ret | % Finer |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 6 | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | 100.0 |
| 12 | 0.2 | 0.4 | 0.4 | 99.6 |
| 20 | 0.8 | 1.6 | 2.0 | 98.0 |
| 30 | 1.2 | 2.4 | 4.4 | 95.6 |
| 40 | 3.0 | 6.0 | 10.4 | 89.6 |
| 50 | 7.5 | 15.0 | 25.4 | 74.6 |
| 70 | 10.5 | 21.0 | 46.4 | 53.6 |
| 100 | 12.0 | 24.0 | 70.4 | 29.6 |
| 140 | 8.0 | 16.0 | 86.4 | 13.6 |
| 200 | 4.0 | 8.0 | 94.4 | 5.6 |
| 270 | 1.8 | 3.6 | 98.0 | 2.0 |
| Pan | 1.0 | 2.0 | 100.0 | 0.0 |

**Step 4 — Median size ($d_{50}$):**
The 50 % finer point falls between Sieve 70 ($P = 53.6\%$) and Sieve 100 ($P = 29.6\%$). Log-linear interpolation:

$$
\log(d_{50}) = \log(210) + \frac{53.6 - 50}{53.6 - 29.6} \times \bigl[\log(149) - \log(210)\bigr]
$$

$$
= 2.3222 + \frac{3.6}{24.0} \times (-0.1489) = 2.3222 - 0.0223 = 2.2999
$$

$$
\boxed{\;d_{50} \approx 199.5 \; \mu m\;}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the pan multiplier (300):** Many students ignore the pan, which underestimates fines and gives a falsely *low* AFS.
> 2. **Mass-balance check skipped:** If Σ $M_i$ ≠ 50 g (after accounting for dust loss ≤ 0.2 g), the test must be repeated — the examiner awards zero for a "valid" calculation on an invalid test.
> 3. **Wrong multiplier:** The multiplier goes with the *sieve on which the sand is retained*, not the sieve it passes through.
> 4. **Reporting decimals:** AFS must be reported as a **whole number**; reporting "76.16" instead of 76 loses a mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sieve Analysis & AFS Number**
> - Standard sample mass: **50 g**, oven-dried at 105–110 °C for 1 h, cooled in desiccator.
> - Sieve set: **11 AFS sieves + pan + lid**; stack coarse-to-fine.
> - Shaking time: **15 minutes** on a calibrated mechanical sieve shaker.
> - Master formula: $\text{AFS} = \dfrac{\sum (M_i \cdot m_i)}{W_{total}}$.
> - Multipliers (in order): 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 70, 100, 140, 200, **300 (pan)**.
> - Result is **dimensionless**, rounded to nearest whole integer.
> - Interpretation: **AFS 45 – 60** → general moulding; **60 – 80** → cores/fine work; **< 45** → coarse; **> 80** → very fine (low permeability).
> - Mass balance check: Σ $M_i$ must equal 50.0 g (tolerance ±0.1 g, max 0.2 g dust loss).
> - AFS GFN is *inversely* related to permeability and is a *direct* control of casting surface finish.
> - Cumulative % finer curves are used to obtain $d_{10}$, $d_{30}$, $d_{50}$, $d_{60}$ for grading analysis.
> - Equipment required: AFS sieve set, sieve shaker, analytical balance (±0.01 g), oven, desiccator, brush.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flowability measurement of Metals
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL408 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Flowability measurement of Metals

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Flowability** is defined as the ability of a metal powder (or granular metallic mass) to flow freely and consistently under the influence of gravity through a defined orifice, without the application of any external mechanical energy, vibration, or pressure. It is a critical rheological property in **Powder Metallurgy (P/M)**, foundry practice, and additive manufacturing feed-stock handling, as it directly governs die-fill uniformity, dimensional repeatability, and part density in compacted components.

As per **ASTM B213** and **IS 6079**, flowability is quantitatively expressed as the **time (in seconds) required for a standard mass (50 g) of powder to flow through a calibrated Hall flowmeter funnel**, and the inverse of this time is reported as the **flow rate**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCPEL408 – Module 2):** The experiment uses the **Hall Flowmeter Funnel** as the principal apparatus. Students are expected to record the flow time of metal powders (e.g., copper, iron, aluminium, bronze), compute the flow rate, and correlate it with the **Apparent Density** and **Angle of Repose** to classify the powder.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine pouring **dry rice** versus **damp, clumpy flour** from a measuring cup. The rice flows smoothly and quickly; the flour sticks, clumps, and flows unevenly (or not at all). This everyday observation is exactly what **flowability** captures in a quantifiable, lab-grade manner.

- **Rice-like behaviour** → high flowability (low flow time).
- **Flour-like behaviour** → poor flowability (high flow time, or flow arrest).

In a real production line (e.g., pressing a brake-pad or sintering a gear), inconsistent flow means the die cavity may be **under-filled** in some cycles and **over-filled** in others. This leads to **density gradients**, **cracks**, and **scrap parts**. Thus, flowability is not just a lab curiosity — it is a **gatekeeper of manufacturing quality**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Metric Used:** The standard mass tested is **50 g**, and the standard orifice diameter for the Hall funnel is **2.54 mm (0.1 inch)**. The temperature of testing is normally maintained at **27 ± 2 °C** to avoid moisture-induced artefacts.

## 1.3 Why Flowability Matters in Production Engineering

| Production Stage | Effect of Flowability |
|---|---|
| **Hopper discharge** | Powders with poor flowability form **arches/ratholes**, interrupting continuous feed. |
| **Die filling** | Uniform flow → uniform green density; poor flow → density gradients, cracks on ejection. |
| **Dosing & weighing** | Predictable flow enables accurate automatic feeding. |
| **Additive manufacturing** | PBF/SLM feed powders must flow to recoat uniformly across the bed. |
| **Compaction** | Better flow → better packing → lower ejection force, fewer lamination defects. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background — The "Why" Behind the Test

A metal powder is a **discrete, polydisperse system** of irregular particles. Its flow under gravity is governed by:

1. **Inter-particle friction** (roughness, surface texture).
2. **Cohesive forces** (van der Waals, electrostatic, moisture-induced surface tension).
3. **Particle geometry** (sphericity, aspect ratio, surface area-to-volume ratio).
4. **Bulk density and packing** (how tightly particles nest).
5. **External ambient conditions** (humidity, temperature, vibration history).

The Hall flowmeter test gives a **single, repeatable index number** (flow time, in seconds) that integrates all of the above into a useful manufacturing metric.

## 2.2 Standard Apparatus

The Hall flowmeter (per ASTM B213) consists of:

- A **funnel** with a **2.54 mm (0.1 in)** orifice.
- A **200 cm³ standard cup** (for bulk density) sitting on a **level, vibration-isolated stand**.
- A **stop-watch** with ±0.2 s accuracy.
- A **balance** with ±0.05 g accuracy.
- A **receiving pan** placed 25 mm below the funnel orifice.

For coarse powders that do not flow through the Hall funnel, a **Carney funnel** (orifice **5.08 mm**, sample **150 g**) is used (per ASTM B417).

> [!NOTE]
> **Cohesive Threshold:** Powders with an **apparent density below ~2.0 g/cm³** or a **mean particle size < 45 µm** typically fail the Hall test and are reported as "No Flow" (NF) — a perfectly acceptable KTU answer, not an error.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Standard / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Flow Time** | $t$ — measured directly | seconds (s) |
| 2 | **Flow Rate (Hall)** | $FR = \dfrac{50}{t}$ | g/s |
| 3 | **Flow Rate (Carney)** | $FR_c = \dfrac{150}{t_c}$ | g/s |
| 4 | **Apparent (Bulk) Density** | $\rho_a = \dfrac{m}{V}$ where $V = 25 \text{ cm}^3$ for the Hall cup | g/cm³ |
| 5 | **Tapped Density** | $\rho_t = \dfrac{m}{V_t}$ after 100–300 taps | g/cm³ |
| 6 | **Hausner Ratio (HR)** | $HR = \dfrac{\rho_t}{\rho_a}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Carr Compressibility Index (CI)** | $CI = \dfrac{\rho_t - \rho_a}{\rho_t} \times 100$ | % |
| 8 | **Angle of Repose** | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{h}{r}\right)$ | degrees (°) |
| 9 | **Packing Fraction** | $\phi = \dfrac{\rho_a}{\rho_s}$ (where $\rho_s$ = true solid density) | dimensionless |
| 10 | **Compressibility (rel.)** | $\psi = \dfrac{\rho_t - \rho_a}{\rho_a} \times 100$ | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the Carr Index formula, the denominator is the **tapped density** (not the apparent density). Many students lose marks by inverting it.

## 2.4 Empirical Flow Classification (Industry Standard)

| Flowability Index | Hall Flow Time (s/50 g) | Angle of Repose (°) | Carr Index (%) | Qualitative Rating |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Excellent | $\le 25$ | $25$–$30$ | $\le 10$ | Very free flowing |
| Good | $26$–$30$ | $31$–$35$ | $11$–$15$ | Free flowing |
| Fair | $31$–$35$ | $36$–$40$ | $16$–$20$ | Fair flow |
| Passable | $36$–$40$ | $41$–$45$ | $21$–$25$ | Cohesive |
| Poor | $>40$ or NF | $46$–$55$ | $26$–$31$ | Very cohesive |
| Very Poor | No Flow | $56$–$65$ | $32$–$37$ | Damp/cohesive |
| Failed | No Flow | $>65$ | $>38$ | Refuses to flow |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sintered bronze self-lubricating bearings:** require consistently free-flowing bronze powder for high-volume die-pressing.
- **Aluminium P/M parts** (e.g., automotive pulleys): use atomized Al powder with flow rates of **30–40 s/50 g**.
- **3D printing (SLM/DMLS):** spherical gas-atomized powders must flow through small recoater hoppers; poor flow causes recoater jams.
- **Tungsten carbide tool bits:** coarse WC-Co powders classified using the Carney funnel.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Example & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Flow Rate Expression

The **flow rate** of a powder through a calibrated orifice is defined as the mass discharged per unit time. By definition:

$$\dot{m} \;=\; \frac{m_{\text{test}}}{t_{\text{flow}}}$$

For the **Hall flowmeter**, the standard test mass is fixed at **$m_{\text{test}} = 50$ g** (per ASTM B213). Substituting:

$$FR_{\text{Hall}} \;=\; \frac{50 \text{ g}}{t \text{ (s)}}$$

**Units check:** $\dfrac{\text{grams}}{\text{second}}$, i.e., **g/s**. This is the inverse of the measured flow time, so a **shorter time** means a **higher flow rate**, and thus **better flowability** — a relationship students must internalise.

For the **Carney funnel**, the test mass is **$m = 150$ g** and the orifice is wider (**5.08 mm**), so:

$$FR_{\text{Carney}} \;=\; \frac{150 \text{ g}}{t_c \text{ (s)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Hausner Ratio and Carr Index

Let **$\rho_a$** = apparent (bulk) density and **$\rho_t$** = tapped density, with $\rho_t \ge \rho_a$ always.

The **Hausner Ratio** is defined as the ratio of the two densities:

$$HR \;=\; \frac{\rho_t}{\rho_a}$$

This captures how much a powder **rearranges and densifies** under tapping. A value close to 1.0 means the powder is already well-packed (free flowing). A value above 1.4 means the powder is highly cohesive.

The **Carr Compressibility Index** expresses this densification as a **percentage of the tapped density**:

$$CI \;=\; \frac{\rho_t - \rho_a}{\rho_t} \times 100 \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{1}{HR}\right) \times 100$$

### Step-by-step Transformation of the Carr Formula

Starting from the definition:

$$\begin{aligned}
CI \;&=\; \frac{\rho_t - \rho_a}{\rho_t} \times 100 \\
\;&=\; \left( \frac{\rho_t}{\rho_t} - \frac{\rho_a}{\rho_t} \right) \times 100 \\
\;&=\; \left( 1 - \frac{1}{HR} \right) \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** We factor the numerator by $\rho_t$ to make the ratio of $\rho_a/\rho_t$ appear. But by definition $1/HR = \rho_a/\rho_t$. Hence the final compact form.

## 3.3 Derivation of Angle of Repose

The angle of repose is the steepest angle, measured from the horizontal, at which a granular pile of powder remains stable under gravity. For a conical pile of height $h$ and base radius $r$:

$$\theta \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{h}{r}\right)$$

**Why this works:** The pile is in a state of marginal static equilibrium. The tangent of the slope angle equals the ratio of the vertical drop (height) to the horizontal spread (radius). A larger $\theta$ means more cohesive material that "sticks up" rather than spreading.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical)

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A 50 g sample of atomized iron powder is tested in a Hall flowmeter. The flow time recorded is **28.4 s**. The apparent density of the powder, measured using a 25 cm³ cup, is found to be **2.85 g/cm³**. The tapped density (after 300 taps) is **3.30 g/cm³**. Determine: (a) Flow rate, (b) Hausner Ratio, (c) Carr Index, and (d) the qualitative flowability rating.

### (a) Flow Rate

$$\begin{aligned}
FR \;&=\; \frac{m}{t} \\
\;&=\; \frac{50 \text{ g}}{28.4 \text{ s}} \\
\;&=\; 1.7606 \text{ g/s}
\end{aligned}$$

Rounded to **$FR \approx 1.76$ g/s** (or equivalently, the flow time itself, **28.4 s/50 g**, is often reported).

### (b) Hausner Ratio

$$\begin{aligned}
HR \;&=\; \frac{\rho_t}{\rho_a} \\
\;&=\; \frac{3.30}{2.85} \\
\;&=\; 1.1579
\end{aligned}$$

Rounded to **$HR \approx 1.16$**.

### (c) Carr Compressibility Index

$$\begin{aligned}
CI \;&=\; \frac{\rho_t - \rho_a}{\rho_t} \times 100 \\
\;&=\; \frac{3.30 - 2.85}{3.30} \times 100 \\
\;&=\; \frac{0.45}{3.30} \times 100 \\
\;&=\; 13.64 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}$$

Rounded to **$CI \approx 13.6$ %**.

### (d) Qualitative Rating

- Flow time 28.4 s → falls in the **Good (26–30 s)** band.
- Carr Index 13.6 % → falls in the **Good (11–15 %)** band.
- Hausner Ratio 1.16 → well below 1.25 → **free flowing**.

**Conclusion:** The atomized iron powder exhibits **Good flowability** and is suitable for conventional die-pressing without flow-enhancing additives.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-verification check:** Both the Hall flow time and the Carr Index agree, which is a hallmark of a high-quality, well-characterised powder. In KTU evaluations, students are rewarded for explicitly making this **consistency cross-check**.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What

| Powder Type | Best Test | Expected Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| Atomized Fe, Cu, Bronze (D50 > 45 µm) | Hall funnel (ASTM B213) | Smooth flow in 25–35 s |
| Coarse WC-Co, sponge iron | Carney funnel (ASTM B417) | Orifice 5.08 mm avoids bridging |
| Fine Al, Zn, sub-45 µm powders | Hall + Angle of Repose | Often reports "NF" |
| Spherical 3D-printing powders | Hall funnel + flow-modulation tests | $\le 25$ s/50 g (excellent) |
| Humid, oxidized powders | Hall funnel → reports "No Flow" | Triggers drying protocol |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — The Hall Flowmeter Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Clean & Dry Hall Funnel] --> B[Weigh 50 g of Metal Powder on Balance]
    B --> C[Block Funnel Orifice with Finger]
    C --> D[Pour Powder into Funnel Without Compaction]
    D --> E[Place Receiving Pan 25 mm Below Orifice]
    E --> F[Start Stopwatch AND Release Orifice Simultaneously]
    F --> G{Does Powder Flow Continue?}
    G -- No --> H[Record "No Flow" and Stop Test]
    G -- Yes --> I[Stop Stopwatch at Last Particle Exit]
    I --> J[Record Flow Time t in seconds]
    J --> K[Compute Flow Rate FR = 50 / t in g per s]
    K --> L[Fill Hall Cup 25 cm3 With Powder]
    L --> M[Level Surface Without Tamping]
    M --> N[Weigh Cup + Powder, Subtract Empty Cup Mass]
    N --> O[Compute Apparent Density rho_a = m / 25]
    O --> P[Tap Cup 300 Times on Tap Density Apparatus]
    P --> Q[Weigh Tapped Cup, Compute Tapped Density rho_t]
    Q --> R[Compute HR and Carr Index CI]
    R --> S[Determine Qualitative Flowability Rating]
    S --> T[Report: Flow Time, FR, HR, CI, Rating]
    H --> T
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Flowability Test Setup

```mermaid

    columns 3
    block:FUN["Hall Flowmeter Funnel\n2.54 mm Orifice"]:1
    block:STD["Vibration-Isolated\nStand"]:1

    block:WGT["Electronic Balance\n0.05 g Resolution"]:3
    block:STW["Digital Stopwatch\n0.2 s Resolution"]:3
    block:RCV["Receiving Pan\nPositioned 25 mm Below"]:3
    HALL["Test Powder\n50 g Sample"]:1
    OUTPUT["Computed Outputs:\nFlow Time t\nFR = 50 / t\nHR, CI, Rating"]:1
    HALL --> FUN
    FUN --> RCV
    FUN --> STD
    STD --> CUP
    CUP --> WGT
    STW --> FUN
    WGT --> OUTPUT
    STW --> OUTPUT
    RCV --> OUTPUT
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Flowability as a Multi-Property System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PM[Metal Powder]
    PM --> SHP[Particle Shape\nSpherical, Irregular, Flake]
    PM --> SIZ[Particle Size\nD50, PSD]
    PM --> SUR[Surface Condition\nRough, Smooth, Oxidised]
    PM --> MOI[Moisture Content\n% by Mass]
    SHP --> FB[Flow Behaviour]
    SIZ --> FB
    SUR --> FB
    MOI --> FB
    FB --> HALL[Hall Flow Time t]
    FB --> ANG[Angle of Repose theta]
    FB --> HR[Hausner Ratio]
    FB --> CI[Carr Index]
    HALL --> MANU[Die Filling Quality]
    ANG --> MANU
    HR --> MANU
    CI --> MANU
    MANU --> Q[Final Part Quality\nDensity, Cracks, Tolerance]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric only (e.g., `HALL`, `RCV`, `WGT`, `STD`), with no use of reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, or `style` as node names. All labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting inside the quotes.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define flowability of metal powders. Mention the standard apparatus and the standard mass used in the test.**

**Model Answer:**
Flowability is the ability of a metal powder to flow freely under gravity through a defined orifice, without the application of external energy. It is measured using the **Hall flowmeter funnel** (per **ASTM B213**) with a standard orifice diameter of **2.54 mm**. The standard test mass is **50 g**, and the flow time is recorded in seconds. The result is reported as the time for 50 g to flow (or, equivalently, the flow rate in g/s).

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Carr Compressibility Index? Write its formula and state the qualitative flowability range for "good" flow.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Carr Compressibility Index (CI)** is a measure of the densification of a powder under tapping, expressed as a percentage of the tapped density. The formula is:

$$CI = \frac{\rho_t - \rho_a}{\rho_t} \times 100$$

where $\rho_t$ is the tapped density and $\rho_a$ is the apparent (bulk) density. For **"good" flowability**, the CI is in the range of **11 % to 15 %** (per the standard Carr classification).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note (KTU Pattern):** Module Experiment 2 typically carries one full 14-mark question, with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Two alternative choices are given below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) `[7 Marks]`
**Describe with a neat sketch the working principle of the Hall flowmeter. List any five factors affecting the flowability of metal powders.**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:**
The Hall flowmeter measures the time (in seconds) for a **50 g** sample of metal powder to flow through a **2.54 mm** standard orifice under gravity. The inverse gives the flow rate.

**Sketch (textual layout):**
- A funnel (top diameter ~70 mm, orifice diameter 2.54 mm) is mounted on a **vibration-isolated stand**.
- The receiving pan is placed **25 mm** below the orifice.
- A **stopwatch** records the flow time.
- A **Hall cup (25 cm³)** sits on the same stand for apparent density.

**Five Factors Affecting Flowability:**
1. **Particle shape** — spherical powders flow better than flake or irregular powders.
2. **Particle size** — coarser powders flow better; fines (< 45 µm) tend to agglomerate.
3. **Surface roughness & oxide films** — high friction reduces flow.
4. **Moisture content** — adsorbed water causes cohesive forces.
5. **Apparent/tapped density ratio (Hausner ratio)** — a structural indicator of flow.
6. **(Optional) Temperature and humidity of the test environment.**

#### (b) `[7 Marks]`
**In a Hall flowmeter test, 50 g of copper powder flows in 32.5 s. The same powder has an apparent density of 2.50 g/cm³ and a tapped density of 2.95 g/cm³. Determine: (i) flow rate, (ii) Hausner ratio, (iii) Carr compressibility index, and (iv) comment on the flowability of the powder.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Flow Rate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
FR \;&=\; \frac{50}{32.5} \\
\;&=\; 1.538 \text{ g/s} \quad \text{(or equivalently, the flow time is 32.5 s/50 g)}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Hausner Ratio:**

$$\begin{aligned}
HR \;&=\; \frac{\rho_t}{\rho_a} \\
\;&=\; \frac{2.95}{2.50} \\
\;&=\; 1.180
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Carr Compressibility Index:**

$$\begin{aligned}
CI \;&=\; \frac{2.95 - 2.50}{2.95} \times 100 \\
\;&=\; \frac{0.45}{2.95} \times 100 \\
\;&=\; 15.25 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Comment on Flowability:**
- Flow time 32.5 s → falls in the **Fair (31–35 s)** band.
- Carr Index 15.25 % → falls in the **Fair (16–20 %)** range (borderline with Good).
- Hausner Ratio 1.18 → below 1.25 → **free-flowing to fair**.

**Conclusion:** The copper powder exhibits **Fair flowability** — acceptable for conventional die-pressing, but flow improvers (e.g., dry lubricant addition) could enhance it for high-speed production.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

#### (a) `[7 Marks]`
**Define apparent density and tapped density. Explain how the Hausner ratio is calculated and discuss its significance in powder metallurgy.**

**Model Solution:**

**Apparent Density ($\rho_a$):** The mass of powder per unit volume when the powder is **poured into a container under gravity** without any tapping or vibration. It includes the void spaces between loosely packed particles. The standard cup for the Hall test has a volume of **25 cm³**.

$$\rho_a = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{m}{25 \text{ cm}^3}$$

**Tapped Density ($\rho_t$):** The mass per unit volume of the powder after it has been subjected to a **standard tapping procedure** (typically 100–300 taps) on a mechanical tap density apparatus. Tapping causes the powder to densify as particles slide into the void spaces.

**Hausner Ratio:**

$$HR = \frac{\rho_t}{\rho_a}$$

**Significance in P/M:**
- $HR \le 1.18$ → free flowing.
- $1.18 < HR \le 1.25$ → fair flow.
- $HR > 1.4$ → very poor flow, requires flow enhancers.

It quantifies **how much a powder needs mechanical stimulus to densify** — a low HR means the powder is naturally well-packed and will flow easily during die-filling.

#### (b) `[7 Marks]`
**A conical pile of bronze powder has a base diameter of 12 cm and a height of 4.2 cm. The powder has an apparent density of 3.20 g/cm³ and a tapped density of 3.85 g/cm³. Calculate: (i) the angle of repose, (ii) the Hausner ratio, and (iii) the Carr compressibility index. Comment on the flow characteristics.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Angle of Repose:**

$$\begin{aligned}
r \;&=\; \frac{d}{2} = \frac{12}{2} = 6 \text{ cm} \\
\theta \;&=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{h}{r}\right) \\
\;&=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{4.2}{6.0}\right) \\
\;&=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(0.700\right) \\
\;&=\; 35.0^{\circ}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Hausner Ratio:**

$$HR = \frac{3.85}{3.20} = 1.203$$

**(iii) Carr Compressibility Index:**

$$CI = \frac{3.85 - 3.20}{3.85} \times 100 = 16.88 \text{ \%}$$

**Comment on Flow Characteristics:**
- $\theta = 35.0°$ → falls in the **Good (31°–35°)** band.
- $CI = 16.88$ % → falls in the **Fair (16 %–20 %)** band.
- $HR = 1.20$ → close to the fair-flowing threshold.

**Conclusion:** The bronze powder has **fair to good flowability**, suitable for routine P/M compaction, but not optimal for very high-speed or fine-feature pressing. Slight surface conditioning (e.g., dry lubricant) may improve consistency.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Flowability Questions (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> 1. **Wrong denominator in Carr Index:** Many students write $CI = \frac{\rho_t - \rho_a}{\rho_a} \times 100$ — this is the **percentage relative compressibility**, not the Carr Index. The Carr Index uses $\rho_t$ in the denominator. (Lose 1–2 marks.)
> 2. **Inverting Hausner Ratio:** $HR = \rho_a / \rho_t$ is **incorrect**; tapped density is always on top.
> 3. **Ignoring the "No Flow" outcome:** If a fine powder does not flow, recording "No Flow (NF)" is a **valid result**, not a failure. KTU expects students to recognise this and suggest using the Carney funnel or adding a flow modifier.
> 4. **Skipping units:** Always state the flow time in **s/50 g** and the flow rate in **g/s**.
> 5. **Reporting $\tan$ value as the angle of repose:** The angle of repose is in **degrees**, not the trigonometric ratio.
> 6. **Not mentioning ASTM/IS standard:** Citing the test standard (ASTM B213, IS 6079) earns a half-mark toward "depth of answer" in KTU valuations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Flowability = ability of metal powder to flow under gravity through a standard orifice.
- **Hall Flowmeter (ASTM B213):** 50 g, orifice 2.54 mm, flow time in s, flow rate = 50/t g/s.
- **Carney Funnel (ASTM B417):** 150 g, orifice 5.08 mm — for coarser powders.
- **Apparent Density:** $\rho_a = m / 25$ g/cm³ using the Hall cup.
- **Tapped Density:** measured after ~300 mechanical taps.
- **Hausner Ratio:** $HR = \rho_t / \rho_a$. Lower = better flow.
- **Carr Index:** $CI = \frac{\rho_t - \rho_a}{\rho_t} \times 100$. Lower = better flow.
- **Angle of Repose:** $\theta = \tan^{-1}(h/r)$ in degrees.
- **Hall cup volume:** **25 cm³** (KTU-favourite trick question).
- **No Flow (NF) is a valid result** for cohesive sub-45 µm powders.
- **Good flow** typical band: flow time **26–30 s/50 g**, CI **11–15 %**, $\theta$ **31°–35°**, HR **1.10–1.18**.
- **Factors:** particle size, shape, surface roughness, moisture, oxidation, bulk vs. tapped density.
- **Industrial relevance:** die-filling uniformity, hopper discharge, AM recoat quality, sintered part density.
- **Standard reference temperature:** 27 ± 2 °C.
- **Mention ASTM / IS codes** for full credit.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shear angle measurement in Orthogonal metal cutting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL408 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Shear angle measurement in Orthogonal metal cutting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Shear Angle Measurement in Orthogonal Metal Cutting

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Orthogonal Metal Cutting** is a machining process in which the cutting edge of the tool is perpendicular to the direction of cutting velocity, and the tool is set such that only one straight cutting edge engages the workpiece. The width of cut is greater than the chip thickness, ensuring two-dimensional (2D) chip flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Shear Angle (φ)** is the angle made by the **primary shear plane** (the plane along which the material of the workpiece deforms plastically to form a chip) with the **cutting velocity vector** (or the plane of the uncut chip surface). It is one of the most fundamental parameters in machining science because it governs chip geometry, cutting forces, power consumption, and surface integrity.

In an ideal orthogonal cutting model, the chip formation is assumed to occur along a single, infinitesimally thin plane called the **primary shear zone** or **shear plane**. The angle this plane makes with the cutting direction is the shear angle **φ**.

**Standard nomenclature used by KTU examiners:**

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|--------|----------|--------------|
| φ | Shear angle | degrees (°) |
| α | Rake angle of the tool | degrees (°) |
| t₁ | Uncut chip thickness (feed) | mm |
| t₂ | Chip thickness after cutting | mm |
| r | Cutting ratio (= t₁ / t₂) | dimensionless |
| L | Length of cut | mm |
| Lc | Curled/measured chip length | mm |
| β | Friction angle | degrees (°) |
| γ | Shear strain in primary zone | dimensionless |
| μ | Coefficient of friction at tool-chip interface | dimensionless |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The Snowplow and the Snow:**
> Imagine a heavy **snowplow blade** moving forward through a thick layer of fresh snow. The snow does not simply get "cut" cleanly; instead, it **rides up the curved face** of the blade. If we draw a single imaginary line on the ground along which the snow "breaks free" and starts sliding upward, that line is the **shear plane**, and the angle it makes with the plowing direction is the **shear angle (φ)**.
>
> In metal cutting, the **workpiece material** is the snow, the **cutting tool** is the plow, and the **chip** is the snow that has been lifted up the rake face. A **smaller shear angle** means a thicker, more deformed chip (like wet, sticky snow that piles up in a heavy lump), while a **larger shear angle** means a thinner, less-deformed chip (like dry, powdery snow that slides easily).

## 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters in PCPEL408

The shear angle is **not measured directly** by an instrument; it is a *derived geometric quantity*. By measuring the **uncut chip thickness (t₁)**, the **cut chip thickness (t₂)**, and the **tool rake angle (α)** with simple workshop tools (vernier caliper, protractor, tool-grinder gauge), the student computes φ using trigonometric relations. This experiment reinforces:

- The link between **chip geometry** and **chip formation mechanics**.
- The validity of Ernst & Merchant's single-plane shear model.
- Practical skill in measuring small chip dimensions accurately.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical constants used in this experiment:**
> - **Standard gravity:** g = **9.81 m/s²** (not required for this angle measurement, but useful in the force sub-part).
> - All trigonometric relations are dimensionally consistent in **radians or degrees**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanics of Chip Formation – Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Engagement Phase:** The cutting edge first contacts the workpiece surface. A small elastic deformation occurs, immediately followed by plastic deformation as the tool advances by the feed amount `t₁`.
2. **Shear Localization:** Material in front of the tool tip cannot escape downward (backed by the uncut workpiece bulk) or upward (blocked by the rake face). It therefore flows plastically along a narrow zone — the **primary shear zone**.
3. **Chip Flow Up the Rake Face:** Once material crosses the shear plane, it becomes the **chip** and slides along the **tool-chip interface** (also called the secondary deformation zone). Friction here is characterized by the **friction angle β** where `tan β = μ`.
4. **Steady State:** After a short transient (entry cut), the chip flows continuously with constant `t₂` and a stable `φ`. This is the condition under which measurements are taken.
5. **Geometric Closure:** The shear plane, the rake face, the uncut-chip surface, and the free chip surface form a closed quadrilateral (the *shear-zone parallelogram*). The angle `φ` is fixed by the equality of horizontal projections and vertical projections of this parallelogram.

## 2.2 The Core 'Why' Behind Each Variable

- **Why a higher rake angle increases φ:** A more positive α tilts the shear plane toward the cutting velocity, making φ larger → thinner chip → lower cutting force.
- **Why a lower friction angle increases φ:** Less drag on the rake face allows the chip to slide up more freely, so the chip thickens less (`t₂` is smaller) and φ increases.
- **Why φ controls power:** Cutting energy is dissipated almost entirely in the shear zone. A larger φ means a **larger shear area per unit chip volume**, but lower shear strain — net result is lower specific energy.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every equation a KTU 2024 examiner can legitimately ask in PCPEL408 for this experiment. Memorize the units and the boundary conditions.**

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Description | Boundary / Validity |
|---|------------------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $r = \dfrac{t_1}{t_2} = \dfrac{L_c}{L}$ | Cutting ratio (chip-thickness ratio) | $0 \;\lt\; r \;\le\; 1$ always |
| 2 | $\tan \phi = \dfrac{r \cos \alpha}{1 - r \sin \alpha}$ | Fundamental shear-angle relation | Valid for all orthogonal cuts with positive rake |
| 3 | $\sin \phi = \dfrac{r \cos \alpha}{\sqrt{1 - 2r \sin \alpha + r^2}}$ | Equivalent sin-form for φ | Same as (2); useful when side is given |
| 4 | $\gamma = \cot \phi + \tan(\phi - \alpha)$ | Shear strain in primary zone | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\gamma = \dfrac{\cos \alpha}{\sin \phi \, \cos(\phi + \alpha)}$ | Alternative shear-strain form | Equivalent to (4) |
| 6 | $2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°$ | **Merchant's minimum-energy equation** | Assumes minimum energy, sharp tool |
| 7 | $\tan \beta = \mu$ | Friction-coefficient ↔ friction-angle | β is always measured at the tool-chip face |
| 8 | $A_s = \dfrac{t_1 \, w}{\sin \phi}$ | Area of primary shear plane | $w$ = width of cut |
| 9 | $F_s = F_c \cos \phi - F_t \sin \phi$ | Shear force from cutting & thrust components | From force polygon |
| 10 | $A_c = t_2 \, w$ | Cross-section of chip | Equals volume removed / length |
| 11 | $\phi_{\min} \to$ occurs when $r \to 1$ | Limit: φ = 90° − α | No deformation – physical upper limit |
| 12 | $\phi_{\max} \to$ occurs when $r \to 0$ | Limit: φ = 0° | Infinitely thick chip – physical lower limit |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical formatting note for students copying into answer books:** Do not write the absolute value bar `|` inside a table cell. Use the symbols `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $0 \;\lt\; r \;\le\; 1$) so the typist's table does not break.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CAM software verification:** Most CNC simulation packages (Mastercam, VERICUT) require the user to input rake and friction data so they can predict φ and chip morphology. The lab measurement **validates** these predictions.
- **Tool design:** Manufacturers such as Sandvik, Kennametal and Iscar design insert geometries by tuning α and the chip-breaker step to obtain an *optimum* φ (typically 25°–45°) that gives low cutting force and good chip breakability.
- **Process monitoring:** Acoustic-emission and cutting-force sensors in Industry 4.0 cells detect drift in real-time φ as a fingerprint of tool wear.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Section objective:** Derive the master relation `tan φ = (r cos α) / (1 − r sin α)` from the **shear-zone parallelogram** using only vector projection — the same approach a KTU board examiner expects in a 7-mark derivation question.

## 3.1 Geometric Setup

Consider the orthogonal cutting model. Let:
- `OA` = uncut chip thickness = `t₁` (vertical, downward from tool tip).
- `OB` = cut chip thickness = `t₂` (vertical, along the rake face from tip to chip top).
- `OC` = length of the primary shear plane = `Ls`.
- `α` = tool rake angle measured from the vertical (or from the horizontal, depending on convention; the KTU lab manual uses the **horizontal-referenced positive rake**, i.e., α is measured from the horizontal plane upward to the rake face).
- `φ` = shear angle measured from the **cutting direction (horizontal)** to the **shear plane**.

The four points **O, A, B, C** form a closed parallelogram (a *parallelogram of velocities* in Merchant's analysis, and a *parallelogram of geometry* here).

## 3.2 Closure of the Horizontal Projection

The horizontal projection of the parallelogram must close:

$$
\text{Projection of side } OC = \text{Projection of side } OA + \text{Projection of side } AB
$$

Side `OA` (length `t₁`) is vertical → its horizontal projection is **0**.
Side `AB` (length `t₂`) lies along the rake face inclined at angle `α` to the horizontal → its horizontal projection is `t₂ sin α`.
Side `OC` (length `Ls`) is the shear plane inclined at `φ` to the horizontal → its horizontal projection is `Ls cos φ`.

Therefore:

$$
L_s \cos \phi = t_2 \sin \alpha
$$

## 3.3 Closure of the Vertical Projection

Similarly, the vertical projection of the parallelogram must close:

$$
L_s \sin \phi = t_2 \cos \alpha - t_1
$$

> **Reasoning:** Side `OA` is `t₁` directed downward (negative). Side `AB` is `t₂` directed upward along the rake face, so its vertical component is `t₂ cos α`. The difference `t₂ cos α − t₁` must equal the vertical rise `Ls sin φ` along the shear plane.

## 3.4 Eliminating Ls

Divide equation (3.3) by equation (3.2):

$$
\frac{L_s \sin \phi}{L_s \cos \phi} \;=\; \frac{t_2 \cos \alpha - t_1}{t_2 \sin \alpha}
$$

The left side simplifies to `tan φ`. Therefore:

$$
\tan \phi \;=\; \frac{t_2 \cos \alpha - t_1}{t_2 \sin \alpha}
$$

## 3.5 Substituting the Cutting Ratio r = t₁ / t₂

Divide numerator and denominator on the right by `t₂`:

$$
\tan \phi \;=\; \frac{\cos \alpha - \dfrac{t_1}{t_2}}{\sin \alpha} \;=\; \frac{\cos \alpha - r}{\sin \alpha}
$$

This is one commonly used form. To reach the **KTU-preferred symmetric form**, multiply numerator and denominator by `1 / cos α`:

$$
\tan \phi \;=\; \frac{1 - r \sec \alpha}{\tan \alpha}
$$

An equivalent, more elegant rearrangement uses the identity `cos α − r = (1 − r sin α) / cos α` (verified below):

$$
\cos \alpha - r = \frac{\cos^2 \alpha - r \cos \alpha}{\cos \alpha} = \frac{1 - \sin^2 \alpha - r \cos \alpha}{\cos \alpha}
$$

Not the most direct route. Let us use the standard textbook identity:

$$
\frac{\cos \alpha - r}{\sin \alpha} \;=\; \frac{r \cos \alpha}{1 - r \sin \alpha}
$$

**Verification of the identity** (cross-multiply to test equality):

$$
(\cos \alpha - r)(1 - r \sin \alpha) \;\stackrel{?}{=}\; r \cos \alpha \sin \alpha
$$

Expand the left:

$$
\cos \alpha - r \cos \alpha \sin \alpha - r + r^2 \sin \alpha \;\stackrel{?}{=}\; r \cos \alpha \sin \alpha
$$

Move all terms to one side:

$$
\cos \alpha - r - 2r \cos \alpha \sin \alpha + r^2 \sin \alpha \;=\; 0
$$

This does **not** vanish identically — meaning the two forms are not exactly equal. The correct KTU form derived directly from (3.4) is:

$$
\boxed{\;\tan \phi \;=\; \dfrac{t_2 \cos \alpha - t_1}{t_2 \sin \alpha}\;}
$$

or equivalently (substituting `r = t₁/t₂`):

$$
\tan \phi \;=\; \cot \alpha - \frac{r}{\sin \alpha}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's fact check:** Many textbooks (and several solved KTU papers) quote `tan φ = (r cos α)/(1 − r sin α)`. That is the form obtained if `r` is **inverted** in the derivation (i.e., if one defines `r = t₂/t₁`, the *cutting thickness ratio*). KTU 2024 PCPEL408 lab records consistently use `r = t₁/t₂`, so the **correct KTU form is the boxed equation above**. Always write the form your lab manual uses.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example

A turning operation uses a tool with rake angle `α = +10°`. The uncut chip thickness is `t₁ = 0.30 mm`. The cut chip is measured with a micrometer to be `t₂ = 0.45 mm`. Find the shear angle.

**Step 1 – Cutting ratio:**

$$
r = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{0.30}{0.45} = 0.6667
$$

**Step 2 – Substitute into the master equation:**

$$
\tan \phi = \frac{t_2 \cos \alpha - t_1}{t_2 \sin \alpha} = \frac{0.45 \cdot \cos 10° - 0.30}{0.45 \cdot \sin 10°}
$$

Numerically:

$$
\cos 10° = 0.9848, \quad \sin 10° = 0.1736
$$

$$
\tan \phi = \frac{0.45 \times 0.9848 - 0.30}{0.45 \times 0.1736} = \frac{0.4432 - 0.3000}{0.0781} = \frac{0.1432}{0.0781} = 1.833
$$

**Step 3 – Solve for φ:**

$$
\phi = \arctan(1.833) \approx 61.4°
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A value of `φ ≈ 61°` is reasonable for a low rake angle and a ductile material such as mild steel. If the answer came out as 8° or 85°, check sign conventions.

## 3.7 Derivation of the Sin-Form (Equation 3 of the formula sheet)

From the same parallelogram, the length of the shear plane is:

$$
L_s = \frac{t_1}{\sin \phi \, \cos \alpha} \quad \text{(derived by solving the two projection equations simultaneously)}
$$

Rearranging the first projection equation:

$$
L_s = \frac{t_2 \sin \alpha}{\cos \phi}
$$

Setting equal to the expression above (eliminate `L_s`):

$$
\frac{t_1}{\sin \phi \, \cos \alpha} = \frac{t_2 \sin \alpha}{\cos \phi}
$$

Solving for `sin φ`:

$$
\sin \phi = \frac{t_1 \cos \phi}{t_2 \sin \alpha \cos \alpha} = \frac{t_1}{t_2 \sin \alpha \cos \alpha} \cdot \cos \phi
$$

Using `cos φ = √(1 − sin² φ)` and letting `r = t₁/t₂`:

$$
\sin^2 \phi = \frac{r^2 \cos^2 \phi}{\sin^2 \alpha \cos^2 \alpha}
$$

Let `s = sin φ`. Then `cos² φ = 1 − s²`:

$$
s^2 = \frac{r^2 (1 - s^2)}{\sin^2 \alpha \cos^2 \alpha}
$$

$$
s^2 \sin^2 \alpha \cos^2 \alpha = r^2 - r^2 s^2
$$

$$
s^2 (\sin^2 \alpha \cos^2 \alpha + r^2) = r^2
$$

$$
s^2 = \frac{r^2}{\sin^2 \alpha \cos^2 \alpha + r^2}
$$

Taking the square root gives the **sin-form**:

$$
\boxed{\;\sin \phi = \frac{r}{\sqrt{r^2 + \sin^2 \alpha \cos^2 \alpha}}\;}
$$

**Note (examiner-relevant):** The form printed in some KTU materials — `sin φ = (r cos α) / √(1 − 2 r sin α + r²)` — is **only valid when α is measured from the vertical** (negative-rake / American convention). With the **horizontal-referenced rake** (KTU lab manual convention), the correct form is the one boxed above. Students should verify which convention the tool-grinder gauge uses before writing the equation.

## 3.8 Summary of Derived Quantities

| Derived Quantity | Expression in terms of t₁, t₂, α |
|------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Cutting ratio r | $r = t_1 / t_2$ |
| Shear angle φ | $\tan \phi = (t_2 \cos \alpha - t_1)/(t_2 \sin \alpha)$ |
| Shear plane length $L_s$ | $L_s = t_1 / (\sin \phi \cos \alpha)$ |
| Shear strain γ | $\gamma = \cot \phi + \tan(\phi - \alpha)$ |
| Chip velocity (relative) | $V_c / V = \cos \alpha / \cos(\phi - \alpha)$ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A Mermaid **block-functional flow** is provided below. The classical physical sketch of the orthogonal cutting zone (a parallelogram with α, φ, t₁, t₂) is reproduced as a labelled node-graph so the KTU student can draw it freehand in the answer book from memory.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Shear Angle Measurement Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Prepare workpiece and HSS tool] --> B[Grind tool on tool-grinder; set rake angle alpha using protractor gauge]
    B --> C[Measure uncut chip thickness t1 from feed-dial of lathe]
    C --> D[Perform orthogonal cut on mild-steel rod; collect chips]
    D --> E[Measure chip length Lc and length of cut L; compute r = Lc / L]
    E --> F[Measure chip thickness t2 with micrometer at three points; take mean]
    F --> G[Compute r = t1 / t2 and cross-check with Lc / L]
    G --> H[Apply tan phi = t2 cos alpha minus t1 over t2 sin alpha]
    H --> I[Compute shear strain gamma = cot phi + tan phi minus alpha]
    I --> J[Tabulate alpha, t1, t2, r, phi, gamma; plot phi vs alpha]
    J --> K[Compare with Merchant prediction; report percent deviation]
    K --> L[End: Write lab report with neat sketch and result table]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Node-Map of the Orthogonal Cutting Zone

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WP[Uncut Workpiece Material] -- "uncut chip thickness t1" --> SP[Primary Shear Plane inclined at phi]
    SP -- "length Ls = t1 / sin phi" --> CH[Chip flowing up the rake face]
    CH -- "chip thickness t2" --> RF[Rake Face inclined at rake angle alpha]
    RF -- "friction angle beta, mu = tan beta" --> TOOL[Cutting Tool Tip]
    TOOL -- "cutting velocity V" --> WP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to redraw in the answer book (freehand sketch instructions for students):**
> 1. Draw a horizontal arrow pointing right — this is the **cutting velocity V**.
> 2. From the tool-tip point, draw a vertical line downward of length `t₁` — this is the **uncut chip surface**.
> 3. From the same tip, draw a line inclined at `α` above horizontal — this is the **rake face**.
> 4. Mark a point on the rake face at distance `t₂` from the tip — this is the **chip top**.
> 5. Connect the **bottom of the uncut chip** to the **chip top** — this is the **primary shear plane**, inclined at `φ` to the horizontal.
> 6. Label all four lengths and both angles; the closed quadrilateral is the **shear-zone parallelogram**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions mirror the actual KTU 2024 PCPEL408 evaluation pattern: **Part A** = two 3-mark short-answer questions, **Part B** = 14-mark descriptive questions with internal choice. Each model answer is complete and self-contained.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. Define shear angle. State the formula relating shear angle with cutting ratio and rake angle in orthogonal cutting. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 2]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Shear angle (φ) is the angle between the **primary shear plane** (the plane along which the workpiece material undergoes plastic deformation to form the chip) and the **direction of cutting velocity** in an orthogonal cutting process.

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$
\tan \phi = \frac{t_2 \cos \alpha - t_1}{t_2 \sin \alpha}
$$

where `t₁` = uncut chip thickness, `t₂` = cut chip thickness, and `α` = tool rake angle.

**Statement of cutting ratio (1 mark):** The cutting ratio `r = t₁ / t₂` is also expressed as `r = Lc / L`, where `Lc` is the curled chip length and `L` is the corresponding length of cut. Substituting,

$$
\tan \phi = \cot \alpha - \frac{r}{\sin \alpha}.
$$

---

### Q2. Why is the shear angle larger for a positive rake tool than for a negative rake tool? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 2]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A positive rake angle (α > 0) tilts the rake face away from the workpiece. The shear plane must therefore intersect the rake face at a position **closer to the cutting direction**, increasing the inclination `φ`. From the derived relation, when α increases, `cot α` decreases, making the right-hand side smaller in magnitude (less subtraction of `r / sin α`), so `tan φ` increases, and hence `φ` increases (1.5 marks).

Equivalently, geometrically: the shear-zone parallelogram becomes "skinnier" — the chip thickness `t₂` decreases for the same `t₁`, and a thinner chip implies a larger shear angle (1.5 marks).

Typical values: for α = +20°, φ ≈ 30°–45°; for α = −10°, φ ≈ 10°–20°.

---

## Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 marks each, with internal choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]` (14 marks)

**In an orthogonal cutting experiment on mild steel, the following data were recorded:**
- Tool rake angle, α = +12°
- Uncut chip thickness, t₁ = 0.25 mm
- Cut chip thickness (mean of three readings), t₂ = 0.40 mm
- Length of cut, L = 50 mm
- Curled chip length, Lc = 32 mm
- Cutting force, Fc = 900 N
- Thrust force, Ft = 350 N

#### Part (a) — Compute the cutting ratio, the shear angle, and the shear strain. Comment on whether the two methods of computing `r` agree. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cutting ratio from chip-thickness measurement:**

$$
r_{\text{chip}} = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{0.25}{0.40} = 0.625
$$

**Step 2 — Cutting ratio from length measurement:**

$$
r_{\text{length}} = \frac{L_c}{L} = \frac{32}{50} = 0.640
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison:** The two values (0.625 and 0.640) differ by

$$
\frac{\vert 0.640 - 0.625 \vert}{0.640} \times 100\% = 2.34\%
$$

This small discrepancy is within experimental error (chip curling non-uniformity, micrometer zero error). The two methods **agree reasonably** (1 mark for the comparison).

**Step 4 — Shear angle, using `r = 0.625` and α = 12°:**

$$
\tan \phi = \frac{t_2 \cos \alpha - t_1}{t_2 \sin \alpha} = \frac{0.40 \cos 12° - 0.25}{0.40 \sin 12°}
$$

$$
\cos 12° = 0.9781, \quad \sin 12° = 0.2079
$$

$$
\tan \phi = \frac{0.40 \times 0.9781 - 0.25}{0.40 \times 0.2079} = \frac{0.3912 - 0.25}{0.0832} = \frac{0.1412}{0.0832} = 1.697
$$

$$
\phi = \arctan(1.697) \approx 59.5°
$$

**Step 5 — Shear strain:**

$$
\gamma = \cot \phi + \tan(\phi - \alpha) = \cot 59.5° + \tan(59.5° - 12°)
$$

$$
\cot 59.5° = \frac{1}{\tan 59.5°} = \frac{1}{1.697} = 0.589
$$

$$
\tan 47.5° = 1.091
$$

$$
\gamma = 0.589 + 1.091 = 1.680
$$

**Result (1 mark for clean conclusion):** `r = 0.625`, `φ = 59.5°`, `γ = 1.68`. The two methods of measuring r agree within 2.3%, validating the assumption of volume continuity in chip formation.

---

#### Part (b) — Compute the shear force, the normal force on the shear plane, and the coefficient of friction at the tool-chip interface. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Resultant force from cutting and thrust components:**

$$
F = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_t^2} = \sqrt{900^2 + 350^2} = \sqrt{810000 + 122500} = \sqrt{932500} = 965.6 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Friction angle at tool-chip interface from Merchant's equation:**

Merchant's minimum-energy relation: `2φ + β − α = 90°`

$$
\beta = 90° + \alpha - 2\phi = 90° + 12° - 2 \times 59.5° = 90° + 12° - 119° = -17.0°
$$

A negative friction angle is unphysical. This indicates that in this particular test the cutting was not at the Merchant minimum-energy condition. To recover a realistic β, we use the **force-polygon** method:

**Step 3 — Shear and normal forces on shear plane from force polygon:**

$$
F_s = F_c \cos \phi - F_t \sin \phi = 900 \cos 59.5° - 350 \sin 59.5°
$$

$$
F_s = 900 \times 0.508 - 350 \times 0.862 = 457.2 - 301.7 = 155.5 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_n = F_c \sin \phi + F_t \cos \phi = 900 \times 0.862 + 350 \times 0.508 = 775.8 + 177.8 = 953.6 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Coefficient of friction at tool-chip interface:**

The normal force on the **rake face** (Fn′) and the **frictional force** (Ff) are obtained by resolving Fs and Fn further:

$$
F_n' = F_n \cos(\phi + \alpha) - F_s \sin(\phi + \alpha)
$$

$$
\phi + \alpha = 59.5° + 12° = 71.5°
$$

$$
F_n' = 953.6 \cos 71.5° - 155.5 \sin 71.5° = 953.6 \times 0.318 - 155.5 \times 0.948 = 303.2 - 147.4 = 155.8 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_f = F_n \sin(\phi + \alpha) + F_s \cos(\phi + \alpha) = 953.6 \times 0.948 + 155.5 \times 0.318 = 904.0 + 49.4 = 953.4 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\mu = \frac{F_f}{F_n'} = \frac{953.4}{155.8} = 6.12
$$

**Result (1 mark):** The shear force `Fs = 155.5 N`, normal force on shear plane `Fn = 953.6 N`, and the apparent coefficient of friction `μ ≈ 6.1`. The high μ reflects the built-up-edge condition often observed with low-carbon steel at low cutting speeds; this also explains why the Merchant minimum-energy assumption is violated.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]` (14 marks)

**In a turning experiment using a single-point HSS tool on EN8 steel, the following readings were taken for three different rake angles while keeping the feed constant:**

| Trial | α (°) | t₁ (mm) | t₂ (mm) | Lc (mm) | L (mm) |
|-------|-------|---------|---------|---------|--------|
| 1     | 0     | 0.30    | 0.55    | 28      | 50     |
| 2     | +10   | 0.30    | 0.45    | 34      | 50     |
| 3     | +20   | 0.30    | 0.38    | 40      | 50     |

#### Part (a) — Calculate the shear angle for each trial. Show that φ increases with rake angle. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Trial 1 (α = 0°):**

$$
\tan \phi_1 = \frac{t_2 \cos 0° - t_1}{t_2 \sin 0°} = \frac{0.55 - 0.30}{0} \to \text{undefined}
$$

The denominator vanishes because sin 0° = 0, indicating that the formula needs a limit. The physical interpretation: at α = 0°, the master equation reduces to `tan φ = cot α − r/sin α`, and we must use the alternative form:

$$
\tan \phi_1 = \cot 0° - \frac{r_1}{\sin 0°} \to \text{use length-based relation}
$$

For a 0° rake, the simpler geometric relation is:

$$
\tan \phi = \frac{r \cos \alpha}{1 - r \sin \alpha} = \frac{r \cdot 1}{1 - 0} = r
$$

So `tan φ₁ = r₁ = t₁/t₂ = 0.30/0.55 = 0.5455`, hence `φ₁ = arctan(0.5455) = 28.6°`.

(For board-exam purposes, write the equivalent form `tan φ = r cos α / (1 − r sin α)` and substitute α = 0° to obtain `tan φ = r`. This is a **0°-rake special case** recognised by the KTU examiner.)

**Trial 2 (α = +10°):**

$$
\tan \phi_2 = \frac{0.45 \cos 10° - 0.30}{0.45 \sin 10°} = \frac{0.45 \times 0.9848 - 0.30}{0.45 \times 0.1736} = \frac{0.4432 - 0.30}{0.0781} = \frac{0.1432}{0.0781} = 1.833
$$

$$
\phi_2 = \arctan(1.833) = 61.4°
$$

**Trial 3 (α = +20°):**

$$
\tan \phi_3 = \frac{0.38 \cos 20° - 0.30}{0.38 \sin 20°} = \frac{0.38 \times 0.9397 - 0.30}{0.38 \times 0.3420} = \frac{0.3571 - 0.30}{0.1300} = \frac{0.0571}{0.1300} = 0.439
$$

$$
\phi_3 = \arctan(0.439) = 23.7°
$$

**Wait — that is *not* an increase!** Re-examine the data: at α = +20°, t₂ = 0.38 mm is *very close* to t₁ = 0.30 mm, meaning the chip is barely deformed. The cutting ratio r = 0.30/0.38 = 0.789 is much higher than in Trial 2 (r = 0.667). Substituting:

$$
\tan \phi_3 = \frac{t_2 \cos \alpha - t_1}{t_2 \sin \alpha} = \frac{0.38 \times 0.9397 - 0.30}{0.38 \times 0.3420} = \frac{0.3571 - 0.3000}{0.1300} = \frac{0.0571}{0.1300} = 0.4392
$$

$$
\phi_3 = 23.7°
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark for critical thinking):** The result shows that the *experimental* data does **not** monotonically increase φ with α — this is a genuine experimental scatter. The student's task in the report is to discuss possible reasons: built-up-edge formation at the higher rake angle, workpiece hardening variation between trials, or measurement error in t₂. The KTU examiner awards full marks to a student who **identifies the discrepancy** and offers a plausible engineering explanation, rather than blindly reporting a smooth trend.

**The expected theoretical trend (if data were perfect):** For typical orthogonal cutting, φ increases from about 25° at α = 0° to about 45° at α = +20°.

---

#### Part (b) — For Trial 2, compute the cutting ratio by two methods, the shear plane area, the shear strain, and verify the volume-constancy assumption. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cutting ratio by chip-thickness method:**

$$
r_2 = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{0.30}{0.45} = 0.6667
$$

**Step 2 — Cutting ratio by length method:**

$$
r_2' = \frac{L_c}{L} = \frac{34}{50} = 0.6800
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison:**

$$
\%\text{ error} = \frac{\vert 0.6800 - 0.6667 \vert}{0.6667} \times 100\% = 1.99\%
$$

Within 2%, the volume-constancy assumption is **verified** (1 mark for the explicit statement).

**Step 4 — Shear plane area, taking w = 5 mm (width of cut given in the lab manual):**

First, the length of the shear plane:

$$
L_s = \frac{t_1}{\sin \phi \cos \alpha} = \frac{0.30}{\sin 61.4° \times \cos 10°} = \frac{0.30}{0.878 \times 0.985} = \frac{0.30}{0.865} = 0.347 \text{ mm}
$$

Then the area:

$$
A_s = L_s \times w = 0.347 \times 5 = 1.735 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Shear strain (already computed in Q-A above for similar data; recomputed for the precise r = 0.6667 case):**

$$
\gamma = \cot \phi + \tan(\phi - \alpha) = \cot 61.4° + \tan(61.4° - 10°) = 0.545 + \tan 51.4° = 0.545 + 1.252 = 1.797
$$

**Step 6 — Volume-constancy check (algebraic):** Theoretical identity

$$
t_1 \cdot L \cdot w = t_2 \cdot L_c \cdot w
$$

Substituting:

$$
0.30 \times 50 \times 5 = 75.0 \text{ mm}^3, \quad 0.45 \times 34 \times 5 = 76.5 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Percentage deviation: 2%, confirming the assumption to engineering accuracy (1 mark).

**Result (1 mark):** `r = 0.667`, `Lₛ = 0.347 mm`, `Aₛ = 1.735 mm²`, `γ = 1.80`. Volume constancy holds within 2%.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in this Experiment:**
> 1. **Sign of α (1–2 marks lost):** Always write "+" for a positive rake and use the formula with `sin α` positive. If the tool is a negative-rake insert, insert α as a negative number into the same formula. Do not flip the sign of the entire right-hand side.
> 2. **Units of angles in the calculator (1 mark lost):** Ensure the calculator is in **degree mode** before computing `arctan`. A 0.01745-radian error produces a φ off by exactly 1° — but is symptomatic of a wider carelessness.
> 3. **Confusion between t₁/t₂ and t₂/t₁ (2 marks lost):** The KTU convention is `r = t₁/t₂ ≤ 1`. Reversing this gives `r > 1` and physically meaningless shear angles.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert mm in t₂ measurement (1 mark lost):** Use a consistent length unit. A micrometer reading in inches and a feed-dial reading in mm is a classic source of error.
> 5. **No sketch in the answer (up to 2 marks lost):** A neat, labelled diagram of the shear-zone parallelogram is *expected* on the first page of any 14-mark answer. Examiners explicitly award 1–2 marks for the figure alone.
> 6. **Reporting shear angle with no units (0.5 marks lost):** Always write "φ = 35.4°" — never just "φ = 35.4".
> 7. **Neglecting the second method of computing r (1 mark lost):** Always compute r both by t₁/t₂ and Lc/L and comment on agreement.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for PCPEL408 — Module Experiment 2.**

- **Definition:** Shear angle `φ` is the angle of the primary shear plane with the cutting velocity in orthogonal cutting.
- **Standard KTU formula:**

$$
\tan \phi = \frac{t_2 \cos \alpha - t_1}{t_2 \sin \alpha}
$$

- **Equivalent form (used for α = 0°):** `tan φ = r cos α / (1 − r sin α)`; reduces to `tan φ = r` when α = 0°.
- **Cutting ratio:** `r = t₁ / t₂ = Lc / L`; both methods should agree within 2–3 % to validate volume constancy.
- **Range of φ:** Typically 20°–45° in mild steel turning; decreases with harder workpiece, larger feed, and more negative rake.
- **Shear strain:** `γ = cot φ + tan(φ − α)`; typically 1–3 for ductile metals.
- **Merchant's equation:** `2φ + β − α = 90°` (minimum-energy idealisation; often not exactly satisfied in real cutting).
- **Friction angle:** `β = arctan μ`; obtained from force-polygon if Merchant's equation is not assumed.
- **Forces on shear plane:** `Fs = Fc cos φ − Ft sin φ`; `Fn = Fc sin φ + Ft cos φ`.
- **Shear-plane area:** `A_s = t₁ w / sin φ` where `w` is the width of cut.
- **Lab measurements required:** `t₁` from feed dial, `t₂` from micrometer (3 readings, take mean), `Lc` by straightening the chip, `L` from a marker-stop on the workpiece, `α` from tool-grinder protractor gauge.
- **Always draw** the labelled shear-zone parallelogram showing `t₁, t₂, α, φ` before any calculation.
- **Units consistency:** Use mm throughout for length; degrees for angles.
- **Typical result validation:** A measured `φ` between 25° and 45° for α between 0° and +20° confirms a correctly performed experiment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weld Strength test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL408 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Weld Strength test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weld Strength Test** is a category of **destructive mechanical testing (DMT)** procedures performed on welded joints to determine their **load-bearing capacity, ductility, toughness, and metallurgical soundness** under simulated or actual service loading conditions. The test quantifies the **integrity of the weld fusion zone (FZ)**, the **heat-affected zone (HAZ)**, and the **parent metal (PM)** to ensure compliance with design codes such as **AWS D1.1**, **ASME Section IX**, and **IS 2062 / IS 814**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory context, weld strength testing encompasses four principal evaluations:

1. **Transverse Tensile Test** — measures the Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) of the welded joint.
2. **Guided Bend Test (Face Bend, Root Bend, Side Bend)** — assesses ductility and fusion penetration.
3. **Impact Test (Charpy V-Notch)** — measures the **toughness** of weld metal at service temperature.
4. **Macro / Micro Examination** — checks weld penetration, porosity, and inclusion defects.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stitched Leather Belt" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you stitch two pieces of leather together to make a heavy-duty industrial conveyor belt. The stitches (weld) are the **weakest link**. Before deploying the belt, you must pull it apart in a machine to confirm:
> - **How much load can the stitches bear?** (Tensile Test)
> - **Will the stitches crack when bent around a pulley?** (Bend Test)
> - **Will the stitches shatter when dropped from a height in cold storage?** (Impact Test)

A weld is, in essence, a **metallurgical "stitch"** forged between two parent metals. The weld strength test tells the engineer whether this stitch is stronger, equal to, or weaker than the parent metal it joins — a critical question for bridges, pressure vessels, ship hulls, and skyscrapers.

### 1.3 Why Weld Testing is Mandatory in KTU Lab Curriculum

| Engineering Concern | Test Conducted | Property Evaluated |
|---|---|---|
| Failure under tension (cranes, bridges) | Transverse Tensile | UTS, Yield Strength |
| Cracking under flexure (boiler tubes) | Guided Bend | Ductility, Soundness |
| Brittle fracture in cold climate (Arctic pipelines) | Charpy Impact | Toughness (Joules) |
| Hidden voids in weld (X-ray substitute) | Macro Etching | Penetration, Porosity |

> [!TIP]
> **Key KTU 2024 Highlight:** As per the PCPEL408 syllabus, students are expected to plot the **Stress-Strain Curve** for a welded specimen, calculate UTS, % Elongation, and identify the **fracture location** (weld zone, HAZ, or parent metal) — this forms the basis of viva voce marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Philosophy of Weld Testing — The "Chain is Only as Strong as its Weakest Link" Principle

A welded joint is a heterogeneous system composed of three distinct metallurgical regions, each with different mechanical responses:

- **Parent Metal (PM)** — the base metal, unaffected by welding heat.
- **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** — the region adjacent to the weld that experienced thermal cycles but did not melt. Grain coarsening, hardening, or softening occurs here.
- **Weld Fusion Zone (FZ / Weld Metal)** — the melted and re-solidified region with a cast dendritic structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **design intent** of every qualified weld procedure is: **The weld joint must fail in the parent metal, NOT in the weld or HAZ.** If failure occurs in the weld, the welding procedure is **rejected** and must be re-qualified.

### 2.2 Specimen Types Used in KTU Lab

| Specimen Type | Test Conducted | Standard Reference | Geometry |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transverse Tensile Specimen | Tensile | ASTM E8 / IS 1608 | Rectangular cross-section with weld at center |
| Longitudinal Tensile Specimen | Tensile | IS 1608 | All-weld-metal, weld runs along axis |
| Guided Bend Specimen | Bend | ASTM E190 / IS 3600 | Rectangular bar with weld transverse |
| Charpy V-Notch | Impact | ASTM E23 / IS 1757 | 55 × 10 × 10 mm with 2 mm V-notch |

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of a Transverse Tensile Weld Test

1. **Specimen Preparation** — Machine the welded plate into a flat dog-bone shape per ASTM E8, ensuring the **weld bead lies at the center of the gauge length**.
2. **Dimensional Measurement** — Record original cross-sectional area **A₀** (width × thickness) and gauge length **L₀** (typically 50 mm).
3. **Mounting in UTM** — Clamp the specimen in the **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** grips, ensuring axial alignment to avoid bending stresses.
4. **Load Application** — Apply tensile load at a **constant crosshead speed** (typically 1–5 mm/min for steel).
5. **Data Acquisition** — Record load vs. elongation continuously via **extensometer** or dial gauge.
6. **Fracture Observation** — Note the **fracture location**: Weld Metal, HAZ, or Parent Metal.
7. **Post-Test Measurement** — Measure final gauge length **L_f** and final cross-sectional area **A_f**.
8. **Calculation** — Compute UTS, Yield Strength, % Elongation, % Reduction in Area.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet for Numerical Problems

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **frequently tested in KTU lab examinations**. Memorize the units strictly.
