| Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engineering Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | F = applied load, A₀ = original area | N/mm² (MPa) |
| Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | ΔL = change in length, L₀ = gauge length | dimensionless |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength | $\text{UTS} = \dfrac{F_{\max}}{A_0}$ | F_max = maximum load | MPa |
| Yield Strength (0.2% offset) | $\sigma_y = \dfrac{F_y}{A_0}$ | F_y = load at 0.2% plastic strain | MPa |
| Percentage Elongation | $\%EL = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | L_f = final gauge length | % |
| Percentage Reduction in Area | $\%RA = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_f} \times 100$ | A_f = final area at fracture | % |
| Impact Toughness | $K = E_{\text{absorbed}}$ | Energy absorbed in Charpy test | Joules (J) |
| Bend Test Mandrel Radius (Face/Root Bend) | $r = \dfrac{W}{2} - t$ | W = die width, t = specimen thickness | mm |
| Bend Angle (Qualifying) | $\theta = 180°$ | Standard for ASME / IS qualified welds | degrees |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Confusing **engineering stress** (uses original A₀) with **true stress** (uses instantaneous area). For lab reports, always use **engineering stress** unless explicitly asked.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure Vessel Fabrication (ASME Sec. VIII):** Every batch of welded boilers undergoes hydrostatic + tensile testing of a **procedure qualification plate (PQP)**.
- **Aerospace (AWS D17.1):** Welded titanium airframe components require 100% bend test qualification.
- **Offshore Pipelines (API 1104):** Charpy impact testing at **–46 °C** is mandatory for Arctic-grade pipelines.
- **Automotive Spot Welds (AWS D8.9):** Shear-tension tests on resistance spot welds ensure crash safety compliance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of % Elongation for a Welded Specimen

**Given:** A low-carbon steel plate is welded using the Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) process. After machining per ASTM E8:
- Original gauge length: $L_0 = 50$ mm
- Original width: $b_0 = 20$ mm
- Original thickness: $t_0 = 6$ mm
- After fracture in UTM, final gauge length: $L_f = 64$ mm
- Final thickness at fracture: $t_f = 3.2$ mm
- Final width at fracture: $b_f = 15$ mm
- Maximum load recorded: $F_{\max} = 78$ kN

**Required:** Compute (a) Original Area A₀, (b) Final Area A_f, (c) UTS, (d) % Elongation, (e) % Reduction in Area.

---

#### Step 1 — Original Cross-Sectional Area (A₀)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= b_0 \times t_0 \\
A_0 &= 20 \;\text{mm} \times 6 \;\text{mm} \\
A_0 &= 120 \;\text{mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* The original cross-sectional area is simply the product of the machined width and plate thickness before any load is applied.

---

#### Step 2 — Final Cross-Sectional Area at Fracture (A_f)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_f &= b_f \times t_f \\
A_f &= 15 \;\text{mm} \times 3.2 \;\text{mm} \\
A_f &= 48 \;\text{mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* At the fracture point, the specimen necks down due to localized plastic deformation. The reduced dimensions are measured after the two halves are fitted back together.

---

#### Step 3 — Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UTS} &= \dfrac{F_{\max}}{A_0} \\
\text{UTS} &= \dfrac{78{,}000 \;\text{N}}{120 \;\text{mm}^2} \\
\text{UTS} &= 650 \;\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* The maximum load sustained by the specimen, divided by the original (not final) area, gives the engineering ultimate tensile strength — a material property used in design codes.

---

#### Step 4 — Percentage Elongation (%EL)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%EL &= \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 \\
\%EL &= \dfrac{64 - 50}{50} \times 100 \\
\%EL &= \dfrac{14}{50} \times 100 \\
\%EL &= 28\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Elongation measures ductility — how much the specimen stretched before fracture. A 28% elongation for low-carbon steel is consistent with **good weld ductility** (typical parent metal value: 30–35%).

---

#### Step 5 — Percentage Reduction in Area (%RA)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%RA &= \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 \\
\%RA &= \dfrac{120 - 48}{120} \times 100 \\
\%RA &= \dfrac{72}{120} \times 100 \\
\%RA &= 60\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Reduction in area is a more sensitive indicator of ductility than elongation because it captures localized necking. For mild steel, %RA > 50% indicates excellent formability.

---

### 3.2 Bend Test Analytical Derivation — Mandrel & Die Geometry

**Given:** A guided bend specimen of thickness $t = 10$ mm is to be tested per IS 3600.

**Required:** Determine the (a) Mandrel diameter, (b) Die inner width, given the standard formula.

For **face bend** and **root bend** tests on plates of thickness $t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Die inner radius} \; R_d &= \dfrac{3t}{2} \\
R_d &= \dfrac{3 \times 10}{2} = 15 \;\text{mm} \\
\text{Mandrel radius} \; r &= \dfrac{3t}{2} \\
r &= 15 \;\text{mm} \\
\text{Die inner width} \; W &= 2(R_d + t) + t \\
W &= 2(15 + 10) + 10 = 55 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* The die width must accommodate the specimen thickness plus clearance for the bend curvature. The mandrel diameter governs the bending strain on the outer fiber: $\varepsilon_{outer} = \dfrac{t}{2r}$.

**Outer Fiber Strain Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{outer} &= \dfrac{t}{2r + t} \times 100 \\
\varepsilon_{outer} &= \dfrac{10}{30 + 10} \times 100 \\
\varepsilon_{outer} &= 25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* A 25% outer-fiber strain is considered the **qualification threshold** for welds per ASME Section IX. If the specimen bends to 180° without cracks > 3 mm, the weld is **qualified**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Weld Strength Testing Protocol

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["WELDED PLATE INPUT"] --> B["NDT INSPECTION - VT AND UT"]
    B --> C{"DEFECT FREE?"}
    C -- NO --> D["REJECT AND REWELD"]
    C -- YES --> E["MACHINE SPECIMEN AS PER ASTM E8 OR IS 1608"]
    E --> F["MEASURE A0 AND L0"]
    F --> G["MOUNT IN UTM HYDRAULIC GRIPS"]
    G --> H["APPLY TENSILE LOAD AT 1 TO 5 MM PER MIN"]
    H --> I["RECORD LOAD VS ELONGATION DATA"]
    I --> J{"FRACTURE OBSERVED"}
    J --> K["RECORD FMAX AND FRACTURE LOCATION"]
    K --> L["MEASURE LF AND AF"]
    L --> M["CALCULATE UTS PERCENT ELONGATION PERCENT RA"]
    M --> N["LOCATE FAILURE ZONE WELD OR HAZ OR PM"]
    N --> O["COMPARE WITH ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA AWS D1.1"]
    O --> P{"WELD QUALIFIED?"}
    P -- YES --> Q["PROCEDURE QUALIFIED REPORT ISSUED"]
    P -- NO --> R["REWORK PROCEDURE SPECIFICATION"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Weld Test Specimen Classification Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TEN["TENSILE TEST FAMILY"]
        T1["TRANSVERSE TENSILE - WELD AT CENTER"]
        T2["LONGITUDINAL TENSILE - ALL WELD METAL"]
    end
    subgraph BEND["BEND TEST FAMILY"]
        B1["FACE BEND - TENSION ON WELD FACE"]
        B2["ROOT BEND - TENSION ON WELD ROOT"]
        B3["SIDE BEND - BENDING ACROSS WIDTH"]
    end
    subgraph IMP["IMPACT TEST FAMILY"]
        I1["CHARPY V NOTCH AT WELD CENTER"]
        I2["CHARPY V NOTCH AT HAZ"]
        I3["CHARPY V NOTCH AT PM REFERENCE"]
    end
    subgraph MET["METALLOGRAPHIC TEST FAMILY"]
        M1["MACRO ETCHING FOR PENETRATION CHECK"]
        M2["MICRO HARDNESS SURVEY ACROSS HAZ"]
    end
    WELD["WELDED JOINT PLATE"] --> TEN
    WELD --> BEND
    WELD --> IMP
    WELD --> MET
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Stress-Strain Response of a Welded Specimen

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["START - APPLY LOAD AT ZERO"] --> E1["ELASTIC REGION - HOOKEAN BEHAVIOR"]
    E1 --> E2{"STRESS EXCEEDS YIELD OF WELD METAL?"}
    E2 -- YES --> P1["PLASTIC DEFORMATION - WELD NECKS FIRST"]
    P1 --> E3{"STRESS EXCEEDS YIELD OF PM?"}
    E3 -- NO --> F1["FRACTURE IN WELD - REJECT WELD"]
    E3 -- YES --> P2["PLASTIC DEFORMATION - PM NECKS"]
    P2 --> UTS["ULTIMATE LOAD REACHED FMAX"]
    UTS --> N["NECKING LOCALIZES IN PM"]
    N --> F2["FRACTURE IN PM - WELD QUALIFIED"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** This block architecture models the **failure preference logic** of a properly qualified weld. A weld is qualified only if failure occurs in the parent metal (PM), confirming the weld metal is **stronger** than the base metal.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *What is the significance of performing a transverse tensile test on a welded specimen? List the parameters measured.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **transverse tensile test** is performed on a welded joint with the **weld bead perpendicular to the loading axis** to evaluate the **overall strength of the welded joint** as a system (weld + HAZ + parent metal).
>
> **Parameters measured:**
> 1. **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)** — maximum load per unit original area (MPa).
> 2. **Percentage Elongation (%EL)** — ductility indicator.
> 3. **Percentage Reduction in Area (%RA)** — localized ductility.
> 4. **Fracture Location** — Weld / HAZ / Parent Metal — the most critical qualitative observation.
>
> **Significance:** The test verifies that the welded joint meets the **minimum strength requirement** of the parent metal and that failure occurs **preferentially in the parent metal**, confirming weld overmatch.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between face bend test and root bend test.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Face Bend Test | Root Bend Test |
|---|---|---|
| Tension Side | Weld face (cap side) | Weld root side |
| Purpose | Detects defects near weld face (porosity, undercut) | Detects lack of penetration, root porosity |
| Standard | ASTM E190 / IS 3600 | ASTM E190 / IS 3600 |
| Failure Criterion | Crack > 3 mm on tension surface at 180° bend | Crack > 3 mm on root surface at 180° bend |

> Both tests are conducted on **transverse guided bend specimens** using a mandrel-and-die fixture in a hydraulic press. The specimen is bent to **180°** (or until failure) and visually inspected for surface cracks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam – July 2024]** *(a)* Describe the procedure for conducting a **transverse tensile test** on a butt-welded mild steel specimen per **ASTM E8** with a neat sketch. *(b)* A welded specimen of original cross-section 20 mm × 6 mm fractured at a maximum load of 78 kN. The final dimensions at the fracture were 15 mm × 3.2 mm and the final gauge length was 64 mm (original gauge length = 50 mm). Calculate the UTS, % Elongation, and % Reduction in Area. **Comment on the weld quality** based on the fracture location (assume the specimen fractured in the parent metal, 30 mm away from the weld centerline).

---

#### Part (a) — Procedure and Sketch (7 marks)

**1. Specimen Preparation (2 marks)**
- Cut a **transverse slice** from the welded plate such that the weld bead lies at the **geometric center** of the gauge length.
- Machine the specimen to the **standard rectangular dog-bone profile** per ASTM E8, maintaining a uniform gauge length of **50 mm** and parallel gauge width of **20 mm**.
- Remove all **undercut, spatter, and weld reinforcement** flush with the plate surface (or leave a small crown, depending on code).

**2. Dimensional Recording (1 mark)**
- Measure original width $b_0$, thickness $t_0$, and gauge length $L_0$ using a **vernier caliper** (±0.02 mm accuracy).
- Calculate original area $A_0 = b_0 \times t_0$.

**3. UTM Setup (2 marks)**
- Mount the specimen in the **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** hydraulic wedge grips.
- Align the specimen axis with the loading axis to avoid **eccentric loading**.
- Attach an **extensometer** to the gauge section (or use a dial gauge).
- Set the crosshead speed to **1–5 mm/min** for steel.

**4. Test Execution and Recording (1 mark)**
- Apply tensile load gradually; record load-elongation data continuously.
- Note the **maximum load (F_max)** and the load at **0.2% offset yield**.
- After fracture, identify and record the **fracture location**.

**5. Post-Test Calculations (1 mark)**
- Measure final gauge length $L_f$ and final cross-section $b_f \times t_f$.
- Compute UTS, %EL, %RA.

**Neat Sketch Description (Mermaid Block Diagram):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["WELDED PLATE WITH BUTT JOINT"] --> B["MACHINE TRANSVERSE SLICE 200 MM LONG"]
    B --> C["REDUCE GAUGE SECTION TO 20 MM WIDTH"]
    C --> D["SPECIMEN WITH WELD AT GAUGE CENTER"]
    D --> E["MOUNT IN UTM GRIPS"]
    E --> F["APPLY TENSILE LOAD AXIALLY"]
    F --> G["RECORD LOAD VS ELONGATION"]
    G --> H["FRACTURE OCCURS - LOCATE AND MEASURE"]
```

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

Given:
- $b_0 = 20$ mm, $t_0 = 6$ mm, $L_0 = 50$ mm
- $F_{\max} = 78$ kN $= 78{,}000$ N
- $b_f = 15$ mm, $t_f = 3.2$ mm, $L_f = 64$ mm

**Step 1: Original Area (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= b_0 \times t_0 \\
A_0 &= 20 \times 6 = 120 \;\text{mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Ultimate Tensile Strength (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UTS} &= \dfrac{F_{\max}}{A_0} \\
\text{UTS} &= \dfrac{78{,}000}{120} = 650 \;\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Percentage Elongation (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%EL &= \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 \\
\%EL &= \dfrac{64 - 50}{50} \times 100 = 28\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Percentage Reduction in Area (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%RA &= \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 \\
A_f &= 15 \times 3.2 = 48 \;\text{mm}^2 \\
\%RA &= \dfrac{120 - 48}{120} \times 100 = 60\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Weld Quality Comment (1 mark)**

> Since the fracture occurred in the **parent metal** (30 mm away from the weld centerline), the weld joint is **qualified** and exhibits **weld overmatch**. The measured UTS of 650 MPa is consistent with mild steel parent metal properties. The high %EL (28%) and %RA (60%) indicate excellent ductility.

---

### **OR (Internal Choice for Question 3B)**

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam – July 2024]** *(a)* Explain the **guided bend test** procedure for a butt-welded specimen per **IS 3600**. State the acceptance criteria. *(b)* A guided bend specimen of thickness 10 mm is bent over a mandrel of radius 15 mm through an angle of 180°. Determine the **outer fiber strain** and comment on whether the weld qualifies per ASME Section IX (acceptance criterion: $\varepsilon_{outer} \geq 20\%$).

---

#### Part (a) — Bend Test Procedure (7 marks)

**1. Specimen Geometry (1 mark)**
- Transverse slice of full plate thickness, width = 38 mm (or 10t, whichever is smaller), length ≈ 250 mm.
- Weld bead at the center, reinforcement **left intact** (not removed).

**2. Test Setup (2 marks)**
- Use a **mandrel-and-die fixture** mounted in a hydraulic press.
- **Die radius** $R_d = 1.5 t = 15$ mm; **mandrel radius** $r = 1.5 t = 15$ mm.
- **Die inner width** $W = 55$ mm (calculated earlier).

**3. Test Conduct (2 marks)**
- Place specimen on the die supports with weld at center.
- Apply load via mandrel to bend the specimen gradually.
- Continue until the specimen forms a **U-shape with included angle of 180°**, OR until fracture.
- For **face bend**: weld face is on the tension (outer) side.
- For **root bend**: weld root is on the tension side.

**4. Acceptance Criteria (2 marks)**
- After bending to 180°, inspect the tension surface under **10× magnification**.
- **Accept** if no single crack exceeds **3 mm** in length.
- **Reject** if any crack > 3 mm is observed, or if complete fracture occurs before 180°.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

Given:
- $t = 10$ mm, $r = 15$ mm, bend angle $\theta = 180°$

**Step 1: Outer Fiber Strain Formula (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{outer} &= \dfrac{t}{2r + t} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* The outer fiber travels along a path of radius $(r + t)$, while the neutral axis follows radius $r$. The strain is the ratio of arc-length difference.

**Step 2: Substitution (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{outer} &= \dfrac{10}{2(15) + 10} \times 100 \\
\varepsilon_{outer} &= \dfrac{10}{40} \times 100 \\
\varepsilon_{outer} &= 25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Comparison with Acceptance Criterion (2 marks)**

> Since $25\% > 20\%$ (ASME Sec. IX acceptance threshold), the **outer fiber strain exceeds the qualification limit**.
>
> **Conclusion:** The bend test is **more severe** than the minimum ASME requirement, indicating a **conservative and reliable test**. If no surface cracks > 3 mm appear after this test, the weld is **qualified for service**.

**Step 4: Additional Validation (1 mark)**

> Inner fiber strain: $\varepsilon_{inner} = -\dfrac{t}{2r - t} \times 100 = -\dfrac{10}{20} \times 100 = -50\%$ (compression). This confirms the specimen experiences both tensile (outer) and compressive (inner) deformation during bending.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Weld Strength Test Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** Writing UTS as "650" instead of "650 MPa" loses 0.5 marks consistently.
> 2. **Confusing L_f and L₀:** Many students calculate %EL as $(L_0 - L_f)/L_0$ — the formula is $(L_f - L_0)/L_0$. Always remember **elongation is an increase**.
> 3. **Using Final Area for UTS:** UTS uses **original area A₀**, not final area A_f. Using A_f gives **true stress at fracture**, not UTS.
> 4. **Not stating fracture location:** Even with correct numerical values, omitting the qualitative observation of "fracture in PM = qualified weld" costs **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Mixing up Bend Test Mandrel and Die:** The **mandrel** is the inner plunger (radius r); the **die** is the outer support (radius R_d). Confusing these loses 1 mark in descriptive questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Weld Strength Test** = Destructive test on welded joints to verify UTS, ductility, toughness.
- ✅ **Transverse Tensile Test** = Weld at gauge center; measures UTS, %EL, %RA; ideal fracture in PM.
- ✅ **Guided Bend Test** = Mandrel + Die fixture; 180° bend; acceptance: no crack > 3 mm.
- ✅ **Charpy Impact Test** = V-notch specimen; measures absorbed energy in Joules.
- ✅ **UTS Formula:** $\text{UTS} = F_{\max} / A_0$ — **always use original area**.
- ✅ **%EL Formula:** $\%EL = (L_f - L_0)/L_0 \times 100$ — **elongation is positive**.
- ✅ **%RA Formula:** $\%RA = (A_0 - A_f)/A_0 \times 100$ — **reduction is positive**.
- ✅ **Outer Fiber Strain (Bend):** $\varepsilon_{outer} = t/(2r + t) \times 100$.
- ✅ **Weld Overmatch:** Weld strength > Parent metal strength → failure in PM = QUALIFIED.
- ✅ **Weld Undermatch:** Weld strength < Parent metal strength → failure in Weld = REJECTED.
- ✅ **Standards:** ASTM E8 (tensile), ASTM E190 (bend), ASTM E23 (impact), IS 1608 / IS 3600 / IS 1757.
- ✅ **Acceptance Angle:** 180° bend for face/root/side bend per ASME Section IX.
- ✅ **Typical Values for Mild Steel:** UTS ≈ 410–650 MPa, %EL ≈ 20–35%, %RA ≈ 50–70%.
- ✅ **Fracture Location Priority (best to worst):** PM > HAZ > Weld Metal > Weld Root.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) in welding, Metallurgical study and Micro hardness test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL408 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) in welding, Metallurgical study and Micro hardness test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** is defined as the region of the base metal adjacent to the weld bead whose microstructure and mechanical properties have been altered by the thermal cycle of the welding process, but which has **not** been melted. The HAZ is a non-fusion zone where solid-state phase transformations, grain growth, and precipitation phenomena occur purely due to the diffusion-controlled response of the parent metal to the moving heat source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCPEL408):**
> *"The Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) is that portion of the base metal in which the microstructure and mechanical properties have been altered by the heat of welding, brazing, soldering, or thermal cutting, but without melting of the parent metal."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The "Toast Halo" of Bread**
> Imagine placing a slice of bread close to a campfire (but not in the flame). The surface closest to the fire **burns to ash** (this is the **Fusion Zone / Weld Metal**). Just next to it, the bread becomes **dark brown and crispy** — chemically and texturally transformed by heat but not burned away (this is the **Coarse Grain HAZ**). Further out, it becomes **light golden and firm** (the **Fine Grain HAZ**). Beyond that, it's **slightly dried and warm** (the **Intercritical / Subcritical HAZ**). Finally, you reach the **untouched soft bread** — the **Unaffected Parent Metal**.
> 
> Just like bread, the steel in a weldment experiences a **thermal gradient**, and each concentric "ring" around the weld pool is exposed to a different peak temperature, producing different microstructures and mechanical responses.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| **Ac1 Temperature** | **723 °C** (Eutectoid temp., low-carbon steel) | Lower critical — start of austenite formation on heating |
| **Ac3 Temperature** | **≈ 850–910 °C** (depends on %C) | Upper critical — complete austenitization |
| **Melting Point of Mild Steel** | **≈ 1500 °C** | Boundary between HAZ and Fusion Zone |
| **Standard Etchant (Nital 2%)** | **2 mL HNO₃ + 98 mL Ethanol** | Used for revealing ferrite–pearlite boundaries |
| **Vickers Load (Microhardness)** | Typically **100 g to 500 g** (HV 0.1 – HV 0.5) | Standard for HAZ traverse |
| **Indentation Spacing** | **≥ 2.5 × diagonal length** | To avoid work-hardening interaction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Metallurgical Study** of the HAZ involves optical microscopy at **50×–500× magnification** to identify phases (ferrite, pearlite, bainite, martensite), while the **Microhardness Test** quantifies the mechanical property variation across the weldment cross-section.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sub-Zones of the Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)

The HAZ is **not a homogeneous region**. Based on the peak temperature experienced during welding, it is subdivided into four distinct sub-zones for low-carbon / low-alloy steels:

#### 2.1.1 Coarse Grain HAZ (CGHAZ)
- **Peak Temperature Range:** Above **≈ 1100 °C**, up to the solidus (≈ 1450 °C).
- **Microstructural Evolution:** Austenite grains grow **coarsely** due to grain-boundary migration at high temperatures. On rapid cooling, this coarse austenite transforms to **coarse ferrite + aligned (Widmanstätten) ferrite + pearlite**, or in hardenable steels, to **coarse martensite / bainite**.
- **Mechanical Response:** **Lowest toughness** in the weldment — most crack-susceptible zone.

#### 2.1.2 Fine Grain HAZ (FGHAZ)
- **Peak Temperature Range:** Between **Ac3 (≈ 910 °C)** and **≈ 1100 °C**.
- **Microstructural Evolution:** Complete austenitization occurs, but grain growth is **restricted** because the temperature is not high enough for extensive coarsening. On cooling, fine equiaxed ferrite + pearlite forms.
- **Mechanical Response:** **Optimum strength-toughness combination** in the HAZ.

#### 2.1.3 Intercritical HAZ (ICHAZ) / Partially Transformed Zone
- **Peak Temperature Range:** Between **Ac1 (≈ 723 °C)** and **Ac3 (≈ 910 °C)**.
- **Microstructural Evolution:** Only a **fraction** of the microstructure transforms to austenite (the carbon-rich pearlite colonies fully austenitize, but ferrite remains largely unaffected). On cooling, the austenite reverts to a **fine mixture of ferrite + carbides**, while the original ferrite is retained.
- **Mechanical Response:** Often the **weakest zone** in multipass welds (Type IV cracking in creep-resistant steels).

#### 2.1.4 Subcritical HAZ (SCHAZ) / Tempered Zone
- **Peak Temperature Range:** Below **Ac1 (≈ 723 °C)**.
- **Microstructural Evolution:** No phase transformation, only **tempering** of pre-existing microstructures (e.g., tempering of martensite in HSLA steel, or spheroidization of pearlite).
- **Mechanical Response:** Slight **softening** compared to parent metal.

### 2.2 Metallurgical Study Procedure

> [!NOTE]
> The metallurgical study is conducted on a **polished and etched cross-section** of the weldment using an **optical / inverted metallurgical microscope**.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. **Sectioning** — Cut the weldment transverse to the weld direction using an abrasive cut-off wheel with coolant.
2. **Mounting** — Hot-mount in phenolic resin (for routine) or cold-mount in epoxy (for delicate samples).
3. **Grinding** — Sequential SiC papers: **180 → 320 → 600 → 800 → 1000 → 1200 grit**.
4. **Polishing** — Diamond paste **6 µm → 3 µm → 1 µm**, followed by final polish with colloidal silica (0.05 µm).
5. **Etching** — **Nital 2%** (most common for carbon/mild steel); **Picral 4%** for carbide contrast; **Kalling's reagent** for stainless steels.
6. **Microscopic Examination** — Capture micrographs at **100×, 200×, 500×** across:
   - Parent Metal (PM)
   - Subcritical HAZ (SCHAZ)
   - Intercritical HAZ (ICHAZ)
   - Fine Grain HAZ (FGHAZ)
   - Coarse Grain HAZ (CGHAZ)
   - Fusion Zone (FZ / Weld Metal)

### 2.3 Microhardness Test Procedure

The **Vickers microhardness test (HV)** is the preferred method for HAZ characterization per IS 1501 / ASTM E384.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. **Identify Indent Locations** — Mark a traverse line perpendicular to the fusion boundary, starting from the **parent metal → through all HAZ sub-zones → into the weld metal**.
2. **Loading** — Apply a load (typically **100 g – 500 g**) for a **dwell time of 10–15 seconds**.
3. **Measurement** — Measure the **two diagonals (d₁, d₂)** of the rhombohedral indent under the microscope.
4. **Calculation** — Compute HV using the formula.
5. **Plot** — Draw a **Hardness Traverse Curve**: Hardness (HV) vs. Distance from weld centreline.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Symbol | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Vickers Hardness** | $HV = 0.1891 \cdot \dfrac{F}{d^{2}}$ | F in **N**, d in **mm** | Microhardness in HAZ traverse |
| 2 | Vickers (kgf) form | $HV = 1.854 \cdot \dfrac{F}{d^{2}}$ | F in **kgf**, d in **mm** | Lab manual convention |
| 3 | Mean Diagonal | $d = \dfrac{d_{1}+d_{2}}{2}$ | mm | Average of two indent diagonals |
| 4 | **Knoop Hardness** | $HK = 0.01423 \cdot \dfrac{F}{d^{2}}$ | F in **N**, d in **mm** (long diagonal) | For brittle / thin HAZ layers |
| 5 | Heat Input (Arc Energy) | $Q = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | **kJ/mm** | Welding parameter effect on HAZ |
| 6 | Cooling Time (t₈/₅) | $t_{8/5} = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi \lambda}\left(\dfrac{1}{500-T_{0}} - \dfrac{1}{800-T_{0}}\right)$ | seconds | Defines HAZ microstructure |
| 7 | HAZ Width (empirical) | $W_{HAZ} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{Q}{v}}$ style trend | mm | Wider with higher heat input |
| 8 | Indent Spacing Rule | Spacing $\geq 2.5 d$ | mm | Prevents strain-field overlap |
| 9 | Ac1 / Ac3 boundary | **723 °C** / **≈ 910 °C** | °C | Defines HAZ sub-zone limits |
| 10 | Etchant — Nital 2% | $2\%$ HNO₃ + $98\%$ C₂H₅OH | vol % | Reveals ferrite grain boundaries |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the Vickers formula, the constant **1.854** comes from the geometry of the square pyramidal indenter (face angle 136°): $\dfrac{2 \sin(136°/2)}{9.81 \cdot d^{2}} \approx 1.854$ when F is in kgf.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Failure Analysis:** HAZ softening or HAZ cracking is the **#1 cause of in-service weld failures** in pressure vessels, pipelines, and offshore structures.
- **Weld Procedure Specification (WPS):** Heat input (Q) is controlled to **manage HAZ width and hardness**.
- **Material Selection:** Creep-resistant steels (P91, P92) require **Type IV cracking** assessment in ICHAZ.
- **Quality Control:** Hardness traverses per **ASME IX / AWS D1.1** acceptance criteria (typically **≤ 350 HV10** for sour-service applications to prevent HIC/SSC).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Vickers Hardness Number (HV)

The Vickers hardness is defined as the **load divided by the surface area of the indentation** produced by a square-based pyramidal diamond indenter with face angle **θ = 136°**.

#### Step 1 — Geometry of the Indentation

The indent is a square of diagonal **d** lying on the surface. The slant height of the pyramid is **L = d / (2 \sin(θ/2))**.

> This comes from resolving the diagonal along the slant face: $\sin(\theta/2) = (d/2) / L$.

#### Step 2 — Surface Area of the Pyramid

The slant area of **one face** is:

$$
A_{face} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot d \cdot L = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot d \cdot \dfrac{d}{2\sin(\theta/2)} = \dfrac{d^{2}}{4 \sin(\theta/2)}
$$

The total slant surface area of the **four-faced pyramid**:

$$
A_{total} = 4 \cdot A_{face} = \dfrac{d^{2}}{\sin(\theta/2)}
$$

#### Step 3 — Substitute θ = 136°

$$
\sin\left(\dfrac{136°}{2}\right) = \sin(68°) = 0.9272
$$

Therefore:

$$
A_{total} = \dfrac{d^{2}}{0.9272} \approx 1.0785 \, d^{2}
$$

#### Step 4 — Definition of Vickers Hardness

By definition, hardness is load divided by contact area:

$$
HV = \dfrac{F}{A_{total}}
$$

Substituting A_total:

$$
HV = \dfrac{F}{1.0785 \, d^{2}}
$$

To express in the **conventional kgf/mm² unit** (since Vickers numbers are reported in kgf), we divide force by g = 9.81 m/s²:

$$
HV = \dfrac{F\,[\text{kgf}]}{1.0785 \, d^{2}\,[\text{mm}^{2}]} \cdot \dfrac{1}{9.81} \cdot 9.81 = \dfrac{F / g}{1.0785 \, d^{2}}
$$

The numerical constant thus becomes:

$$
\dfrac{1}{1.0785 \times 9.81 / 1000} \approx 1.854
$$

> Wait — let me re-derive carefully. The standard derivation yields:

$$
\dfrac{1}{1.0785} \approx 0.9272
$$

Converting F from N to kgf divides by 9.81:

$$
HV = \dfrac{F / 9.81}{0.9272 \, d^{2}} = \dfrac{F}{9.81 \times 0.9272 \, d^{2}} = \dfrac{F}{9.098 \, d^{2}}
$$

So the coefficient is:

$$
\dfrac{1}{9.098} \approx 0.1099 \quad \text{when } F \text{ is in N, } d \text{ in mm}
$$

But in practice, the standard textbook coefficient is:

$$
HV = 0.1891 \cdot \dfrac{F_N}{d^{2}}
$$

Let me re-derive using the **face area (not slant area)**. The Vickers hardness uses the **projected contact area** (normal to the load direction), not the slant area:

#### Re-derivation with Projected Area

The contact area on the surface (projected) is the **square of diagonal d**:

$$
A_{proj} = \dfrac{d^{2}}{2}
$$

> Because a square of diagonal d has side $s = d/\sqrt{2}$, so area $= s^{2} = d^{2}/2$.

The pyramid indenter has projected depth $h_{p}$ such that:

$$
\tan(\theta/2) = \dfrac{d/2}{h_{p}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; h_{p} = \dfrac{d/2}{\tan(68°)} = \dfrac{d}{2 \times 2.4751} = \dfrac{d}{4.9502}
$$

The slant surface area:

$$
A_{slant} = 4 \cdot \dfrac{d \cdot \sqrt{(d/2)^{2} + h_{p}^{2}}}{2} = 2d \sqrt{\dfrac{d^{2}}{4} + \dfrac{d^{2}}{24.502}}
$$

#### Final Standard Form (Accepted Convention)

The conventional Vickers formula used universally in lab manuals is:

$$
\boxed{HV = 1.854 \cdot \dfrac{F}{d^{2}}}
$$

where **F is in kgf** and **d is in mm**, giving HV in **kgf/mm²**.

For SI (Newtons), the constant changes:

$$
\boxed{HV = 0.1891 \cdot \dfrac{F}{d^{2}}}
$$

where **F is in N**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of constant 0.1891:**
> $1.854 / 9.81 = 0.18899 \approx 0.1891$ ✓
> This confirms $HV_{SI} = HV_{kgf} / 9.81$.

### 3.2 Derivation of Heat Input (Q) in Arc Welding

The heat input per unit length of weld is the arc energy supplied per millimetre of travel:

$$
Q = \dfrac{\text{Arc Power}}{\text{Travel Speed}} = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{S}
$$

To express in **kJ/mm**, with V in Volts, I in Amperes, and S in mm/min:

$$
Q\,[\text{kJ/mm}] = \dfrac{V\,[\text{V}] \times I\,[\text{A}] \times 60\,[\text{s/min}]}{S\,[\text{mm/min}] \times 1000\,[\text{W/kW}]}
$$

Including the **arc efficiency factor η** (which accounts for losses to spatter, radiation, and convection):

| Process | Efficiency η |
|---------|--------------|
| Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) | **0.75 – 0.85** |
| Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG) | **0.65 – 0.85** |
| Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG) | **0.40 – 0.60** |
| Submerged Arc Welding (SAW) | **0.85 – 0.95** |
| Flux-Cored Arc Welding (FCAW) | **0.75 – 0.85** |

#### Step-by-Step Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A SMAW weld is made with V = 25 V, I = 200 A, travel speed S = 5 mm/s. Calculate the heat input. Take η = 0.80.

**Solution:**

**Step 1** — Convert speed to mm/min:

$$
S = 5 \;\text{mm/s} \times 60 = 300 \;\text{mm/min}
$$

**Step 2** — Compute arc power:

$$
P = V \times I = 25 \times 200 = 5000 \;\text{W} = 5 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 3** — Apply efficiency:

$$
P_{effective} = \eta \cdot P = 0.80 \times 5 = 4 \;\text{kW} = 4 \;\text{kJ/s}
$$

**Step 4** — Heat input per mm:

$$
Q = \dfrac{4 \;\text{kJ/s}}{5 \;\text{mm/s}} = 0.8 \;\text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Alternative using the formula directly:**

$$
Q = \dfrac{0.80 \times 25 \times 200 \times 60}{300 \times 1000} = \dfrac{240000}{300000} = 0.8 \;\text{kJ/mm} \;\checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** For carbon steel, **1 kJ/mm heat input ≈ 1 mm HAZ width** (under standard arc welding conditions, 10–20 mm plate).

### 3.3 Derivation of Cooling Time t₈/₅

The **cooling time between 800 °C and 500 °C (t₈/₅)** is the most critical metallurgical parameter — it directly determines whether **ferrite, bainite, or martensite** forms in the HAZ.

For a thick plate (3D heat flow) under arc welding:

$$
t_{8/5} = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi \lambda}\left(\dfrac{1}{500 - T_{0}} - \dfrac{1}{800 - T_{0}}\right)
$$

where:

- **Q** = heat input [J/mm]
- **λ** = thermal conductivity of steel [W/(mm·K)] ≈ **0.025 – 0.035 W/(mm·K)** for mild steel
- **T₀** = preheat / interpass temperature [°C]

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** A **higher heat input** or **higher preheat temperature (T₀)** → **longer t₈/₅** → coarser, softer microstructures. A **lower heat input** → **shorter t₈/₅** → harder, more brittle martensite.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — HAZ Sub-Zone Correlation

| Sub-Zone | Peak T (°C) | Austenitization | On Cooling Forms | Relative Hardness | Cracking Risk |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| **CGHAZ** | 1100 → Solidus | Complete + Grain growth | Coarse ferrite + Widmanstätten or Martensite | **Highest** (hardenable steel) | **Hydrogen / Cold cracking** |
| **FGHAZ** | Ac3 → 1100 | Complete, fine grains | Fine ferrite + Pearlite | Moderate-High | Low |
| **ICHAZ** | Ac1 → Ac3 | Partial | Mixed (retained ferrite + new phases) | **Lowest** (in HAZ) | **Type IV cracking** |
| **SCHAZ** | < Ac1 | None | Tempered parent structure | Slight softening | Creep (long-term) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Weldment Cross-Section with HAZ Sub-Zones

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Parent Metal BM] --> B[Subcritical HAZ SCHAZ below 723 C]
    B --> C[Intercritical HAZ ICHAZ 723 to 910 C]
    C --> D[Fine Grain HAZ FGHAZ 910 to 1100 C]
    D --> E[Coarse Grain HAZ CGHAZ 1100 to Solidus]
    E --> F[Unmixed Zone UMZ partial melting]
    F --> G[Weld Metal Fusion Zone above Liquidus]
    G --> H[Unmixed Zone UMZ]
    H --> I[Coarse Grain HAZ CGHAZ]
    I --> J[Fine Grain HAZ FGHAZ]
    J --> K[Intercritical HAZ ICHAZ]
    K --> L[Subcritical HAZ SCHAZ]
    L --> M[Parent Metal BM]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram shows the **symmetric mirror-image** of a single-pass weld: the HAZ sub-zones appear on both sides of the central weld pool. Each zone is defined by its peak-temperature band during the thermal cycle.

### 4.2 Hardness Traverse — Typical Profile Across a Mild Steel Weldment

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph LEFT[Parent Metal Left]
        PM1[Parent Metal 150 HV]
    end
    subgraph SCHAZ_L[Subcritical HAZ Left]
        SC1[Softening 140 HV]
    end
    subgraph ICHAZ_L[Intercritical HAZ Left]
        IC1[Lowest 130 HV]
    end
    subgraph FGHAZ_L[Fine Grain HAZ Left]
        FG1[Refined 165 HV]
    end
    subgraph CGHAZ_L[Coarse Grain HAZ Left]
        CG1[Hardest 220 HV]
    end
    subgraph WELD[Center Weld Metal]
        WM1[Weld Bead 200 HV]
    end
    PM1 --- SC1
    SC1 --- IC1
    IC1 --- FG1
    FG1 --- CG1
    CG1 --- WM1
```

### 4.3 Process Flow — Microhardness Testing of HAZ

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 Section Weldment Transverse] --> B[Step 2 Mount in Bakelite]
    B --> C[Step 3 Grind 180 to 1200 grit]
    C --> D[Step 4 Polish 6 to 0.05 micron]
    D --> E[Step 5 Etch with Nital 2 percent]
    E --> F[Step 6 Optical Microscopy Identify Zones]
    F --> G[Step 7 Mark Indent Locations on Traverse Line]
    G --> H[Step 8 Vickers Load 300 g Dwell 15 s]
    H --> I[Step 9 Measure Diagonals d1 and d2]
    I --> J[Step 10 Calculate HV using Formula]
    J --> K[Step 11 Plot Hardness Traverse Curve]
    K --> L[Step 12 Compare with Acceptance Criteria]
```

### 4.4 Microstructural Transformation Map During Welding Thermal Cycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Room Temperature Parent Metal Ferrite plus Pearlite] -->|Heating above Ac1| B[Austenite starts forming in Pearlite]
    B -->|Heating above Ac3| C[Complete Austenite Fine Grains]
    C -->|Heating above 1100 C| D[Austenite Grain Coarsening]
    D -->|Rapid Cooling| E[Martensite or Bainite Formation]
    D -->|Slow Cooling| F[Coarse Ferrite plus Pearlite]
    F --> G[Coarse Grain HAZ Final Structure]
    C -->|Moderate Cooling| H[Fine Ferrite plus Pearlite]
    H --> I[Fine Grain HAZ Final Structure]
    B -->|Cooling from ICHAZ range| J[Mixed Retained Ferrite plus New Phases]
    J --> K[Intercritical HAZ Final Structure]
    A -->|Heating below Ac1| L[Tempering of Pearlite]
    L --> M[Subcritical HAZ Final Structure]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define the Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) in welding. Name any three sub-zones of the HAZ in carbon steel.**

**Model Answer:**

> The Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) is the region of the base metal adjacent to the weld bead whose microstructure and mechanical properties have been altered by the heat of the welding process, but which has **not undergone melting**.
>
> Three sub-zones of the HAZ in carbon steel are:
> 1. **Coarse Grain HAZ (CGHAZ)** — Peak temperature > 1100 °C
> 2. **Fine Grain HAZ (FGHAZ)** — Peak temperature between Ac3 and 1100 °C
> 3. **Intercritical HAZ (ICHAZ)** — Peak temperature between Ac1 and Ac3
>
> *(Self-explanatory — no further sub-parts needed. Full 3 marks.)*

---

#### **Question A2:** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model QP]`
**What is the etchant used for metallographic examination of mild steel? State the Vickers microhardness formula with units.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Etchant for mild steel:** **Nital 2%** — a solution of **2 mL concentrated HNO₃ in 98 mL ethanol (C₂H₅OH)**. It reveals ferrite grain boundaries and pearlite colonies by selective etching.
>
> **Vickers Microhardness Formula:**
>
> $$HV = 1.854 \cdot \dfrac{F}{d^{2}}$$
>
> where **F** is the applied load in **kgf** and **d** is the mean diagonal of the indent in **mm**. HV is reported in **kgf/mm²**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the various sub-zones of the Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) in a low-carbon steel weldment. Mention the peak-temperature range and microstructure formed in each sub-zone.

**(b) [7 Marks]** During a Vickers microhardness test on a welded mild-steel specimen, a load of **300 gf** is applied for 15 seconds. The two diagonals of the indent measured are **d₁ = 42.5 µm** and **d₂ = 43.1 µm**. Calculate the Vickers hardness number and state the zone classification if the measured hardness is **higher than the parent metal**.

---

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

The HAZ in a low-carbon steel weldment is classified into **four sub-zones** based on the peak temperature experienced during the weld thermal cycle:

**Sub-Zone 1 — Coarse Grain HAZ (CGHAZ):**
- **Peak temperature:** Above **1100 °C**, up to the solidus (~1450 °C).
- **Microstructure formed:** Austenite grains grow coarse due to grain-boundary migration at very high temperatures. On rapid cooling, this transforms to **coarse ferrite + Widmanstätten ferrite + pearlite** (or coarse martensite/bainite in hardenable steels).
- **Properties:** Lowest toughness, most susceptible to hydrogen cracking.

**Sub-Zone 2 — Fine Grain HAZ (FGHAZ):**
- **Peak temperature:** Between **Ac3 (~910 °C)** and **1100 °C**.
- **Microstructure formed:** Complete austenitization occurs, but grain growth is restricted. On cooling, **fine equiaxed ferrite + pearlite** forms.
- **Properties:** Optimum combination of strength and toughness.

**Sub-Zone 3 — Intercritical HAZ (ICHAZ):**
- **Peak temperature:** Between **Ac1 (~723 °C)** and **Ac3 (~910 °C)**.
- **Microstructure formed:** Partial transformation — pearlite colonies fully transform to austenite but ferrite remains unchanged. On cooling, austenite reverts to **fine ferrite + carbides**.
- **Properties:** Often the **weakest zone**; susceptible to **Type IV cracking** in creep-resistant steels.

**Sub-Zone 4 — Subcritical HAZ (SCHAZ):**
- **Peak temperature:** Below **Ac1 (~723 °C)**.
- **Microstructure formed:** No phase transformation — only **tempering** of the parent metal structure.
- **Properties:** Slight **softening** compared to unaffected parent metal.

> The schematic must be drawn showing: **PM → SCHAZ → ICHAZ → FGHAZ → CGHAZ → Weld Metal → CGHAZ → FGHAZ → ICHAZ → SCHAZ → PM**, with peak-temperature labels.

---

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given Data:**

$$
F = 300 \;\text{gf} = 0.300 \;\text{kgf}
$$

$$
d_{1} = 42.5 \;\mu\text{m} = 0.0425 \;\text{mm}
$$

$$
d_{2} = 43.1 \;\mu\text{m} = 0.0431 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 1 — Mean Diagonal (d):**

$$
d = \dfrac{d_{1} + d_{2}}{2} = \dfrac{0.0425 + 0.0431}{2} = \dfrac{0.0856}{2} = 0.0428 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Vickers Formula:**

$$
HV = 1.854 \cdot \dfrac{F}{d^{2}} = 1.854 \cdot \dfrac{0.300}{(0.0428)^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute d²:**

$$
d^{2} = (0.0428)^{2} = 0.001832 \;\text{mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and Solve:**

$$
HV = 1.854 \cdot \dfrac{0.300}{0.001832} = 1.854 \cdot 163.76 = 303.6
$$

$$
\boxed{HV \approx 304 \;\text{HV 0.3}}
$$

**Step 5 — Zone Classification:**

Since the measured hardness **HV = 304** is significantly **higher than the parent metal** (typically **150–180 HV** for mild steel), and the indent was taken close to the fusion boundary, the location is classified as the **Coarse Grain HAZ (CGHAZ)** — where rapid cooling of coarse austenite produces harder phases like **fine pearlite / bainite / martensite**.

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the step-by-step procedure for the **metallurgical study** of a welded joint. List the standard reagents, grades of emery paper, and typical magnifications used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **heat input** (in kJ/mm) for a **GMAW (MIG)** weld made with arc voltage **28 V**, current **250 A**, and travel speed **6 mm/s**. Take arc efficiency η = **0.80**. Comment on the **expected HAZ width** and **microhardness trend** in the HAZ.

---

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step-by-step Metallurgical Study Procedure:**

**Step 1 — Sampling & Sectioning:**
A small coupon is cut **transverse** to the weld direction using an abrasive cut-off wheel with continuous coolant flow to avoid heat-induced microstructural changes.

**Step 2 — Mounting:**
The coupon is hot-mounted in **phenolic resin** (~150 °C, 25 MPa) to facilitate handling. Delicate samples may be cold-mounted in epoxy.

**Step 3 — Grinding (Sequential):**
The mounted specimen is ground on a series of **silicon carbide (SiC) abrasive papers** in the following order:

$$
180 \;\text{grit} \;\rightarrow\; 320 \;\text{grit} \;\rightarrow\; 600 \;\text{grit} \;\rightarrow\; 800 \;\text{grit} \;\rightarrow\; 1000 \;\text{grit} \;\rightarrow\; 1200 \;\text{grit}
$$

Water is used as a lubricant. The specimen is rotated **90°** between successive grades until scratches from the previous grade are completely removed.

**Step 4 — Polishing:**
- **Rough polishing:** Diamond paste **6 µm** on a napless cloth.
- **Fine polishing:** Diamond paste **3 µm**, then **1 µm**.
- **Final polishing:** **Colloidal silica (0.05 µm)** on a chemotextile cloth to remove fine scratches.

**Step 5 — Etching:**
The polished surface is swabbed or immersed in the etchant:

- **Nital 2%** (2 mL HNO₃ + 98 mL ethanol) — for **mild / carbon steels** (most common).
- **Picral 4%** — for revealing **carbide networks** in alloy steels.
- **Kalling's reagent** — for **stainless steels**.

Etching time: **5–30 seconds**, followed by immediate rinsing with water and ethanol, then drying with hot air.

**Step 6 — Microscopic Examination:**
The etched specimen is observed under an **inverted metallurgical microscope** at the following magnifications:

| Magnification | Purpose |
|---------------|---------|
| **50× – 100×** | Overview of weld bead, HAZ width measurement |
| **200× – 500×** | Identification of phases (ferrite, pearlite, bainite, martensite) |
| **1000×** (oil immersion) | Fine microstructural features, grain size |

**Step 7 — Micrograph Recording:**
Photomicrographs are captured at standard magnifications for comparison with reference standards (e.g., ASTM E112 for grain size).

---

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given Data:**

$$
V = 28 \;\text{V}, \quad I = 250 \;\text{A}, \quad S = 6 \;\text{mm/s}, \quad \eta = 0.80
$$

**Step 1 — Compute Arc Power:**

$$
P = V \cdot I = 28 \times 250 = 7000 \;\text{W} = 7 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Arc Efficiency:**

$$
P_{effective} = \eta \cdot P = 0.80 \times 7 = 5.6 \;\text{kW} = 5.6 \;\text{kJ/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat Input per mm:**

$$
Q = \dfrac{P_{effective}}{S} = \dfrac{5.6 \;\text{kJ/s}}{6 \;\text{mm/s}} = 0.933 \;\text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Using the direct formula (verification):**

$$
Q = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} = \dfrac{0.80 \times 28 \times 250 \times 60}{6 \times 60 \times 1000}
$$

> Wait — careful: when S is given in mm/s, convert to mm/min first: $S = 6 \times 60 = 360$ mm/min

$$
Q = \dfrac{0.80 \times 28 \times 250 \times 60}{360 \times 1000} = \dfrac{336000}{360000} = 0.933 \;\text{kJ/mm} \;\checkmark
$$

**Step 4 — Expected HAZ Width:**

Using the rule of thumb (**1 kJ/mm ≈ 1 mm HAZ width** for mild steel, 10–20 mm plate):

$$
W_{HAZ} \approx 0.93 \;\text{mm} \;\text{per side}
$$

Total HAZ width on both sides ≈ **1.8 – 2.0 mm**.

**Step 5 — Expected Microhardness Trend in HAZ:**

At **0.933 kJ/mm**, the heat input is **moderate-to-high** for MIG welding. Expected microhardness profile:

| Location | Expected HV | Reason |
|----------|-------------|--------|
| **Parent Metal** | **150–180 HV** | As-rerolled ferrite + pearlite |
| **SCHAZ** | **140–160 HV** | Slight tempering → softening |
| **ICHAZ** | **130–160 HV** | Partial transformation → softest HAZ zone |
| **FGHAZ** | **170–200 HV** | Refined grains → slight strengthening |
| **CGHAZ** | **200–260 HV** | Coarse austenite → harder transformation products |
| **Weld Metal** | **180–230 HV** | Solidified cast structure |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Heat input unit mismatch (–2 marks):** Students often forget to convert **S from mm/s to mm/min** (multiply by 60) or forget to **divide by 1000** to get kJ instead of J. Always show units explicitly.
> 2. **Wrong diagonal in Vickers (–2 marks):** The Vickers formula uses the **mean diagonal d = (d₁+d₂)/2**, not the individual d₁ or d₂. Using only one diagonal is a common error.
> 3. **Forgetting arc efficiency η (–1 mark):** Always multiply by η per the textbook convention; without η, the answer is the *gross* heat input, not the *effective* heat input delivered to the workpiece.
> 4. **Confusing HAZ with Fusion Zone (–3 marks in descriptive):** The HAZ is the **non-melted** zone; the fusion zone / weld metal has crossed the liquidus. Mixing these in Part (a) of descriptive questions is the most common conceptual error.
> 5. **Hardness profile in wrong direction (–2 marks):** Hardness **rises** from parent metal → CGHAZ, **drops** at ICHAZ (local minimum), then rises again at FGHAZ and CGHAZ. Drawing a monotonic curve loses 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — HAZ, Metallurgical Study & Microhardness Test**

- ✅ **HAZ = Non-melted base metal** whose microstructure/properties altered by welding heat (NOT the fusion zone).
- ✅ **Four sub-zones (low-carbon steel):** CGHAZ → FGHAZ → ICHAZ → SCHAZ, arranged from fusion boundary outward.
- ✅ **Ac1 = 723 °C**, **Ac3 ≈ 910 °C**, **Solidus ≈ 1450 °C**, **Liquidus ≈ 1500 °C** for mild steel.
- ✅ **CGHAZ** is the **hardest but most brittle** zone → prone to **hydrogen-induced cold cracking**.
- ✅ **ICHAZ** is often the **softest / weakest** zone → prone to **Type IV cracking** in creep-resistant steels.
- ✅ **Standard etchant for mild steel = Nital 2%** (2 mL HNO₃ + 98 mL ethanol).
- ✅ **Grinding sequence:** 180 → 320 → 600 → 800 → 1000 → 1200 grit SiC paper.
- ✅ **Vickers formula:** $HV = 1.854 \cdot \dfrac{F}{d^{2}}$ (F in kgf, d in mm).
- ✅ **SI form:** $HV = 0.1891 \cdot \dfrac{F}{d^{2}}$ (F in N, d in mm).
- ✅ **Indent spacing rule:** ≥ 2.5 × diagonal length to avoid strain-field overlap.
- ✅ **Microhardness load:** Typically **100–500 g** with **10–15 s** dwell time.
- ✅ **Heat input formula:** $Q = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm (with S in mm/min).
- ✅ **Arc efficiency:** SMAW ≈ 0.80, GMAW ≈ 0.75, GTAW ≈ 0.50, SAW ≈ 0.90.
- ✅ **t₈/₅ cooling time** is the master parameter controlling HAZ microstructure.
- ✅ **Rule of thumb:** 1 kJ/mm heat input → ~1 mm HAZ width (in standard arc welding).
- ✅ **Hardness traverse** shows: **PM → SCHAZ (soft) → ICHAZ (minimum) → FGHAZ (rising) → CGHAZ (peak) → WM**.
- ✅ **Acceptance limit (ASME / NACE):** Typically **≤ 350 HV10** for sour-service welds.
- ✅ **Knoop hardness** is preferred for **thin / brittle layers** (e.g., carburized HAZ surfaces).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formability test of sheet metals using Ericsson Cupping Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL408 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Formability test of sheet metals using Ericsson Cupping Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Erichsen Cupping Test (ECT)** — also colloquially referred to as the *Ericsson Cupping Test* in certain regional syllabi and laboratory manuals — is a standardized **sheet metal formability test** governed by **IS 10175, ISO 8490, ASTM E643, and DIN 50101**. The test quantifies the deep-drawing capability of a sheet metal by forcing a standardized hemispherical punch into a firmly clamped blank until the **first visible crack** appears on the dome. The depth of cup formation at the moment of fracture is termed the **Erichsen Index (IE)**, expressed in **millimetres (mm)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCPEL408):**
> The formability of a sheet metal is its ability to undergo plastic deformation into a desired shape without cracking or excessive thinning. The Erichsen Cupping Test evaluates this property by simulating **biaxial stretching** conditions identical to those encountered in real-world deep-drawing operations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Engineering Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Balloon-in-a-Box Experiment**
> Imagine stretching a piece of kitchen aluminium foil across a wide bowl, then pressing a cricket ball into it from below. If the foil stretches deeply before tearing, the foil is *formable*; if it snaps immediately, it is *brittle*. The Erichsen test is the engineering-precise industrial version of this thought-experiment — replacing the ball with a **20 mm hemispherical punch**, the bowl with a **circular die cavity**, and the foil with a **90 mm × 90 mm specimen of standard thickness**.

The fundamental principle is:

> *"The deeper the punch can penetrate the clamped sheet before cracking, the higher the ductility and formability of the material."*

### 1.3 Salient Features (At a Glance)

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Punch diameter | **20 mm** (hemispherical) |
| Specimen size | **90 mm × 90 mm** (square) |
| Blank-holder force | **10 kN** (typical) |
| Punch descent speed | **5 – 10 mm/min** |
| Erichsen Index range (mild steel) | **8 – 12 mm** |
| Erichsen Index range (stainless steel) | **10 – 14 mm** |
| Erichsen Index range (aluminium) | **6 – 9 mm** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

The Erichsen Cupping Test belongs to the family of **simulative formability tests** that replicate the biaxial stretching experienced by sheet metal during industrial forming. The test generates a **Forming Limit Diagram (FLD)** data point for **equibiaxial tension** at the dome apex.

The test phenomenon is governed by the following sequential logic steps:

- **Step 1 — Clamping:** The flat blank is rigidly clamped between the die and the blank-holder. This simulates the *draw-bead* and *die-corner* constraints of an industrial press.
- **Step 2 — Punch Initiation:** A polished hemispherical punch (20 mm Ø) approaches the blank without contact, establishing the reference zero-depth datum.
- **Step 3 — Biaxial Stretching:** As the punch advances, the central region of the blank experiences **equibiaxial tensile strain**. Material flows from the flange region into the die cavity (similar to deep drawing).
- **Step 4 — Strain Localisation:** Due to **strain hardening (n-value)** and **anisotropy (R-value)** effects, deformation localises at the dome apex.
- **Step 5 — Necking:** At a critical strain, a **diffuse neck** forms, followed by a **localised neck**.
- **Step 6 — Fracture:** The localised neck culminates in a through-thickness crack. The **penetration depth at this instant is the Erichsen Index (IE)**.
- **Step 7 — Visualisation:** The fracture zone is detected using a **dye penetrant** or **magnifying lens** for precise IE measurement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Equibiaxial"?** At the dome apex (pole), the curvature is symmetric in all radial directions. Hence, both principal strains (ε₁ and ε₂) are equal — a state known as **equibiaxial tension**. This is the most severe formability condition for sheet metals.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form the backbone of KTU 2024 numerical questions on this experiment.**

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| IE | Erichsen Index | $IE = h_{f}$ | mm |
| R | Radius of spherical dome | $R = \dfrac{a^{2} + h^{2}}{2h}$ | mm |
| a | Half of dome base diameter | $a = d / 2$ | mm |
| ε₁ | Major (meridional) true strain | $\varepsilon_{1} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{R + h}{R}\right)$ | dimensionless |
| ε₂ | Minor (circumferential) true strain | $\varepsilon_{2} = \varepsilon_{1}$ (at apex) | dimensionless |
| ε₃ | Thickness true strain | $\varepsilon_{3} = -(\varepsilon_{1} + \varepsilon_{2}) = -2\varepsilon_{1}$ | dimensionless |
| t_f | Final thickness at fracture | $t_{f} = t_{0}\,e^{\varepsilon_{3}}$ | mm |
| e₁ | Engineering major strain | $e_{1} = \dfrac{2h}{d}$ (shallow-dome approx.) | dimensionless |
| IE* | Formability rating | $IE^{*} = \dfrac{IE}{t_{0}}$ (depth-to-thickness ratio) | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** The relation $\varepsilon_{1} + \varepsilon_{2} + \varepsilon_{3} = 0$ holds under the **incompressibility / volume constancy** assumption for plastic deformation. Students must explicitly state this assumption before applying it.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Erichsen Index is the **industry-accepted benchmark** for:

- **Material Selection:** Comparing formability of low-carbon steel, stainless steel, aluminium alloys, and brass for press-shop applications.
- **Process Control:** Validating incoming sheet stock against OEM specifications in automotive and white-goods industries.
- **Heat-Treatment Verification:** Quantifying the effect of annealing, temper rolling, and recrystallisation on ductility.
- **Coating Evaluation:** Comparing formability of galvannealed, galvanised, and bare sheet metals.
- **Lubricant Performance:** Assessing the effectiveness of deep-drawing lubricants (graphite, MoS₂, PTFE-based).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Radius of Curvature (R) for a Spherical Cap

Consider a sheet metal dome formed into a spherical cap of height **h** and base radius **a** (= d/2, where d is the measured dome diameter at fracture).

From the Pythagorean theorem applied to the spherical-cap geometry:

$$
R^{2} = a^{2} + (R - h)^{2}
$$

Expanding the squared term:

$$
R^{2} = a^{2} + R^{2} - 2Rh + h^{2}
$$

Cancelling $R^{2}$ from both sides:

$$
0 = a^{2} - 2Rh + h^{2}
$$

Solving for **R**:

$$
\boxed{R = \dfrac{a^{2} + h^{2}}{2h}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **fundamental geometric relation** linking dome height, base radius, and curvature radius. It is the first step in any Erichsen test numerical analysis.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Major (Meridional) True Strain at the Dome Apex

For a thin sheet, the meridional arc length from the pole to the dome edge is:

$$
s = R\,\theta \quad \text{where} \quad \theta = \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{a}{R}\right)
$$

In the original (undeformed) flat blank, the material at the pole had a meridional length equal to **R** (the radius of curvature at the pole). Hence, the engineering meridional strain is:

$$
e_{1} = \dfrac{s - R}{R} = \theta - 1
$$

For small deformations, using the series expansion of $\arcsin(x) \approx x + x^{3}/6$, the engineering strain simplifies to:

$$
e_{1} \approx \dfrac{h}{R} \quad \text{(shallow-dome approximation)}
$$

Converting engineering strain to **true strain** using $\varepsilon = \ln(1 + e)$:

$$
\varepsilon_{1} = \ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{h}{R}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{R + h}{R}\right)
$$

Substituting the value of **R**:

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_{1} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{a^{2} + h^{2} + 2h^{2}}{a^{2} + h^{2}}\right) = \ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{2h^{2}}{a^{2} + h^{2}}\right)}
$$

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Examination Style)

**Problem Statement (Model Question):**

> A mild steel sheet specimen of dimensions **90 mm × 90 mm × 1.0 mm** is subjected to the Erichsen Cupping Test. The punch penetration is stopped at the first appearance of a visible crack on the dome. The following readings are recorded:
>
> - Cup depth at fracture: **h = 10.2 mm**
> - Dome diameter at fracture: **d = 25.4 mm**
> - Original blank thickness: **t₀ = 1.0 mm**
>
> Calculate:
> 1. The Erichsen Index (IE)
> 2. The radius of curvature of the dome (R)
> 3. The major and minor principal strains at the apex
> 4. The final thickness at the fracture zone
> 5. The formability rating $IE^{*}$

**Complete Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Erichsen Index (IE):**

By definition, the Erichsen Index is the cup depth at the instant of first crack:

$$
IE = h_{f} = 10.2 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Radius of Curvature (R):**

First, compute the base half-radius:

$$
a = \dfrac{d}{2} = \dfrac{25.4}{2} = 12.7 \text{ mm}
$$

Apply the spherical-cap formula:

$$
R = \dfrac{a^{2} + h^{2}}{2h} = \dfrac{(12.7)^{2} + (10.2)^{2}}{2 \times 10.2}
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{161.29 + 104.04}{20.4} = \dfrac{265.33}{20.4} = 13.006 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Major True Strain (ε₁) at the Dome Apex:**

$$
\varepsilon_{1} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{R + h}{R}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{13.006 + 10.2}{13.006}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{23.206}{13.006}\right)
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{1} = \ln(1.7842) = 0.5790
$$

**Step 4 — Minor True Strain (ε₂) at the Dome Apex:**

At the dome apex, the deformation is **equibiaxial**:

$$
\varepsilon_{2} = \varepsilon_{1} = 0.5790
$$

**Step 5 — Thickness Strain (ε₃) and Final Thickness:**

By the **incompressibility** condition for plastic deformation:

$$
\varepsilon_{1} + \varepsilon_{2} + \varepsilon_{3} = 0
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{3} = -(\varepsilon_{1} + \varepsilon_{2}) = -2 \times 0.5790 = -1.158
$$

Final thickness:

$$
t_{f} = t_{0}\,e^{\varepsilon_{3}} = 1.0 \times e^{-1.158} = 1.0 \times 0.3141 = 0.314 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Formability Rating (IE\*):**

$$
IE^{*} = \dfrac{IE}{t_{0}} = \dfrac{10.2}{1.0} = 10.2
$$

**Result Tabulation:**

| Quantity | Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| IE | 10.2 mm | Excellent formability for 1 mm mild steel |
| R | 13.006 mm | Dome is shallow (R > h) |
| ε₁ | 0.579 | High major strain |
| ε₂ | 0.579 | Equibiaxial strain state |
| ε₃ | −1.158 | Severe thinning at apex |
| t_f | 0.314 mm | 68.6 % thickness reduction |
| IE\* | 10.2 | Indicates good deep-drawing grade |

> [!NOTE]
> **Material Classification (Industry Standard):** An $IE^{*}$ value of **8 – 12** is typical of **deep-drawing quality (DDQ)** mild steel, while values above **12** indicate **extra-deep-drawing (EDDQ)** grades used in automotive panels.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Erichsen Cupping Test Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Specimen Preparation] --> B[Step 2: Surface Cleaning and Lubrication]
    B --> C[Step 3: Specimen Placement on Die Cavity]
    C --> D[Step 4: Blank-Holder Clamping 10 kN]
    D --> E[Step 5: Punch Calibration and Zero-Setting]
    E --> F[Step 6: Hemispherical Punch Descent 5 mm per min]
    F --> G{Crack Detected via Dye Penetrant}
    G -- No Crack --> F
    G -- Yes Crack --> H[Step 7: Immediate Punch Stoppage]
    H --> I[Step 8: Measurement of Cup Depth h_f]
    I --> J[Step 9: Measurement of Dome Diameter d]
    J --> K[Step 10: Calculation of Erichsen Index IE]
    K --> L[Step 11: Calculation of Major and Minor Strains]
    L --> M[Step 12: Final Thickness Measurement and Result Documentation]
    M --> N[End: Formability Report Generation]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of the Erichsen Testing Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DriveSystem [Drive and Control Subsystem]
        M1[AC Servo Motor]
        M2[Lead Screw Assembly]
        M3[Linear Encoder for Depth Measurement]
    end

    subgraph ToolingAssembly [Tooling Assembly]
        T1[20 mm Hemispherical Punch]
        T2[Blank Holder with Clamping Bolts]
        T3[Die with 27 mm Cavity Diameter]
    end

    subgraph DetectionSubsystem [Crack Detection Subsystem]
        D1[10X Magnifying Lens]
        D2[Dye Penetrant Applicator]
        D3[Crack Detection Indicator Light]
    end

    M1 --> M2 --> T1
    T1 --> Spec[Sheet Metal Specimen]
    Spec --> T2
    Spec --> T3
    T1 --> M3
    T1 --> D1
    D2 --> Spec
    D1 --> D3
    D3 --> StopSignal[Emergency Punch Stoppage Signal]
    StopSignal --> M1
```

### 4.3 Schematic of the Specimen Geometry and Strain State

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Flat Specimen 90 x 90 x t0] --> B[Initial State - Zero Strain]
    B --> C[Punch Contacts Center of Blank]
    C --> D[Biaxial Stretching Initiated at Pole]
    D --> E[Strain Hardening Increases Local Strength]
    E --> F[Necking Localised at Dome Apex]
    F --> G[Through-Thickness Crack Forms]
    G --> H[Final Dome: Height h_f and Diameter d]
    H --> I[Erichsen Index IE Equals h_f]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *Define the term "Erichsen Index". What does it signify about the sheet metal?*

**Model Answer:**

The **Erichsen Index (IE)** is defined as the **depth of cup formation (in mm) at the instant the first visible crack appears on the dome** during a standard Erichsen Cupping Test conducted as per IS 10175 / ISO 8490.

It signifies the **formability** (or deep-drawing capability) of a sheet metal under biaxial stretching conditions. A **higher IE value** indicates **superior ductility and formability**, making the material suitable for deep-drawing operations such as automobile body panels, kitchen sinks, and appliance housings. Conversely, a **lower IE value** indicates **brittle behaviour** and limited formability. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *Name any two international standards that govern the Erichsen Cupping Test. State the standard punch diameter and specimen size used in the test.*

**Model Answer:**

Two international standards governing the Erichsen Cupping Test are:

1. **IS 10175 : 2001** (Indian Standard)
2. **ISO 8490 : 1986** (International Standard)

Other equivalent standards include **ASTM E643** and **DIN 50101**.

- **Standard punch diameter:** **20 mm** (hemispherical, polished)
- **Standard specimen size:** **90 mm × 90 mm** (square blank of uniform thickness)

**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a schematic diagram, explain the **principle, apparatus, and procedure** of the Erichsen Cupping Test. State the role of the blank-holder. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 1.5 mm thick aluminium alloy specimen is tested in an Erichsen machine. The cup depth at the first crack is **9.8 mm** and the dome diameter is **27 mm**. Determine the **Erichsen Index**, the **major and minor principal strains at the dome apex**, and the **final thickness** at the fracture zone. Comment on the formability. **(7 marks)**

### 5.3 Model Solutions for Question A

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

**Principle:**
The Erichsen Cupping Test is based on the principle of **biaxial stretching** of a clamped sheet metal blank by a hemispherical punch. The depth of penetration at which fracture occurs is a direct measure of the sheet's formability. **(1 mark)**

**Apparatus (refer to Section 4.2 block diagram):**
- Erichsen testing machine with a **20 mm hemispherical punch**
- **Die** with a circular cavity of approximately 27 mm diameter
- **Blank-holder** with clamping bolts (typical force 10 kN)
- **Depth gauge** or linear encoder for measuring cup depth
- **Magnifying lens (10X)** and dye penetrant for crack detection
- **Lubricant** (graphite grease) for reducing friction **(2 marks)**

**Procedure (refer to Section 4.1 flowchart):**
1. Cut a 90 mm × 90 mm specimen; clean and lubricate the contacting surface.
2. Clamp the specimen firmly between the die and the blank-holder.
3. Zero the depth gauge with the punch just touching the specimen.
4. Advance the punch at 5–10 mm/min.
5. Continuously monitor the dome surface for the first visible crack.
6. Stop the punch immediately on crack detection.
7. Record the cup depth (h_f) and dome diameter (d).
8. The Erichsen Index IE = h_f. **(3 marks)**

**Role of the Blank-Holder:**
The blank-holder provides a **uniform clamping force** to prevent **wrinkling** of the flange region during punch descent. It also controls the **material inflow** into the die cavity, ensuring that deformation is concentrated as **biaxial stretching** at the dome apex rather than as **drawing-in** from the flange. Without adequate blank-holder force, the specimen wrinkles and produces invalid results. **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Original thickness: t₀ = 1.5 mm
- Cup depth at fracture: h = 9.8 mm
- Dome diameter: d = 27 mm

**Step 1 — Erichsen Index:**

$$
IE = h_{f} = 9.8 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Dome Geometry:**

$$
a = \dfrac{d}{2} = \dfrac{27}{2} = 13.5 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{a^{2} + h^{2}}{2h} = \dfrac{(13.5)^{2} + (9.8)^{2}}{2 \times 9.8} = \dfrac{182.25 + 96.04}{19.6} = \dfrac{278.29}{19.6} = 14.20 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Major True Strain (ε₁) at Apex:**

$$
\varepsilon_{1} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{R + h}{R}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{14.20 + 9.8}{14.20}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{24.00}{14.20}\right) = \ln(1.6901) = 0.5247
$$

**Step 4 — Minor True Strain (ε₂) at Apex:**

$$
\varepsilon_{2} = \varepsilon_{1} = 0.5247 \quad \text{(equibiaxial state at apex)}
$$

**Step 5 — Thickness Strain and Final Thickness:**

$$
\varepsilon_{3} = -(\varepsilon_{1} + \varepsilon_{2}) = -2 \times 0.5247 = -1.0494
$$

$$
t_{f} = t_{0}\,e^{\varepsilon_{3}} = 1.5 \times e^{-1.0494} = 1.5 \times 0.3501 = 0.525 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Formability Comment:**

The formability rating $IE^{*}$ is:

$$
IE^{*} = \dfrac{IE}{t_{0}} = \dfrac{9.8}{1.5} = 6.53
$$

This value falls in the range **6 – 9**, which is typical of **aluminium alloys** (refer Section 1.3). The material exhibits **moderate formability** suitable for shallow-drawing applications. The thickness reduction of approximately 65 % indicates significant localised thinning prior to fracture. **(7 marks)**

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **constructional features** of a standard Erichsen cupping test machine with a neat block diagram. List the standards applicable to this test. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **factors affecting the Erichsen Cupping Test results**. Explain how the IE value guides **material selection for deep-drawing applications** in the automotive industry. **(7 marks)**

### 5.4 Model Solutions for Question B

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

**Constructional Features (refer to Section 4.2 block diagram):**

1. **Frame and Bed:** A robust cast-iron frame houses the entire machine, providing rigidity and vibration-free operation. **(1 mark)**
2. **Punch Assembly:** A **20 mm diameter hemispherical punch** made of hardened, polished tool steel (HRC 60+). The punch is mounted on a precision lead screw driven by an AC servo motor. **(1 mark)**
3. **Die Assembly:** A die block with a **circular cavity of 27 mm diameter** and a 60° spherical seat. The die surface is also hardened and polished. **(1 mark)**
4. **Blank-Holder:** A flat ring that clamps the specimen against the die with a **force of 10 kN** applied through hydraulic or mechanical means. **(1 mark)**
5. **Depth Measurement System:** A **linear encoder** (or dial gauge) with **0.01 mm resolution** measures the punch penetration in real-time. **(1 mark)**
6. **Crack Detection System:** A **10X magnifying lens** combined with a **dye penetrant** (typically a red or fluorescent dye) is used to identify the first crack. Some modern machines have automated image-based crack detection. **(1 mark)**
7. **Control Panel:** Houses the on/off switch, speed selector, emergency stop, and digital readout. **(1 mark)**

**Applicable Standards (4 major):**

- **IS 10175 : 2001** — Indian Standard
- **ISO 8490 : 1986** — International Organisation for Standardisation
- **ASTM E643** — American Society for Testing and Materials
- **DIN 50101** — German Industrial Standard

**(7 marks total)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

**Factors Affecting the Erichsen Cupping Test Results:**

1. **Specimen Thickness:** Thicker sheets generally exhibit higher IE values in mm but lower $IE^{*}$ (depth-to-thickness) ratios. Results should be compared at **constant thickness**. **(1 mark)**
2. **Lubrication Condition:** Inadequate or non-uniform lubrication artificially lowers the IE value by promoting premature tearing at the punch-specimen interface. **(1 mark)**
3. **Blank-Holder Force:** Excessive force restricts material flow and causes early fracture; insufficient force causes wrinkling. Both produce invalid results. **(1 mark)**
4. **Punch Speed:** High punch speeds can cause adiabatic heating, altering the strain-hardening behaviour. Standard tests use 5–10 mm/min. **(1 mark)**
5. **Surface Finish and Defects:** Scratches, oxidation, or rolling marks on the specimen surface act as crack initiators, reducing the measured IE. **(1 mark)**
6. **Grain Size and Anisotropy:** Fine-grained, isotropic materials exhibit higher IE values. The **R-value (normal anisotropy)** strongly influences deep-drawing performance. **(1 mark)**
7. **Testing Temperature:** Elevated temperatures increase ductility and IE; sub-zero temperatures reduce them. Tests are standardised at **23 ± 5 °C**. **(1 mark)**

**Material Selection for Deep-Drawing Applications:**

The IE value serves as a **primary screening parameter** in the automotive industry for selecting sheet metals for body panels, fuel tanks, and structural components:

- **IE < 6 mm:** Suitable only for simple bending and shallow forming (e.g., brackets, covers).
- **IE 6 – 9 mm:** Suitable for moderate drawing (e.g., aluminium alloys for bonnets, doors).
- **IE 8 – 12 mm:** Deep-drawing quality (DDQ) steel — used for fenders, floor pans, and oil pans.
- **IE > 12 mm:** Extra-deep-drawing quality (EDDQ) steel — used for complex automotive stampings such as rear quarter panels and dashboard supports.

Leading automotive OEMs (e.g., Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki) specify minimum IE values of **10 mm** for critical safety and Class-A surface panels. **(7 marks total)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in Erichsen Test Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the incompressibility assumption** before applying $\varepsilon_{1} + \varepsilon_{2} + \varepsilon_{3} = 0$. Examiner deducts **1 mark**.
> 2. **Using the wrong formula for R.** Students often write $R = a^{2}/(2h)$, forgetting the $h^{2}$ term. This gives incorrect strain values. Deduct **2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing engineering and true strains.** The question typically asks for **true strain**; using $e = 2h/d$ directly without the logarithmic conversion results in **2-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Not specifying the test standard.** The examiner expects at least one standard code (IS 10175, ISO 8490, or ASTM E643). Deduct **1 mark** if omitted.
> 5. **Misinterpreting equibiaxial state.** At the dome apex, $\varepsilon_{2} = \varepsilon_{1}$, **not** $\varepsilon_{2} = 0$. This is a frequent conceptual error costing **2 marks**.
> 6. **Unit errors.** Final thickness must be in **mm**, strain in **dimensionless** (or stated as a ratio). Deduct **0.5 mark** per unit inconsistency.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- **Erichsen Cupping Test** = standardised biaxial stretching test for sheet metal formability.
- **Erichsen Index (IE)** = cup depth (h_f) at first visible crack, measured in **mm**.
- **Higher IE ⇒ better formability** (qualitative relationship).
- **Standard punch diameter** = **20 mm** (hemispherical, polished tool steel).
- **Standard specimen** = **90 mm × 90 mm** square blank, with uniform thickness.
- **Blank-holder force** = **10 kN** (typical) — prevents wrinkling, controls material inflow.
- **Punch speed** = **5 – 10 mm/min** (to avoid adiabatic heating effects).
- **Lubricant** = graphite grease or PTFE-based — to minimise friction at the punch-blank interface.
- **Standards applicable:** IS 10175, ISO 8490, ASTM E643, DIN 50101.
- **Dome curvature radius:** $R = (a^{2} + h^{2})/(2h)$ — fundamental geometric relation.
- **Major strain at apex:** $\varepsilon_{1} = \ln((R + h)/R)$ — true (logarithmic) strain.
- **Equibiaxial state at apex:** $\varepsilon_{1} = \varepsilon_{2}$ — both principal strains are equal in magnitude.
- **Volume constancy (incompressibility):** $\varepsilon_{1} + \varepsilon_{2} + \varepsilon_{3} = 0$ — required for deriving thickness strain.
- **Formability rating:** $IE^{*} = IE / t_{0}$ — used for comparing materials at normalised thickness.
- **Mild steel IE range:** 8 – 12 mm; **Aluminium:** 6 – 9 mm; **Stainless steel:** 10 – 14 mm.
- **Engineering strain approximation (shallow domes):** $e_{1} \approx 2h/d$ — useful for quick calculations only.
- **Strain state at apex is equibiaxial tension** — the most severe formability condition.
- **Test detects formability, not strength** — high IE does not imply high tensile strength.
- **Crack detection methods:** dye penetrant, 10X magnifying lens, automated image processing.
- **Industry application:** material selection for deep-drawing in automotive, white-goods, and packaging industries.
- **KDU 2024 ESE Hot Topics:** numerical problems on ε₁, ε₂, ε₃ calculation; comparison of formability between materials; standards listing; and role of blank-holder.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deformation Analysis of sheet metals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL408 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Deformation Analysis of sheet metals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sheet Metal Deformation Analysis** is the experimental and analytical study of how thin metallic sheets (typical thickness $\le 6\,\text{mm}$) respond to applied forming forces, characterized by measuring key mechanical properties such as **yield strength ($\sigma_y$)**, **ultimate tensile strength (UTS)**, **percent elongation**, **strain-hardening exponent ($n$)**, **strength coefficient ($K$)**, **plastic strain ratio ($r$-value)**, and **normal anisotropy ($\bar{r}$)** under uniaxial, biaxial, and plane-strain loading conditions.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Production Engineering Lab (PCPEL408), this experiment typically involves performing **uniaxial tensile tests** on sheet metal specimens cut along three orientations (0°, 45°, 90°) relative to the rolling direction. The data is then processed to determine the **Lankford coefficient (R-value)**, the **strain-hardening exponent ($n$)**, and the **Strength Coefficient ($K$)** using the **Hollomon power-law equation**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Stretching a piece of chewing gum vs. stretching a piece of brittle paper**

Imagine you are stretching a thin piece of **chewing gum** between your hands. As you pull, it does not snap immediately — it elongates smoothly, thins down, and becomes harder to stretch the more you pull it (this is *strain hardening*). Now, if you pull a piece of **butter paper**, it stretches very little before tearing, and the tear line may run straight across or at an angle depending on the fibers in the paper (this is *anisotropy*).

In sheet metal forming (automobile body panels, tin cans, aircraft skins), engineers MUST know:
