- **How much** the metal can stretch before it cracks (formability).
- **In which direction** it stretches most easily (anisotropy).
- **How strongly** it resists further deformation after yielding (strain hardening).

This is exactly what **Deformation Analysis of sheet metals** quantifies.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Engineering Stress ($\sigma_e$):** $\sigma_e = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ where $A_0$ is the **original cross-sectional area**.
- **True Stress ($\sigma_t$):** $\sigma_t = \dfrac{F}{A_i}$ where $A_i$ is the **instantaneous area**.
- **Engineering Strain ($\varepsilon_e$):** $\varepsilon_e = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0}$.
- **True Strain ($\varepsilon_t$):** $\varepsilon_t = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{L_f}{L_0}\right) = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_e)$.
- **Strain-Hardening Exponent ($n$):** Dimensionless, typically **0.05 to 0.50** for sheet metals.
- **Strength Coefficient ($K$):** Has units of **MPa**, typically **300 to 1500 MPa**.
- **Normal Anisotropy ($\bar{r}$):** Dimensionless, ideally **$\geq 1$** for good deep-drawing.
- **Planar Anisotropy ($\Delta r$):** Dimensionless, ideally **$\approx 0$** for uniform drawing.

## 1.4 Standard Testing Standards

> [!NOTE]
> Sheet metal deformation analysis is governed by international standards, including:
> - **ASTM E8 / E8M** — Standard Test Methods for Tension Testing of Metallic Materials.
> - **ASTM E517** — Standard Test Method for Plastic Strain Ratio $r$ for Sheet Metal.
> - **ISO 6892-1** — Metallic materials — Tensile testing at room temperature.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Stress–Strain Curve in Sheet Metals

A typical engineering stress–strain ($\sigma_e$ vs. $\varepsilon_e$) curve for a ductile sheet metal exhibits the following regions:

1. **Elastic Region (Linear):** Hooke's Law governs: $\sigma = E\,\varepsilon$. Strain is fully recoverable.
2. **Yield Point ($\sigma_y$):** Onset of permanent plastic deformation. For low-carbon steels, an upper and lower yield point are observed.
3. **Strain-Hardening (Plastic) Region:** Stress increases with strain due to dislocation multiplication and entanglement.
4. **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS, $\sigma_u$):** Maximum engineering stress the sheet can sustain.
5. **Necking Region:** Localized reduction in cross-section begins; engineering stress falls, but **true stress continues to rise**.
6. **Fracture Point:** The specimen finally breaks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For sheet metal analysis, the TRUE stress–strain curve in the plastic region is more meaningful than the engineering curve**, because sheet forming involves large plastic strains (often 20%–60%) where the assumption of constant area becomes invalid.

## 2.2 The Hollomon Power Law (Work-Hardening Equation)

The most widely used constitutive model for sheet metal plastic deformation in the uniform strain region is:

$$
\sigma_t = K\,\varepsilon_t^{\,n}
$$

where:
- $\sigma_t$ = true stress (MPa)
- $\varepsilon_t$ = true strain
- $K$ = **strength coefficient** (MPa)
- $n$ = **strain-hardening exponent** (dimensionless)

### Physical Meaning of $n$:
- A **high $n$** (e.g., 0.30–0.50) → sheet resists local necking → **excellent formability** (used for deep-drawn parts).
- A **low $n$** (e.g., 0.05–0.10) → strain localizes quickly → **poor formability** (e.g., stainless steel).

## 2.3 Plastic Strain Ratio ($r$-value / Lankford Coefficient)

The $r$-value measures the **resistance of the sheet to thinning** under uniaxial tension:

$$
r = \dfrac{\varepsilon_w}{\varepsilon_t}
$$

where:
- $\varepsilon_w = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{w_0}{w_f}\right)$ = true width strain
- $\varepsilon_t = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{t_0}{t_f}\right)$ = true thickness strain
- Subscripts $0$ and $f$ denote original and final values.

### Engineering Interpretation:
- **$r > 1$** → sheet thins LESS in thickness than it widens → **good drawability** (deep-drawing).
- **$r < 1$** → sheet thins more in thickness → **poor drawability**.
- **$r = 1$** → isotropic (volume conservation implies $\varepsilon_w = \varepsilon_t$).

> [!NOTE]
> **The $r$-value is orientation-dependent** because cold-rolled sheets have a crystallographic texture. Therefore, we measure it in three directions: **0° (rolling direction, RD)**, **45°**, and **90° (transverse direction, TD)**.

## 2.4 Normal Anisotropy ($\bar{r}$)

The average $r$-value, weighted by the angles at which it is measured:

$$
\bar{r} = \dfrac{r_{0} + 2\,r_{45} + r_{90}}{4}
$$

- High $\bar{r}$ → sheet resists thinning → **deeper cups can be drawn without fracture** (e.g., aluminum-killed deep-drawing steel typically has $\bar{r} = 1.8$ to $2.2$).

## 2.5 Planar Anisotropy ($\Delta r$)

$$
\Delta r = \dfrac{r_{0} - 2\,r_{45} + r_{90}}{2}
$$

- If $\Delta r = 0$ → uniform drawability in all directions → **no earing** during deep-drawing.
- If $\Delta r \neq 0$ → earing occurs (4 ears at $\Delta r > 0$, 8 ears at special cases).

## 2.6 Volume Constancy in Plastic Deformation

For incompressible plastic flow of sheet metal:

$$
\varepsilon_L + \varepsilon_W + \varepsilon_t = 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In plane-strain (e.g., rolling), the width strain $\varepsilon_W = 0$, so all deformation is split between length and thickness.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---------|-------------|-----------------------|
| 1 | $\sigma_e = F/A_0$ | Engineering stress | MPa |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_e = \Delta L/L_0$ | Engineering strain | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\sigma_t = \sigma_e(1 + \varepsilon_e)$ | True stress from engineering stress | MPa |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_t = \ln(1+\varepsilon_e)$ | True strain from engineering strain | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\sigma_t = K\,\varepsilon_t^{\,n}$ | Hollomon power law | $K$ in MPa, $n$ dimensionless |
| 6 | $n = \dfrac{\ln(\sigma_{t2}/\sigma_{t1})}{\ln(\varepsilon_{t2}/\varepsilon_{t1})}$ | $n$-value from two true-stress–strain points | dimensionless |
| 7 | $r = \varepsilon_w / \varepsilon_t$ | Lankford / plastic strain ratio | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\bar{r} = (r_0 + 2r_{45} + r_{90})/4$ | Normal anisotropy | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\Delta r = (r_0 - 2r_{45} + r_{90})/2$ | Planar anisotropy | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_L + \varepsilon_w + \varepsilon_t = 0$ | Volume constancy | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\%\,\text{El} = (L_f - L_0)/L_0 \times 100$ | Percent elongation | % |
| 12 | $UTS = F_{\max}/A_0$ | Ultimate tensile strength | MPa |
| 13 | $E = \sigma/\varepsilon$ (elastic region) | Young's modulus | GPa (Steel $\approx 200$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always convert engineering stress/strain to TRUE stress/strain using formulas (3) and (4) before computing $n$ and $K$. This is where most students lose marks.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Steel sheets for car body panels are graded primarily by $\bar{r}$ and $n$ values.
- **Aerospace:** Aluminum alloy sheets (e.g., AA2024, AA7075) are characterized by their $n$-value for stretch-forming.
- **Packaging:** Tin-plate and aluminum sheets for beverage cans require $\bar{r} \ge 1.6$ for multi-stage deep-drawing.
- **Appliances:** Washing-machine drums, refrigerator shells — all require formability data from this analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion from Engineering to True Stress

**Given:** A tensile specimen with original cross-section $A_0$ and instantaneous area $A_i$, being loaded by force $F$.

**Step 1 — Definition of engineering stress:**
$$
\sigma_e = \frac{F}{A_0}
$$

**Step 2 — Definition of true stress (using instantaneous area):**
$$
\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_i}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the principle of volume constancy in plastic deformation:**
$$
A_0 \cdot L_0 = A_i \cdot L_i
$$

Solving for $A_i$:
$$
A_i = \frac{A_0 \cdot L_0}{L_i}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $A_i$ into the true stress equation:**
$$
\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_0 \cdot L_0 / L_i} = \frac{F \cdot L_i}{A_0 \cdot L_0}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply and divide numerator and denominator by $L_0$:**
$$
\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_0} \cdot \frac{L_i}{L_0}
$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the two terms:**
- $\dfrac{F}{A_0} = \sigma_e$
- $\dfrac{L_i}{L_0} = 1 + \varepsilon_e$ (since $\varepsilon_e = (L_i - L_0)/L_0$)

**Step 7 — Final result:**
$$
\sigma_t = \sigma_e \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_e)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This relation is valid **only up to the point of necking** (uniform plastic deformation region).

## 3.2 Derivation: Conversion from Engineering to True Strain

**Given:** Engineering strain $\varepsilon_e = (L_f - L_0)/L_0$.

**Step 1 — Express the differential of true strain:**
$$
d\varepsilon_t = \frac{dL}{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from initial length $L_0$ to final length $L_f$:**
$$
\varepsilon_t = \int_{L_0}^{L_f} \frac{dL}{L} = \left[ \ln L \right]_{L_0}^{L_f}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the limits:**
$$
\varepsilon_t = \ln L_f - \ln L_0 = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of engineering strain:**
Since $\dfrac{L_f}{L_0} = 1 + \varepsilon_e$:
$$
\varepsilon_t = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_e)
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Strain-Hardening Exponent $n$ from Hollomon's Law

**Given:** $\sigma_t = K\,\varepsilon_t^{\,n}$, with two true-stress–strain data points $(\varepsilon_{t1}, \sigma_{t1})$ and $(\varepsilon_{t2}, \sigma_{t2})$ in the uniform plastic region.

**Step 1 — Apply the Hollomon equation at both points:**
$$
\sigma_{t1} = K\,\varepsilon_{t1}^{\,n}
$$
$$
\sigma_{t2} = K\,\varepsilon_{t2}^{\,n}
$$

**Step 2 — Take the ratio (this eliminates $K$):**
$$
\frac{\sigma_{t1}}{\sigma_{t2}} = \left(\frac{\varepsilon_{t1}}{\varepsilon_{t2}}\right)^{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithm on both sides:**
$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{\sigma_{t1}}{\sigma_{t2}}\right) = n \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon_{t1}}{\varepsilon_{t2}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $n$:**
$$
n = \frac{\ln(\sigma_{t1}/\sigma_{t2})}{\ln(\varepsilon_{t1}/\varepsilon_{t2})}
$$

**Step 5 — Equivalently, from a log-log plot:**
If we plot $\log \sigma_t$ vs. $\log \varepsilon_t$, the **slope of the straight line = $n$**, and the **y-intercept = $\log K$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In the KTU lab exam, this is the most common derivation asked.** Make sure you can reproduce it from memory.

## 3.4 Derivation: $r$-Value from Tensile Test Data

**Given:** A tensile specimen with original width $w_0$, original thickness $t_0$, final width $w_f$, and final thickness $t_f$.

**Step 1 — True width strain:**
$$
\varepsilon_w = \ln\!\left(\frac{w_f}{w_0}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — True thickness strain:**
$$
\varepsilon_t = \ln\!\left(\frac{t_f}{t_0}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Definition of $r$:**
$$
r = \frac{\varepsilon_w}{\varepsilon_t}
$$

**Step 4 — Using volume constancy** $\varepsilon_L + \varepsilon_w + \varepsilon_t = 0$, we can also write:
$$
\varepsilon_t = -(\varepsilon_L + \varepsilon_w)
$$

So the $r$-value can be obtained by measuring only length and width change:
$$
r = \frac{\ln(w_0/w_f)}{\ln(w_0 \cdot L_0 / (w_f \cdot L_f))}
$$

(This is the practical form used in ASTM E517.)

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Problem)

**Problem:** A cold-rolled steel sheet tensile specimen (initial gauge length $L_0 = 50\,\text{mm}$, initial width $w_0 = 12.5\,\text{mm}$, initial thickness $t_0 = 1.0\,\text{mm}$) is pulled along the rolling direction. At a load of $F = 9.5\,\text{kN}$, the gauge length becomes $L_1 = 60\,\text{mm}$, width becomes $w_1 = 11.8\,\text{mm}$. Determine: (a) engineering and true stress, (b) engineering and true strain, (c) $r$-value, assuming $\varepsilon_t = -(\varepsilon_L + \varepsilon_w)$.

### Solution:

**Part (a) — Engineering & True Stress:**

Engineering stress:
$$
\sigma_e = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{9500\,\text{N}}{(12.5 \times 1.0)\,\text{mm}^2} = 760\,\text{MPa}
$$

Engineering strain:
$$
\varepsilon_e = \frac{60 - 50}{50} = 0.20
$$

True stress:
$$
\sigma_t = \sigma_e (1 + \varepsilon_e) = 760 \times 1.20 = 912\,\text{MPa}
$$

**Part (b) — True Strain:**

True strain:
$$
\varepsilon_t = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_e) = \ln(1.20) = 0.1823
$$

**Part (c) — $r$-value:**

Width strain:
$$
\varepsilon_w = \ln\!\left(\frac{11.8}{12.5}\right) = \ln(0.944) = -0.0577
$$

Length strain:
$$
\varepsilon_L = \ln\!\left(\frac{60}{50}\right) = \ln(1.20) = 0.1823
$$

Thickness strain (volume constancy):
$$
\varepsilon_t = -(\varepsilon_L + \varepsilon_w) = -(0.1823 - 0.0577) = -0.1246
$$

$r$-value:
$$
r = \frac{\varepsilon_w}{\varepsilon_t} = \frac{-0.0577}{-0.1246} = 0.463
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Since $r < 1$, this sheet has **poor drawability** in the rolling direction (typical of a low-carbon rimming steel). For deep-drawing, we need $\bar{r} \ge 1.6$.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Common Calculations Summary

| Quantity | Input Data | Output | Formula |
|----------|-----------|--------|---------|
| Engineering Stress | $F$, $A_0$ | $\sigma_e$ | $\sigma_e = F/A_0$ |
| True Stress | $\sigma_e$, $\varepsilon_e$ | $\sigma_t$ | $\sigma_t = \sigma_e(1+\varepsilon_e)$ |
| True Strain | $\varepsilon_e$ | $\varepsilon_t$ | $\varepsilon_t = \ln(1+\varepsilon_e)$ |
| $n$-value | 2 pts $(\sigma_t, \varepsilon_t)$ | $n$ | $n = \Delta\ln\sigma_t / \Delta\ln\varepsilon_t$ |
| $K$ (Strength Coeff.) | $n$, one data point | $K$ | $K = \sigma_t / \varepsilon_t^{\,n}$ |
| $r$-value | $w_0, w_f, t_0, t_f$ | $r$ | $r = \ln(w_0/w_f) / \ln(t_0/t_f)$ |
| Normal Anisotropy | $r_0, r_{45}, r_{90}$ | $\bar{r}$ | $\bar{r} = (r_0 + 2r_{45} + r_{90})/4$ |
| Planar Anisotropy | $r_0, r_{45}, r_{90}$ | $\Delta r$ | $\Delta r = (r_0 - 2r_{45} + r_{90})/2$ |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
>
> **Q1. Define the strain-hardening exponent ($n$) and strength coefficient ($K$) in the Hollomon power law. What is their physical significance in sheet metal forming? (3 Marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The Hollomon power law expresses true stress as $\sigma_t = K \cdot \varepsilon_t^{\,n}$.
> - The **strain-hardening exponent ($n$)** is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies the rate at which a metal hardens with plastic strain. Physically, it indicates the **resistance to localized necking** — a high $n$ distributes strain more uniformly, improving stretch formability.
> - The **strength coefficient ($K$)** has units of MPa and represents the true stress at $\varepsilon_t = 1$. It is a measure of the **overall strength level** of the material.
> - In sheet metal forming, **high $n$** is essential for stretch-forming operations (e.g., automobile outer panels), while a combination of high $K$ and high $n$ is needed for high-strength applications.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
>
> **Q2. What is the Lankford coefficient ($r$-value) and why is it important in sheet metal deformation analysis? (3 Marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The Lankford coefficient (or **plastic strain ratio $r$**) is defined as the ratio of true width strain to true thickness strain in a uniaxial tensile test:
> $$
> r = \frac{\varepsilon_w}{\varepsilon_t}
> $$
> - It measures the **resistance of the sheet to thinning** during plastic deformation.
> - **Importance:** A high $r$-value ($\ge 1.6$) indicates that the sheet thins slowly under tension, making it suitable for **deep-drawing operations** (e.g., beverage cans, automobile fuel tanks).
> - It is **orientation-dependent** due to crystallographic texture from rolling, so it must be measured in 0°, 45°, and 90° directions and averaged to give the **normal anisotropy $\bar{r}$**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 2, ESE Pattern 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** With neat sketches, describe the procedure for determining the strain-hardening exponent ($n$) and strength coefficient ($K$) of a sheet metal specimen using a uniaxial tensile test. Mention the relevant standard, specimen geometry, and the data reduction methodology. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> **(b)** A deep-drawing quality steel sheet specimen (initial gauge length $L_0 = 50\,\text{mm}$, width $w_0 = 12.5\,\text{mm}$, thickness $t_0 = 0.8\,\text{mm}$) is tested along the rolling direction. The following true stress–true strain data are obtained in the uniform plastic region:
> | Point | $\varepsilon_t$ | $\sigma_t$ (MPa) |
> |-------|-----------------|------------------|
> | 1 | 0.05 | 380 |
> | 2 | 0.20 | 540 |
>
> Calculate the **strain-hardening exponent ($n$)** and the **strength coefficient ($K$)**. Comment on the formability of the sheet. **\[7 Marks\]**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Procedure [7 Marks]

**1. Standard & Specimen:** The test follows **ASTM E8** for sheet metals. A flat tensile specimen of gauge length $L_0 = 50\,\text{mm}$ and width $w_0 = 12.5\,\text{mm}$ is prepared by milling or wire-EDM to avoid affecting surface and edge properties.

**2. Orientation:** Specimens are cut at **0°, 45°, and 90°** to the rolling direction to capture anisotropy.

**3. Pre-test measurements:** Initial dimensions $L_0$, $w_0$, $t_0$ are recorded using a vernier caliper and micrometer (average of 3 readings).

**4. Testing:** The specimen is mounted on a Universal Testing Machine (UTM) equipped with an **extensometer** and pulled at a constant crosshead speed (typically 1–5 mm/min) per **ASTM E8**.

**5. Data recording:** Load vs. elongation is recorded continuously until fracture. Yield point, UTS, and fracture load are identified.

**6. Post-test measurements:** Final dimensions $L_f$, $w_f$, $t_f$ are measured near the fracture zone.

**7. Data reduction:** Engineering and true stress–strain values are computed. A log-log plot of $\sigma_t$ vs. $\varepsilon_t$ in the uniform plastic region yields a straight line of slope $n$ and intercept $\log K$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:** Two data points $(\varepsilon_{t1}, \sigma_{t1}) = (0.05, 380)$ and $(\varepsilon_{t2}, \sigma_{t2}) = (0.20, 540)$.

**Step 1 — Compute $n$ using the slope formula:**
$$
n = \frac{\ln(\sigma_{t1}/\sigma_{t2})}{\ln(\varepsilon_{t1}/\varepsilon_{t2})}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**
$$
n = \frac{\ln(380/540)}{\ln(0.05/0.20)} = \frac{\ln(0.7037)}{\ln(0.25)}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate logarithms:**
$$
\ln(0.7037) = -0.3514 \quad ; \quad \ln(0.25) = -1.3863
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $n$:**
$$
n = \frac{-0.3514}{-1.3863} = 0.2535 \approx 0.25
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $K$ using Hollomon's law at point 1:**
$$
\sigma_{t1} = K \cdot \varepsilon_{t1}^{\,n} \implies K = \frac{\sigma_{t1}}{\varepsilon_{t1}^{\,n}}
$$
$$
K = \frac{380}{(0.05)^{0.2535}}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $(0.05)^{0.2535}$:**
$$
\ln(0.05) = -2.9957 \implies (0.05)^{0.2535} = e^{-2.9957 \times 0.2535} = e^{-0.7595} = 0.4681
$$

**Step 7 — Final $K$ value:**
$$
K = \frac{380}{0.4681} = 811.8 \approx 812\,\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 8 — Formability comment:**
With $n = 0.25$, the sheet exhibits **good stretch formability** (typical $n$ for deep-drawing steel is 0.20–0.30). This indicates uniform strain distribution and resistance to localized necking. Hence, the sheet is suitable for **moderate-to-deep drawing operations** such as automotive panels.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**
>
> **(a)** Define the terms **normal anisotropy ($\bar{r}$)** and **planar anisotropy ($\Delta r$)**. Explain how these are determined experimentally, and discuss their significance in controlling **earing** during deep-drawing. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> **(b)** Three tensile specimens of an aluminum alloy sheet (gauge length 50 mm, width 12.5 mm, thickness 1.0 mm) are tested along 0°, 45°, and 90° to the rolling direction. The final dimensions after fracture are:
> | Orientation | $L_f$ (mm) | $w_f$ (mm) | $t_f$ (mm) |
> |-------------|------------|------------|------------|
> | 0° | 62.0 | 11.20 | 0.88 |
> | 45° | 60.5 | 11.50 | 0.91 |
> | 90° | 61.0 | 11.35 | 0.89 |
>
> Calculate $r_0$, $r_{45}$, $r_{90}$, the **normal anisotropy $\bar{r}$**, and the **planar anisotropy $\Delta r$**. Predict whether the cup will show **earing** during deep-drawing. **\[7 Marks\]**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Theory [7 Marks]

- **Normal anisotropy ($\bar{r}$):** Average $r$-value across the three principal sheet orientations, defined as
  $$
  \bar{r} = \frac{r_0 + 2r_{45} + r_{90}}{4}
  $$
  It quantifies the **average resistance to thinning** in the sheet plane. **Higher $\bar{r}$ → better deep-drawability.**

- **Planar anisotropy ($\Delta r$):** Defined as
  $$
  \Delta r = \frac{r_0 - 2r_{45} + r_{90}}{2}
  $$
  It quantifies the **variation in $r$-value with direction** in the sheet plane. **$\Delta r \neq 0$ leads to earing** during deep-drawing.

- **Earing mechanism:** During deep-drawing, if $\bar{r}$ is the same in all directions, the cup wall is uniform. However, if $\Delta r \neq 0$, the metal flows more easily in directions of higher $r$, producing **4 ears** (if $\Delta r > 0$, ears appear at 0°/90°) or **6 ears** (if $\Delta r < 0$). Earing causes:
  - Material waste (ears must be trimmed off).
  - Surface defects on the drawn part.

- **Experimental determination:** Conduct uniaxial tensile tests on specimens cut at 0°, 45°, and 90° to the rolling direction, measure width and thickness strains, compute $r$ for each, and apply the formulas above.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Common data:** $L_0 = 50\,\text{mm}$, $w_0 = 12.5\,\text{mm}$, $t_0 = 1.0\,\text{mm}$.

**Step 1 — $r_0$ at 0° orientation:**
$$
\varepsilon_{L,0} = \ln(62.0/50) = \ln(1.24) = 0.2151
$$
$$
\varepsilon_{w,0} = \ln(11.20/12.5) = \ln(0.896) = -0.1098
$$
$$
\varepsilon_{t,0} = \ln(0.88/1.0) = \ln(0.88) = -0.1278
$$
$$
r_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_{w,0}}{\varepsilon_{t,0}} = \frac{-0.1098}{-0.1278} = 0.859
$$

**Step 2 — $r_{45}$ at 45° orientation:**
$$
\varepsilon_{L,45} = \ln(60.5/50) = \ln(1.21) = 0.1906
$$
$$
\varepsilon_{w,45} = \ln(11.50/12.5) = \ln(0.92) = -0.0834
$$
$$
\varepsilon_{t,45} = \ln(0.91/1.0) = \ln(0.91) = -0.0943
$$
$$
r_{45} = \frac{-0.0834}{-0.0943} = 0.884
$$

**Step 3 — $r_{90}$ at 90° orientation:**
$$
\varepsilon_{L,90} = \ln(61.0/50) = \ln(1.22) = 0.1989
$$
$$
\varepsilon_{w,90} = \ln(11.35/12.5) = \ln(0.908) = -0.0965
$$
$$
\varepsilon_{t,90} = \ln(0.89/1.0) = \ln(0.89) = -0.1165
$$
$$
r_{90} = \frac{-0.0965}{-0.1165} = 0.828
$$

**Step 4 — Normal anisotropy $\bar{r}$:**
$$
\bar{r} = \frac{r_0 + 2r_{45} + r_{90}}{4} = \frac{0.859 + 2(0.884) + 0.828}{4}
$$
$$
\bar{r} = \frac{0.859 + 1.768 + 0.828}{4} = \frac{3.455}{4} = 0.864
$$

**Step 5 — Planar anisotropy $\Delta r$:**
$$
\Delta r = \frac{r_0 - 2r_{45} + r_{90}}{2} = \frac{0.859 - 1.768 + 0.828}{2}
$$
$$
\Delta r = \frac{-0.081}{2} = -0.0405
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation & Earing Prediction:**

- $\bar{r} = 0.864 < 1$ → sheet has **poor drawability** (it thins more easily than it widens).
- $\Delta r = -0.0405 \neq 0$ → there IS variation in the planar $r$-value.

Since $\Delta r < 0$, the direction of **maximum $r$ is at 45°** (the diagonals), and the **minimum $r$ is at 0°/90°**. The cup will exhibit **earing with 4 ears located at 0° and 90°** to the rolling direction (because the metal resists deformation less at 0°/90°, it flows more easily there, causing ears to bulge out at those angles). The earing magnitude is small (since $\vert \Delta r \vert$ is small) but measurable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often confuse the **width and thickness** in the $r$-value formula. Remember: $r = \varepsilon_w / \varepsilon_t$ (width strain divided by thickness strain), **NEVER** length strain in the denominator.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to convert to **true (logarithmic) strain**. The $r$-value MUST use true strain; using engineering strain gives an incorrect value.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Calculating $\Delta r$ with the wrong sign convention. Re-derive: $\Delta r = (r_0 + r_{90} - 2r_{45})/2$ is correct, but $(r_0 - 2r_{45} + r_{90})/2$ is the SAME expression written differently — students often drop a minus sign.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Failing to state the assumption of **volume constancy** before computing the thickness strain. This is a 1-mark deduction per KTU scheme.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** For low $\bar{r}$ (like our 0.864), the sheet is **not suitable for deep-drawing**; examiners expect this comment explicitly.
> - **Mark Loss Pattern:** Typically 1 mark is deducted for unit omissions, 1 for missing the formability comment, and 1–2 for sign errors in $\Delta r$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Module Experiment 2: Deformation Analysis of Sheet Metals**

- **Standard test methods:** ASTM E8 (tensile), ASTM E517 ($r$-value), ISO 6892-1.
- **Specimen orientation:** 0° (rolling direction), 45°, and 90° (transverse) — always test all three.
- **Hollomon Power Law:** $\sigma_t = K\,\varepsilon_t^{\,n}$ — the **backbone equation** of sheet metal plastic deformation.
- **$n$-value (strain-hardening exponent):** Typical range **0.05–0.50**; high $n$ → uniform strain → good stretch formability.
- **$K$-value (strength coefficient):** Units MPa; computed as $K = \sigma_t / \varepsilon_t^{\,n}$.
- **Engineering → True conversion:**
  - $\sigma_t = \sigma_e (1 + \varepsilon_e)$
  - $\varepsilon_t = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_e)$
- **Lankford coefficient $r$:** $r = \varepsilon_w / \varepsilon_t$; typical deep-drawing steel has $r \approx 1.5$–$2.0$.
- **Normal anisotropy $\bar{r} = (r_0 + 2r_{45} + r_{90})/4$:** higher → better deep-drawability.
- **Planar anisotropy $\Delta r = (r_0 - 2r_{45} + r_{90})/2$:** ideally $\approx 0$ → no earing.
- **Volume constancy:** $\varepsilon_L + \varepsilon_w + \varepsilon_t = 0$ — used to compute thickness strain from measured length and width strains.
- **Earing rule:** $\Delta r > 0$ → 4 ears at 0° and 90°; $\Delta r < 0$ → 4 ears at 45°; $\Delta r = 0$ → no earing.
- **Forming Limit Diagram (FLD):** Graph of $\varepsilon_1$ (major strain) vs. $\varepsilon_2$ (minor strain) showing safe, necking, and fracture zones — a graphical extension of this analysis.
- **Young's Modulus of typical sheet steels:** $E \approx 200\,\text{GPa}$.
- **Typical yield strength of low-carbon steel sheet:** $200$–$350\,\text{MPa}$.
- **Log-log plot technique:** $\log \sigma_t$ vs. $\log \varepsilon_t$ gives slope $= n$ and y-intercept $= \log K$.
- **Uniaxial tensile test limitation:** Only gives the **left half** of the FLD (negative minor strain); equibiaxial bulge test is needed for the right half.
- **Examiner-favorite derivations:** (1) Engineering-to-true stress conversion, (2) $n$-value from two data points, (3) $r$-value from length/width/thickness data.
- **Always comment on formability** at the end of any numerical problem — this is a **mandatory closing statement** for full marks.

---

topics.title: Tool wear assessment and verification of Taylor's Tool life equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL408 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Tool wear assessment and verification of Taylor's Tool life equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛠️ Module Experiment 2: Tool Wear Assessment & Verification of Taylor's Tool Life Equation

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tool Wear** is defined as the gradual loss of material from the cutting edge of a tool due to the mechanical, thermal, and chemical action of the workpiece during machining. The progressive deterioration directly affects the surface finish, dimensional accuracy, and power consumption of the machining process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tool Life (T)** is defined as the actual cumulative cutting time (usually in **minutes**) during which a cutting tool operates satisfactorily before it reaches a predetermined **end-of-life criterion** (typically uniform flank wear VB = **0.3 mm** or maximum flank wear VBmax = **0.6 mm**), as per **ISO 3685** standards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Taylor's Tool Life Equation** is an empirical relationship, proposed by **Frederick W. Taylor (1907)**, that correlates cutting speed with tool life. The classical form is given by:
> $$\large V \cdot T^{n} = C$$
> where $V$ = cutting speed (m/min), $T$ = tool life (min), $n$ = Taylor's tool life exponent (a dimensionless constant, typically **0.20–0.25** for HSS tools and **0.25–0.40** for carbide tools), and $C$ = Taylor's constant (cutting speed corresponding to $T = 1$ min).

---

### 🍎 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are driving a car. The faster you drive (higher speed), the quicker your **car tires wear out** (shorter life). If you drive slowly, the tires last much longer. **Tool wear behaves exactly the same way!**

- **Cutting speed ($V$)** is analogous to the **driving speed of the car**.
- **Tool life ($T$)** is analogous to **how long the tires last** before they need replacement.
- The relationship is **not linear** — it is a **power law** (an inverse curved relationship).
- The constant **$C$** tells you about the "quality" of the tool material (better tool $\Rightarrow$ higher $C$).
- The exponent **$n$** tells you how *sensitive* the tool is to cutting speed changes.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it this way:** Doubling the cutting speed does NOT halve the tool life — it can reduce tool life to as little as **$\frac{1}{16}$** of its original value! This is why machinists always select the **lowest possible cutting speed** that still gives acceptable productivity.

---

### 🎯 Key Physical Metrics to Remember

| Metric | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Uniform Flank Wear ($V_B$)** | **0.3** | mm |
| **Maximum Flank Wear ($V_{B,\max}$)** | **0.6** | mm |
| **Crater Wear Depth ($K_T$)** | $0.06 + 0.3f$ | mm |
| **Taylor's Exponent ($n$) for HSS** | **0.20 – 0.25** | dimensionless |
| **Taylor's Exponent ($n$) for Carbide** | **0.25 – 0.40** | dimensionless |
| **Standard Reference Workpiece** | **0.2% C Steel (AISI 1040)** | — |
| **Standard Reference Tool** | **ISO P10 / P20 grade carbide** | — |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Tool Wear (Zones of Failure)

When a single-point cutting tool is used, wear is not uniform. It occurs in distinct, well-defined regions:

1. **Flank Wear (V_B)** — Wear on the **relief (flank) face** of the tool caused by rubbing against the machined surface. This is the **most commonly used** wear criterion because it directly affects surface finish and dimensional accuracy.

2. **Crater Wear (K_T)** — Wear on the **rake face** of the tool, forming a depression called a *crater*. It is caused by the high temperature at the tool-chip interface and chemical diffusion.

3. **Nose Wear** — Wear that occurs at the **tool nose radius** region. It combines the effects of flank and crater wear.

4. **Notch Wear (V_N)** — A localized groove that forms at the **depth-of-cut line (DOC)** due to work-hardening of the workpiece material.

5. **Built-Up Edge (BUE) Failure** — Material from the workpiece welds to the tool tip, then breaks off taking a small piece of tool material with it.

---

## 2.2 The Three Stages of Tool Wear Curve

The classic **tool wear vs. cutting time** curve has three distinct regions:

| Stage | Time Range | Wear Behaviour | Practical Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I. Initial Wear** | 0 – $T_0$ | Rapid, non-linear | Tool "seats" itself; BUE forms |
| **II. Steady-State Wear** | $T_0$ – $T$ | Slow, nearly linear | **Useful cutting region** |
| **III. End-of-Life Failure** | After $T$ | Rapid, accelerating | **Tool must be replaced** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Taylor tool life $T$ is the cumulative cutting time corresponding to the **boundary between Stage II and Stage III**, where wear begins to accelerate catastrophically.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** and have been asked in previous KTU University Examinations. Master them thoroughly.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Meaning of Variables | Boundary/Standard Values |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Taylor's Original Equation** | $V \cdot T^{n} = C$ | $V$ = cutting speed, $T$ = tool life, $n$ = exponent, $C$ = constant | $0.20 \le n \le 0.40$ |
| 2 | **Logarithmic Form** | $\log V = \log C - n \log T$ | Used for plotting experimental data | Linear graph on log-log scale |
| 3 | **Effect of Cutting Speed Change** | $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{n}$ | Predicts new tool life for new speed | — |
| 4 | **Extended Taylor Equation** | $V \cdot T^{n} \cdot f^{a} \cdot d^{b} = K$ | Includes feed $f$ and depth $d$ | $a \approx 0.5$–$1.0$, $b \approx 0.1$–$0.3$ |
| 5 | **Flank Wear Criterion (ISO 3685)** | $V_B = 0.3$ mm | Uniform flank wear limit | Constant criterion |
| 6 | **Maximum Flank Wear Criterion** | $V_{B,\max} = 0.6$ mm | Local flank wear limit | For finishing operations |
| 7 | **Crater Wear Criterion** | $K_T = 0.06 + 0.3f$ | $f$ = feed (mm/rev) | For medium carbon steel |
| 8 | **Taylor Exponent from Slope** | $n = \dfrac{\log V_1 - \log V_2}{\log T_2 - \log T_1}$ | From two-point data | Average of multiple readings |
| 9 | **Tool Life Ratio Rule** | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = \left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{1/n}$ | Direct ratio form | $n = 0.25$ (typical) |
| 10 | **Cutting Speed** | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | $D$ = dia (mm), $N$ = rpm | m/min |

> **🔑 Pro Tip:** When verifying Taylor's equation, **always take the log of both sides** to get a linear graph. The **slope gives $-n$** and the **intercept gives $\log C$**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CAM Software**: Modern CNC machines use the **extended Taylor equation** to automatically recommend cutting speeds based on tool material, workpiece, and tool life requirement.
- **Cost Optimization**: A production engineer uses Taylor's equation to choose between **higher speed (more productivity)** vs. **lower speed (more tool life)** — the **economic cutting speed** minimizes total cost.
- **Tool Database**: Tool manufacturers like Sandvik, Kennametal, and Mitsubishi publish $n$ and $C$ values for their tool grades — these are direct Taylor's constants.
- **Adaptive Control**: Modern CNC machines monitor cutting force and adjust speed in real time to keep wear rate at Stage II, maximizing productivity.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Logarithmic Form of Taylor's Equation (From $V \cdot T^n = C$ to $\log V = \log C - n \log T$)

This is the most important derivation for the lab. It allows us to verify Taylor's equation by plotting experimental data on a **log-log graph**.

### Step 1: Start with Taylor's Original Equation
$$V \cdot T^{n} = C$$

### Step 2: Take the Logarithm (Base 10) of Both Sides
$$\log(V \cdot T^{n}) = \log C$$

### Step 3: Apply the Logarithm Product Rule — $\log(A \cdot B) = \log A + \log B$
$$\log V + \log(T^{n}) = \log C$$

### Step 4: Apply the Power Rule — $\log(A^{k}) = k \cdot \log A$
$$\log V + n \cdot \log T = \log C$$

### Step 5: Rearrange to Match the Equation of a Straight Line ($y = mx + c$)
$$\log V = -n \cdot \log T + \log C$$

### Step 6: Identify the Components of a Line

| Line Component | Standard Form $y = mx + c$ | Taylor's Form $\log V = -n \log T + \log C$ |
|---|---|---|
| $y$ (vertical axis) | $y$ | $\log V$ |
| $x$ (horizontal axis) | $x$ | $\log T$ |
| Slope $m$ | $m$ | $-n$ |
| Y-intercept $c$ | $c$ | $\log C$ |

### ✅ Conclusion
The plot of **$\log V$ vs. $\log T$** must yield a **straight line** with:
- **Slope** $= -n$  $\Rightarrow$  $n = -\text{slope}$
- **Y-intercept** $= \log C$  $\Rightarrow$  $C = 10^{\text{intercept}}$

This is precisely **how Taylor's equation is verified experimentally** in this lab experiment.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effect of Cutting Speed on Tool Life

Starting from the same Taylor equation applied to two different speeds:
$$V_1 \cdot T_1^{n} = C \quad \text{and} \quad V_2 \cdot T_2^{n} = C$$

### Step 1: Equate Both Expressions (Both equal C)
$$V_1 \cdot T_1^{n} = V_2 \cdot T_2^{n}$$

### Step 2: Divide Both Sides by $V_2 \cdot T_1^{n}$
$$\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{T_2^{n}}{T_1^{n}}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Quotient Power Rule — $\dfrac{A^{n}}{B^{n}} = \left(\dfrac{A}{B}\right)^{n}$
$$\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{n}$$

### ✅ Final Result — Tool Life Ratio Rule
$$\boxed{\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{n} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = \left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{1/n}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Check:** If $n = 0.25$ and the cutting speed is **doubled** ($V_2 = 2 V_1$), then $T_2 = T_1 / 2^{0.25} = T_1 / 1.189$. So the tool life reduces to about **84%** of its original value.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Extended Taylor Equation (Including Feed and Depth of Cut)

The classical Taylor equation ignores feed and depth. To generalize:

### Step 1: Write Tool Life as a Function of All Process Variables
$$T = f(V, f, d)$$

### Step 2: Assume a Power-Law Relationship
$$T \cdot V^{\alpha} \cdot f^{\beta} \cdot d^{\gamma} = C'$$

### Step 3: Express in Taylor's Standard Form
Rearranging so that speed has unit exponent:
$$V \cdot T^{n} \cdot f^{a} \cdot d^{b} = K$$

where:
- $n = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}$  (Taylor's exponent)
- $a = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}$  (feed exponent, typically $0.5$–$1.0$)
- $b = \dfrac{\gamma}{\alpha}$  (depth exponent, typically $0.1$–$0.3$)
- $K = C'^{1/\alpha}$  (generalized Taylor constant)

### Step 4: Take the Logarithm
$$\log V + n \log T + a \log f + b \log d = \log K$$

This is a **multi-linear regression** problem in real life — solved by **multiple linear regression** on logged data.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example for Tool Life Verification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Sample Data** (workpiece: AISI 1040, tool: carbide P20, $f = 0.2$ mm/rev, $d = 2$ mm):

| Trial | Cutting Speed $V$ (m/min) | Tool Life $T$ (min) | $\log V$ | $\log T$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 80 | 90.0 | 1.9031 | 1.9542 |
| 2 | 100 | 30.0 | 2.0000 | 1.4771 |
| 3 | 125 | 12.0 | 2.0969 | 1.0792 |
| 4 | 160 | 4.5 | 2.2041 | 0.6532 |
| 5 | 200 | 1.8 | 2.3010 | 0.2553 |

### Step 1: Compute Slopes Between Successive Pairs (Two-Point Method)

$$\text{Slope} = \dfrac{\log V_2 - \log V_1}{\log T_2 - \log T_1}$$

**Between Trial 1 and 2:**
$$\text{Slope} = \dfrac{2.0000 - 1.9031}{1.4771 - 1.9542} = \dfrac{0.0969}{-0.4771} = -0.2031$$

**Between Trial 2 and 3:**
$$\text{Slope} = \dfrac{2.0969 - 2.0000}{1.0792 - 1.4771} = \dfrac{0.0969}{-0.3979} = -0.2435$$

**Between Trial 3 and 4:**
$$\text{Slope} = \dfrac{2.2041 - 2.0969}{0.6532 - 1.0792} = \dfrac{0.1072}{-0.4260} = -0.2516$$

**Between Trial 4 and 5:**
$$\text{Slope} = \dfrac{2.3010 - 2.2041}{0.2553 - 0.6532} = \dfrac{0.0969}{-0.3979} = -0.2435$$

### Step 2: Compute the Average Slope
$$\bar{m} = \dfrac{(-0.2031) + (-0.2435) + (-0.2516) + (-0.2435)}{4} = \dfrac{-0.9417}{4} = -0.2354$$

### Step 3: Determine the Exponent $n$
$$\boxed{n = -\bar{m} = 0.2354}$$

> This value lies within the expected range of **0.20–0.25** for HSS or **0.25–0.40** for carbide, validating the experimental procedure.

### Step 4: Determine the Constant $C$ Using $V = 100$ m/min and $T = 30$ min
$$C = V \cdot T^{n} = 100 \cdot (30)^{0.2354}$$

$$(30)^{0.2354} = e^{0.2354 \cdot \ln 30} = e^{0.2354 \cdot 3.4012} = e^{0.8007} = 2.2272$$

$$C = 100 \cdot 2.2272 = 222.72 \; \text{m/min}$$

### ✅ Final Verified Taylor's Equation for the Above Conditions:
$$\boxed{V \cdot T^{0.2354} = 222.72}$$

### Step 5: Validation — Plug Back in Another Trial
For Trial 3: $V = 125$ m/min, expected $T$:
$$T = \left(\dfrac{C}{V}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\dfrac{222.72}{125}\right)^{1/0.2354} = (1.7818)^{4.248} = 12.5 \; \text{min}$$

Observed $T = 12.0$ min  $\Rightarrow$ **Error = 4.2%** (Acceptable for lab verification).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the following terms with reference to tool wear in machining:**
(a) Flank wear
(b) Crater wear
(c) Tool life

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(a) Flank Wear (1 mark):**
Flank wear is the progressive loss of material from the **relief (flank) face** of the cutting tool, occurring due to rubbing between the tool and the freshly machined workpiece surface. It is measured as $V_B$ and is the most commonly used wear criterion in practice. The standard limit is **$V_B = 0.3$ mm** as per ISO 3685.

**(b) Crater Wear (1 mark):**
Crater wear is the formation of a depression on the **rake face** of the tool, caused by high temperature and chemical diffusion at the tool-chip interface. It is measured as depth $K_T$ and is especially significant at high cutting speeds with carbide tools.

**(c) Tool Life (1 mark):**
Tool life is the **total cumulative cutting time** (in minutes) during which a tool performs satisfactorily before reaching a prescribed end-of-life criterion, typically $V_B = 0.3$ mm for uniform flank wear. It is the fundamental dependent variable in Taylor's tool life equation.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**State Taylor's tool life equation and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Taylor's tool life equation is:
$$V \cdot T^{n} = C$$

**Significance of each term (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

- **$V$ (cutting speed in m/min):** The independent process variable that has the **most dominant effect** on tool life. Higher $V$ drastically reduces tool life.

- **$T$ (tool life in min):** The dependent variable representing the **useful cutting time** of the tool.

- **$n$ (Taylor's tool life exponent):** A dimensionless constant that indicates how **sensitive the tool material is to cutting speed changes**. Lower $n$ means the tool is *less* sensitive to speed changes. Typical values: **0.20–0.25 for HSS, 0.25–0.40 for carbide**.

- **$C$ (Taylor's constant):** The cutting speed corresponding to a tool life of **1 minute**. It is essentially a measure of **tool material quality and hot hardness** — higher $C$ means a better tool.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### ✅ Question A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**During a Taylor's tool life verification experiment on a lathe, the following data was obtained using a carbide tool on mild steel workpiece (AISI 1040) at feed $f = 0.2$ mm/rev and depth of cut $d = 2$ mm:**

| Trial | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cutting Speed $V$ (m/min)** | 60 | 90 | 120 | 150 | 180 |
| **Tool Life $T$ (min)** | 75 | 22 | 9.5 | 4.0 | 1.8 |

**(a) Plot $\log V$ vs. $\log T$ and determine Taylor's tool life equation.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) If the cutting speed is increased to 200 m/min, predict the new tool life. Also comment on the validity of the equation with respect to the standard value of $n$.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📋 Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Plotting and Equation Determination (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute the logarithm values (2 marks)**

| Trial | $V$ (m/min) | $T$ (min) | $\log V$ | $\log T$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 60 | 75 | 1.7782 | 1.8751 |
| 2 | 90 | 22 | 1.9542 | 1.3424 |
| 3 | 120 | 9.5 | 2.0792 | 0.9777 |
| 4 | 150 | 4.0 | 2.1761 | 0.6021 |
| 5 | 180 | 1.8 | 2.2553 | 0.2553 |

**Step 2: Apply the two-point method to find slope $m$ between successive trials (3 marks)**

Between Trial 1 and 2:
$$m_{1-2} = \dfrac{1.9542 - 1.7782}{1.3424 - 1.8751} = \dfrac{0.1760}{-0.5327} = -0.3304$$

Between Trial 2 and 3:
$$m_{2-3} = \dfrac{2.0792 - 1.9542}{0.9777 - 1.3424} = \dfrac{0.1250}{-0.3647} = -0.3427$$

Between Trial 3 and 4:
$$m_{3-4} = \dfrac{2.1761 - 2.0792}{0.6021 - 0.9777} = \dfrac{0.0969}{-0.3756} = -0.2580$$

Between Trial 4 and 5:
$$m_{4-5} = \dfrac{2.2553 - 2.1761}{0.2553 - 0.6021} = \dfrac{0.0792}{-0.3468} = -0.2284$$

**Average slope:**
$$\bar{m} = \dfrac{(-0.3304) + (-0.3427) + (-0.2580) + (-0.2284)}{4} = \dfrac{-1.1595}{4} = -0.2899$$

**Step 3: Determine $n$ and $C$ (2 marks)**

$$n = -\bar{m} = 0.2899 \approx 0.29$$

For $C$, use Trial 3: $V = 120$, $T = 9.5$
$$C = V \cdot T^{n} = 120 \cdot (9.5)^{0.29}$$

$(9.5)^{0.29} = e^{0.29 \cdot \ln 9.5} = e^{0.29 \cdot 2.2513} = e^{0.6529} = 1.9212$

$$C = 120 \cdot 1.9212 = 230.5 \; \text{m/min}$$

### ✅ Verified Taylor's Equation:
$$\boxed{V \cdot T^{0.29} = 230.5}$$

---

### Part (b) — Prediction and Validity (7 marks)

**Step 1: Predict new tool life at $V = 200$ m/min (3 marks)**

$$T = \left(\dfrac{C}{V}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\dfrac{230.5}{200}\right)^{1/0.29} = (1.1525)^{3.448}$$

$$\log T = 3.448 \cdot \log(1.1525) = 3.448 \cdot 0.0617 = 0.2127$$

$$T = 10^{0.2127} = 1.63 \; \text{min}$$

**Step 2: Validity check (4 marks)**

- The experimentally obtained value of $n = 0.29$ falls within the standard range for **carbide tools (0.25–0.40)**, validating the equation.
- The value of $C = 230.5$ m/min is realistic for a P20 grade carbide tool on mild steel.
- The small variation in individual slopes (from $-0.228$ to $-0.343$) is due to **experimental scatter** arising from BUE formation, workpiece inhomogeneity, and tool wear measurement errors.
- The equation is therefore **validated within acceptable experimental tolerance (typically ±10%)**.

---

### ✅ Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a) With a neat sketch, explain the different types of tool wear in single-point cutting tools. List the standard ISO 3685 wear criteria.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Two turning operations are performed on the same workpiece using identical tools. In operation 1, cutting speed $V_1 = 100$ m/min gives tool life $T_1 = 60$ min. In operation 2, cutting speed $V_2 = 150$ m/min. If Taylor's exponent is $n = 0.25$, determine (i) the new tool life $T_2$, and (ii) the percentage reduction in tool life when the speed is increased by 50%.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📋 Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Types of Tool Wear (7 marks)

**Types of Tool Wear (5 marks — 1 mark each + sketch):**

1. **Flank Wear ($V_B$):** Wear on the relief face due to rubbing against the machined surface. Uniform wear criterion: $V_B = 0.3$ mm.

2. **Crater Wear ($K_T$):** A depression on the rake face due to diffusion at high temperatures. Criterion: $K_T = 0.06 + 0.3f$.

3. **Nose Wear:** Combined wear at the tool nose radius region.

4. **Notch Wear ($V_N$):** Localized groove at the depth of cut line due to work-hardening.

5. **Built-Up Edge (BUE):** Workpiece material welded to cutting edge, then tearing off portions of the tool.

**ISO 3685 Standard Wear Criteria (2 marks):**
- Uniform flank wear: $V_B = 0.3$ mm
- Maximum flank wear: $V_{B,\max} = 0.6$ mm
- Crater wear: $K_T = 0.06 + 0.3f$ (where $f$ is feed in mm/rev)
- Tool life is the cumulative cutting time when **any one** of the above criteria is first reached.

---

### Part (b) — Tool Life Prediction (7 marks)

**Given:** $V_1 = 100$ m/min, $T_1 = 60$ min, $V_2 = 150$ m/min, $n = 0.25$

**Step 1: Use the Tool Life Ratio Rule (4 marks)**

$$\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{n}$$

$$\dfrac{100}{150} = \left(\dfrac{T_2}{60}\right)^{0.25}$$

$$0.6667 = \left(\dfrac{T_2}{60}\right)^{0.25}$$

Raise both sides to the power $1/0.25 = 4$:

$$(0.6667)^{4} = \dfrac{T_2}{60}$$

$$0.1975 = \dfrac{T_2}{60}$$

$$T_2 = 60 \cdot 0.1975 = 11.85 \; \text{min}$$

**Step 2: Percentage reduction in tool life (3 marks)**

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1} \cdot 100 = \dfrac{60 - 11.85}{60} \cdot 100 = 80.25\%$$

### ✅ Final Answers:
- **New tool life $T_2 = 11.85$ min**
- **Percentage reduction = 80.25%**

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Students often forget to convert cutting speed to **m/min** (not mm/s or m/s) when plugging into Taylor's equation. This loses **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Log Confusion:** When plotting $\log V$ vs $\log T$, students sometimes swap axes. **Always put $T$ (independent variable the experimenter controls) on the X-axis** and $V$ on the Y-axis.
> 3. **Exponent Sign Error:** Taylor's exponent $n$ is **positive**. The slope is **negative**. Don't forget the minus sign when reporting $n = -m$.
> 4. **Skipping Units in Final Answer:** Always state the units of $C$ (m/min) and $n$ (dimensionless) in the final equation. Unanswered units lose **1 mark**.
> 5. **Ignoring Wear Criteria:** Many students write tool life values without specifying that they correspond to **$V_B = 0.3$ mm** (or whichever ISO criterion is used). This is a **1-mark penalty**.
> 6. **Averaging the Slope Incorrectly:** Always use the **arithmetic mean of all individual slopes** between successive data points, not just the first and last point.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Tool wear** = gradual loss of tool material; the **main types** are flank, crater, nose, notch, and BUE.
- ✅ **Tool life ($T$)** = cumulative cutting time when wear reaches the **ISO 3685 criterion** ($V_B = 0.3$ mm uniform or $0.6$ mm maximum).
- ✅ **Taylor's Equation:** $V \cdot T^{n} = C$ — The most important formula in the entire module.
- ✅ **Log-log form:** $\log V = \log C - n \log T$ — must be a **straight line** for verification.
- ✅ **Slope of log-log plot = $-n$**, **Y-intercept = $\log C$**.
- ✅ **$n$ values:** 0.20–0.25 (HSS) and 0.25–0.40 (carbide).
- ✅ **Speed-Tool Life Rule:** Doubling speed does NOT halve tool life; it reduces it by a factor of $2^{1/n}$.
- ✅ **Extended Taylor:** $V \cdot T^{n} \cdot f^{a} \cdot d^{b} = K$ — accounts for feed and depth of cut.
- ✅ **Crater wear criterion:** $K_T = 0.06 + 0.3f$ mm.
- ✅ **Three wear stages:** Initial (rapid), Steady (linear, useful), Failure (catastrophic).
- ✅ **Standard workpiece for testing:** AISI 1040 (0.2% C steel).
- ✅ **Verification procedure:** Conduct 5 trials at different speeds, measure $T$ for $V_B = 0.3$ mm, plot $\log V$ vs $\log T$, fit straight line, extract $n$ and $C$.
- ✅ **Cutting speed formula:** $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ m/min (where $D$ in mm, $N$ in rpm).
- ✅ **Taylor's constant $C$** has units of **m/min** (not dimensionless!).
- ✅ **Always mention** which ISO wear criterion was used to define $T$.
- ✅ **Linear regression on log-log data** is the modern, more accurate alternative to the two-point method.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Axis and 3 axis cutting Forces measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL408 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Axis and 3 axis cutting Forces measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Production Engineering** and **machining science**, cutting force measurement refers to the experimental determination of the resistance offered by the workpiece material against the tool during a metal cutting operation. When a single-point cutting tool engages a workpiece, the resultant cutting force is resolved into **three mutually perpendicular components** along the orthogonal coordinate axes established in the machine tool reference frame.

The three orthogonal cutting force components are:

- **F<sub>c</sub> (Tangential / Principal Cutting Force):** Acts in the direction of cutting velocity, tangent to the workpiece surface.
- **F<sub>f</sub> (Feed Force / Axial Force):** Acts in the direction of feed motion, parallel to the workpiece axis.
- **F<sub>r</sub> (Radial / Thrust / Depth Force):** Acts perpendicular to the machined surface, radially into the workpiece.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The laboratory experiment distinguishes between **axis (2-axis) cutting force measurement** — typically recording F<sub>c</sub> and F<sub>f</sub> using a simpler dynamometer — and **3-axis cutting force measurement**, where F<sub>c</sub>, F<sub>f</sub>, and F<sub>r</sub> are simultaneously captured using a multi-component **tool dynamometer** (usually a strain-gauge type).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are slicing a dense, cold loaf of bread with a serrated knife while pushing it forward with controlled pressure.

- The **forward push you apply** along the slice direction represents the **F<sub>c</sub>** — the dominant tangential cutting force.
- The **horizontal motion you make to advance the knife** corresponds to **F<sub>f</sub>** — the feed force, which is usually smaller.
- The **downward pressure** you exert to keep the knife engaged with the loaf represents **F<sub>r</sub>** — the radial force trying to push the tool away from the workpiece.

A **cutting force dynamometer** is essentially the "smart weighing scale" placed beneath the tool — much like a bathroom scale that can simultaneously read your weight in three directions when you push against a wall, lean on a railing, and press the floor at the same time.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

- **Standard units:** **Newton (N)** in SI; **kgf** in legacy CGS data.
- **Common derived metric:** **Specific Cutting Energy (SCE)** expressed in **N·mm⁻²** or **J·mm⁻³**.
- **Sampling rate of modern dynamometers:** typically **1 kHz to 50 kHz**.
- **Tool-workpiece materials are standardized** in KTU labs to **Mild Steel workpiece + HSS tool** (or carbide on a lathe) for repeatability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Resultant Force (R):** The vector sum of the three orthogonal force components, expressed as $R = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_f^2 + F_r^2}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Resolution of Cutting Force

The cutting force system is derived from a **single resultant force R** acting at the tool–chip interface. This resultant is conventionally resolved in **two reference frames**:

| Reference Frame | Axes | Force Components | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Merchant's Frame (Rake Plane)** | Cutting Velocity, Feed, Radial | F<sub>c</sub>, F<sub>f</sub>, F<sub>r</sub> | Machine-tool design, power estimation |
| **Orthogonal Cutting (2-D Shear Plane)** | Shear, Friction, Rake Normal | F<sub>s</sub>, F, N | Theoretical chip-formation analysis |

For a turning operation, the **3-axis dynamometer** measures the signals from strain gauges mounted in three orthogonal directions. The amplified signals are calibrated against known loads, producing voltage-to-force calibration constants (**k<sub>x</sub>, k<sub>y</sub>, k<sub>z</sub>** in **N/µV** or **N/V**).

### 2.1.1 The "Why" behind Three-Component Measurement

- **Power estimation** of the spindle motor requires F<sub>c</sub>: $P = F_c \cdot v_c$, where $v_c$ is the cutting speed in **m/min**.
- **Feed mechanism deflection** and chatter stability analysis require F<sub>f</sub>.
- **Tool wear, surface finish, and form error** are correlated with F<sub>r</sub>.
- A **2-axis (axis) dynamometer** captures only the in-plane components (F<sub>c</sub> + F<sub>f</sub>), which is sufficient for basic turning power calculations but **insufficient for full Merchant's Circle analysis**.

## 2.2 Merchant's Force Circle — Geometric Relations

The Merchant Circle is a graphical vector representation that links the **resultant force R** to its three orthogonal components and the **friction angle β** along the tool–chip interface.

### 2.2.1 Step-by-Step Force Resolution Logic

1. **Define R** as the vector sum of the three measured dynamometer components.
2. **Project R** onto the rake face to obtain the **friction force F** and the **normal force N**.
3. **The angle between R and N** is the **friction angle β**, where $\tan\beta = F/N$.
4. **The angle between the shear plane and the cutting velocity** is the **shear angle φ**.
5. **The angle between R and F<sub>c</sub>** is denoted as **α** (or θ in some texts), the **inclination angle of the resultant**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The relationship $\tan \alpha = F_f / F_c$ is repeatedly tested in KTU Board Examinations. Commit this to memory.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resultant Force $R$ | $R = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_f^2 + F_r^2}$ | N | Vector magnitude of 3-D force |
| 2 | Resultant in 2-D plane | $R_{xy} = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_f^2}$ | N | Axis (2-axis) dynamometer reading |
| 3 | Inclination Angle | $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(F_f / F_c)$ | degrees (°) | Angle of R with cutting velocity |
| 4 | Power Consumed (Spindle) | $P = F_c \cdot v_c$ | W (Watts) | $v_c$ in m/s for SI result |
| 5 | Specific Cutting Energy | $SCE = F_c / (f \cdot d)$ | N·mm⁻² | $f$ = feed (mm/rev), $d$ = depth (mm) |
| 6 | Shear Force (Merchant) | $F_s = R \cdot \cos(\phi + \beta - \gamma)$ | N | γ = rake angle |
| 7 | Friction Coefficient | $\mu = \tan\beta = F/N$ | dimensionless | $\beta$ = friction angle |
| 8 | Shear Angle (Merchant) | $\phi = 45° + \gamma/2 - \beta/2$ | degrees | Merchant's empirical model |
| 9 | Chip Thickness Ratio | $r = t_1 / t_2$ | dimensionless | $t_1$ = uncut chip thickness, $t_2$ = chip thickness |
| 10 | Calibration Law (Dynamometer) | $F = k \cdot V_{out}$ | N | $k$ = calibration constant (N/V) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Merchant's Circle and SCE relationships are foundational to **CNC tool selection databases**, **CAD/CAM feed-rate optimizers**, and **predictive chatter suppression algorithms** used in modern CNC controllers (Fanuc, Siemens, Heidenhain).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Resultant Force and Inclination Angle from 3-Axis Measurements

### 3.1.1 Given Conditions

- The dynamometer outputs three calibrated force signals: **F<sub>x</sub>, F<sub>y</sub>, F<sub>z</sub>**.
- The coordinate system is aligned such that:
  - **X-axis** = direction of cutting velocity (→ F<sub>c</sub>)
  - **Y-axis** = direction of feed motion (→ F<sub>f</sub>)
  - **Z-axis** = radial direction (→ F<sub>r</sub>)

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Resultant Magnitude

**Step 1:** The cutting force vector $\vec{R}$ in Cartesian coordinates is:

$$
\vec{R} = F_x \hat{i} + F_y \hat{j} + F_z \hat{k}
$$

**Step 2:** The magnitude (Euclidean norm) of the vector is obtained from the Pythagorean extension in 3-D:

$$
\vert \vec{R} \vert = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}
$$

**Step 3:** The cross-sectional force in the machining plane (the "axis" or 2-D force measured by a 2-axis dynamometer) is:

$$
R_{xy} = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2} = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_f^2}
$$

**Step 4:** The inclination angle α (the angle between the resultant R and the principal cutting force F<sub>c</sub>) is:

$$
\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{F_y}{F_x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{F_f}{F_c}\right)
$$

**Step 5:** The angle between R and the radial direction (often called the **secondary angle**) is:

$$
\delta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{R_{xy}}{F_z}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{F_c^2 + F_f^2}}{F_r}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The angle α is **always between 0° and 90°** in standard turning because F<sub>c</sub> is the dominant component. For a sharp HSS tool on mild steel, α typically lies in the range **15° to 30°**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Cutting Energy (SCE)

### 3.2.1 Definition of SCE

SCE is the energy required to remove a unit volume of material, an intrinsic property of the workpiece-tool pair.

**Step 1:** Material removal rate (MRR) for a turning operation:

$$
MRR = v_c \cdot f \cdot d
$$

where:
- $v_c$ = cutting speed (mm/min)
- $f$ = feed rate (mm/rev)
- $d$ = depth of cut (mm)

**Step 2:** Power consumed at the cutting zone:

$$
P = F_c \cdot v_c \quad \text{(with } v_c \text{ in mm/min, result in N·mm/min)}
$$

**Step 3:** Energy per unit volume = Power / MRR:

$$
SCE = \frac{P}{MRR} = \frac{F_c \cdot v_c}{v_c \cdot f \cdot d} = \frac{F_c}{f \cdot d}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** SCE is reported in **N·mm⁻²** (= **J·mm⁻³**). Typical values: **0.7 – 2.5 N·mm⁻²** for mild steel with HSS tools; **1.5 – 4.0 N·mm⁻²** for cast iron.

## 3.3 Derivation: Merchant's Shear Angle Equation

The shear angle φ describes the orientation of the deformation plane inside the workpiece material during chip formation.

**Step 1:** From geometric analysis of the Merchant Circle, the equilibrium of forces on the shear plane gives:

$$
F_s = R \cos(\phi + \beta - \gamma)
$$

where:
- $F_s$ = shear force on the shear plane
- $R$ = resultant cutting force
- $\phi$ = shear angle
- $\beta$ = friction angle
- $\gamma$ = tool rake angle

**Step 2:** Equilibrium along the cutting velocity direction (X) and feed direction (Y) yields:

$$
F_c = R \cos\alpha, \qquad F_f = R \sin\alpha
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting and differentiating the shear power with respect to φ (Merchant's minimum energy principle, $dF_s/d\phi = 0$) yields:

$$
\phi = 45° + \frac{\gamma}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}
$$

**Step 4:** Verification — for a tool with $\gamma = 0°$ and friction angle $\beta = 30°$:

$$
\phi = 45° + 0° - 15° = 30°
$$

This 30° shear angle is the **textbook minimum** for orthogonal cutting, consistent with experimental chip morphology.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Cutting Parameters on Forces

| Parameter Increased | Effect on F<sub>c</sub> | Effect on F<sub>f</sub> | Effect on F<sub>r</sub> | Dominant Driver |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cutting speed $v_c$ | Slight decrease | Slight decrease | Slight decrease | BUE suppression |
| Feed $f$ | **Strong increase (∝ f⁰·⁸)** | Strong increase | Strong increase | Chip cross-section area |
| Depth of cut $d$ | **Strong increase (∝ d¹·⁰)** | Strong increase | Strong increase | Same as feed |
| Rake angle $\gamma$ (more positive) | **Decrease** | Decrease | Decrease | Shear force reduction |
| Tool wear (VB) | **Increase** | Increase | Strong increase | Friction at flank |
| Coolant usage | Moderate decrease | Decrease | Moderate decrease | Thermal softening reduction |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: 3-Axis Cutting Force Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Workpiece Rotating on Lathe Spindle] --> B[Single Point Cutting Tool Engages]
    B --> C[3 Component Tool Dynamometer Mounted Below Tool Holder]
    C --> D1[Strain Gauge Channel X Tangential Fx]
    C --> D2[Strain Gauge Channel Y Feed Fy]
    C --> D3[Strain Gauge Channel Z Radial Fz]
    D1 --> E1[Wheatstone Bridge Amplifier X]
    D2 --> E2[Wheatstone Bridge Amplifier Y]
    D3 --> E3[Wheatstone Bridge Amplifier Z]
    E1 --> F[Multichannel Data Acquisition System DAq]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Digital Signal Processor Calibration Constants kx ky kz]
    G --> H1[Display Fx Fc in Newtons]
    G --> H2[Display Fy Ff in Newtons]
    G --> H3[Display Fz Fr in Newtons]
    H1 --> I[Post Processing Resultant R Power SCE]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Merchant Circle Plot and SCE Tabulation]
```

## 4.2 Process Logic: Axis vs 3-Axis Force Measurement Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Axis 2 Component Force Measurement] --> B1[Measures Fc and Ff only]
    A1 --> C1[Uses single strain gauge bridge or simple dynamometer]
    A1 --> D1[Output: 2 force components plus Rxy]
    A1 --> E1[Sufficient for: Power estimation and basic analysis]
    A2[3 Axis 3 Component Force Measurement] --> B2[Measures Fc Ff and Fr simultaneously]
    A2 --> C2[Uses multi component strain gauge dynamometer]
    A2 --> D2[Output: 3 force components plus full R]
    A2 --> E2[Sufficient for: Merchant Circle Shear Angle and full FEA validation]
    B1 --> F[KTU Lab Decision]
    B2 --> F
    F --> G1[If only power and feed force needed: Choose Axis]
    F --> G2[If chip formation or tool wear study needed: Choose 3 Axis]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Merchant Force Circle Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Merchant_Force_Circle[Merchant Force Circle Components]
        M1[Center O of Circle] --> M2[Vector Rc along X axis cutting velocity]
        M1 --> M3[Vector Rf along Y axis feed direction]
        M1 --> M4[Vector Rr along Z axis radial]
        M2 --> M5[Angle alpha between Rc and Resultant R]
        M3 --> M5
        M4 --> M6[Angle delta between Rxy and Resultant R]
        M5 --> M7[Resultant R as diagonal of force parallelepiped]
        M6 --> M7
        M7 --> M8[Friction force F along tool chip interface]
        M7 --> M9[Normal force N perpendicular to tool chip interface]
        M8 --> M10[Friction angle beta = tan inverse of F over N]
        M9 --> M10
    end
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Experimental Setup Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Step 1 Machine Preparation] --> S2[Mount Workpiece and Set Cutting Parameters]
    S2 --> S3[Step 2 Tool Installation on Dynamometer]
    S3 --> S4[Step 3 Calibration Apply Known Loads and Record Voltage]
    S4 --> S5[Step 4 Zero Balance the DAq System]
    S5 --> S6[Step 5 Run Trial Cut at Low Parameters]
    S6 --> S7[Step 6 Record Fc Ff Fr for Set 1]
    S7 --> S8[Step 7 Vary Feed and Depth Record Sets 2 3 4]
    S8 --> S9[Step 8 Calculate R alpha Power and SCE]
    S9 --> S10[Step 9 Plot F vs f and F vs d Graphs]
    S10 --> S11[Step 10 Plot Merchant Circle and Tabulate Results]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** In the schematic above, each rectangular block represents a **functional stage** in the measurement chain. The dynamometer (Block C) is the heart of the system — it transduces mechanical force into a proportional electrical voltage via **piezoresistive strain gauges** bonded to elastic sensing elements.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define 3-axis cutting force measurement. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer:**
3-axis cutting force measurement is the simultaneous experimental determination of the three mutually perpendicular components of the resultant cutting force — namely the **principal cutting force F<sub>c</sub>** (along cutting velocity), the **feed force F<sub>f</sub>** (along feed direction), and the **radial force F<sub>r</sub>** (perpendicular to the machined surface) — using a multi-component **tool dynamometer** equipped with calibrated strain gauges in three orthogonal axes. The resultant is given by $R = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_f^2 + F_r^2}$.

### Question 2: Distinguish between axis and 3-axis cutting force measurement. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Axis (2-Component) | 3-Axis (3-Component) |
|---|---|---|
| **Components Measured** | F<sub>c</sub> and F<sub>f</sub> | F<sub>c</sub>, F<sub>f</sub>, and F<sub>r</sub> |
| **Dynamometer Type** | Simple 2-channel dynamometer | Multi-component tool dynamometer |
| **Cost & Complexity** | Low | High |
| **Application** | Power and feed-drive analysis | Merchant Circle, FEA, chatter, tool wear studies |
| **Resultant** | $R_{xy} = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_f^2}$ | $R = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_f^2 + F_r^2}$ |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A: (a) Explain the working principle of a 3-component strain gauge tool dynamometer with a neat block diagram. (7 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Construction (3 marks):**
A 3-component strain gauge tool dynamometer consists of a **rigid cylindrical or octagonal tool-holding body** machined from a high-strength elastic material (typically **nickel-chrome steel or maraging steel**). At least **eight strain gauges** are bonded to the body at strategic locations: four gauges measure the **tangential force F<sub>c</sub>** (mounted perpendicular to the cutting velocity), two gauges measure the **feed force F<sub>f</sub>**, and two gauges measure the **radial force F<sub>r</sub>**. The gauges are wired in **Wheatstone bridge** configurations to maximize sensitivity and compensate for temperature variations.

**(ii) Working Principle (3 marks):**
When the cutting tool engages the workpiece, the three force components deform the dynamometer body. Each deformation produces a **microscopic strain (ε)** in the bonded gauges, which changes their **electrical resistance (R)** according to the gauge law:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon
$$

where $G_f$ is the **gauge factor** (typically **2.0 – 2.2** for metallic gauges). The change in resistance unbalances the Wheatstone bridge, producing a **proportional output voltage** that is amplified and converted to a force value using a pre-determined calibration constant $k$ (N/V).

**(iii) Block Diagram Description (1 mark):**
Cutting tool → Tool dynamometer (strain gauges) → Bridge excitation → Amplifier → DAq system → PC display of F<sub>c</sub>, F<sub>f</sub>, F<sub>r</sub>.

### Question A: (b) A turning operation on mild steel with HSS tool gives the following dynamometer readings: F<sub>c</sub> = 1200 N, F<sub>f</sub> = 450 N, F<sub>r</sub> = 380 N. Cutting speed = 90 m/min, feed = 0.2 mm/rev, depth of cut = 2 mm. Calculate the resultant force, inclination angle, spindle power, and specific cutting energy. (7 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Resultant Force**

$$
R = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_f^2 + F_r^2} = \sqrt{1200^2 + 450^2 + 380^2}
$$

$$
R = \sqrt{1{,}440{,}000 + 202{,}500 + 144{,}400} = \sqrt{1{,}786{,}900} \approx 1336.7 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2: Inclination Angle**

$$
\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{F_f}{F_c}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{450}{1200}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.375) \approx 20.56°
$$

**Step 3: Spindle Power (convert $v_c$ to m/s)**

$$
v_c = \frac{90}{60} = 1.5 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
P = F_c \cdot v_c = 1200 \times 1.5 = 1800 \text{ W} = 1.8 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4: Specific Cutting Energy**

$$
SCE = \frac{F_c}{f \cdot d} = \frac{1200}{0.2 \times 2} = \frac{1200}{0.4} = 3000 \text{ N·mm}^{-2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $R \approx 1336.7$ N, $\alpha \approx 20.56°$, $P = 1.8$ kW, $SCE = 3000$ N·mm⁻². The high SCE indicates a relatively hard workpiece-tool combination, consistent with mild steel + HSS.

---

### Question B: (a) With a neat sketch, describe the procedure to measure cutting forces in a turning operation using a 3-axis dynamometer. List the precautions to be taken. (7 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Apparatus Required (2 marks):**
Lathe machine, 3-component tool dynamometer, single-point cutting tool, mild steel workpiece, charge amplifier, multi-channel DAq system, personal computer with logging software, dial indicator, micrometer, and vernier caliper.

**(ii) Procedure (4 marks):**
1. **Mount the workpiece** securely in the lathe chuck and check runout using a dial indicator (runout < 0.02 mm).
2. **Install the dynamometer** on the tool post and mount the cutting tool on the dynamometer's tool holder; ensure the tool tip is at the correct centre height.
3. **Calibrate the dynamometer** by applying known weights (e.g., 100 N, 500 N, 1000 N) in each of the three directions and recording the corresponding voltage outputs. Compute calibration constants $k_x$, $k_y$, $k_z$ in N/V.
4. **Balance the DAq system** to zero all three channels with no load.
5. **Set cutting parameters** ($v_c$, $f$, $d$) for Trial 1 and engage the tool; record steady-state values of F<sub>c</sub>, F<sub>f</sub>, F<sub>r</sub> over a 5-second window.
6. **Repeat** the experiment for at least 3 different combinations of feed and depth of cut, keeping $v_c$ constant.
7. **Compute** $R$, $\alpha$, $P$, and $SCE$ for each trial and tabulate results.
8. **Plot** the variation of F<sub>c</sub> with feed and depth of cut.

**(iii) Precautions (1 mark):**
- Ensure dynamometer is rigidly mounted to avoid vibration artefacts.
- Tool overhang should be minimized.
- Avoid BUE regime (use coolant and proper speed range).
- Zero the system immediately before each cut.

### Question B: (b) Explain the concept of Merchant's force circle and derive the expression for the shear angle. (7 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Concept of Merchant's Force Circle (3 marks):**
The Merchant's force circle is a geometric vector diagram that represents the **equilibrium of the resultant cutting force R** at the tool–chip interface. The circle has its center at the origin, with R represented as a chord. The friction force F and the normal force N are resolved along and perpendicular to the tool–chip contact (rake face). The angle between R and N is the **friction angle β**, satisfying $\tan\beta = F/N = \mu$ (coefficient of friction). The shear angle φ is the angle between the shear plane and the cutting velocity direction.

**(ii) Derivation of Shear Angle (4 marks):**
From the equilibrium of forces on the shear plane:

$$
F_s = R \cos(\phi + \beta - \gamma)
$$

and on the rake face:

$$
F = R \sin\beta, \qquad N = R \cos\beta
$$

The shear plane area is:

$$
A_s = \frac{A_0}{\sin\phi}
$$

where $A_0 = f \cdot d$ is the uncut chip cross-section. The shear force and shear stress are:

$$
\tau_s = \frac{F_s}{A_s}
$$

Applying Merchant's **minimum energy criterion** ($d\tau_s/d\phi = 0$) and using $\tan\beta = F/N$:

$$
\frac{d}{d\phi}\left[\frac{R \cos(\phi + \beta - \gamma) \sin\phi}{A_0}\right] = 0
$$

After differentiation and trigonometric simplification:

$$
\phi = 45° + \frac{\gamma}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}
$$

This is the **Merchant shear angle equation**, valid for orthogonal cutting with continuous chip formation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to convert the cutting speed from **m/min to m/s** when computing the spindle power. A 60× numerical error can cost **2 full marks**. Always double-check your units before final submission. Also, do not forget to mention the **gauge factor (G<sub>f</sub>)** in any question about strain gauge working — omitting it is a common 1-mark deduction.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three force components:** F<sub>c</sub> (tangential, largest), F<sub>f</sub> (feed, smallest in turning), F<sub>r</sub> (radial, dominates in drilling/milling).
- **Resultant:** $R = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_f^2 + F_r^2}$ — must be in **Newtons**.
- **Inclination angle:** $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(F_f / F_c)$, normally between **15° – 30°** in turning.
- **Spindle power:** $P = F_c \cdot v_c$ with $v_c$ in **m/s** for SI watts.
- **SCE:** $SCE = F_c / (f \cdot d)$ in **N·mm⁻²**.
- **Merchant's shear angle:** $\phi = 45° + \gamma/2 - \beta/2$.
- **Dynamometer types:** Mechanical (deflection + dial gauge), Hydraulic (pressure gauge), **Piezoelectric** (charge output), **Strain gauge** (voltage output — most common in KTU labs).
- **Calibration equation:** $F = k \cdot V_{out}$ where $k$ is the **calibration constant in N/V**.
- **Cutting force increases with:** feed, depth of cut, tool wear, and **decreases with:** higher cutting speed, larger positive rake angle, and effective cooling.
- **Always state units** in numerical answers — losing 0.5 mark for missing units is the most common KTU deduction.
- **Use the correct trigonometric identities** when converting between Merchant and orthogonal force components.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non Destructive Testing of Components - Dye Penetrant test, Magnetic Crack detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL408 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Non Destructive Testing of Components - Dye Penetrant test, Magnetic Crack detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) — Foundational Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** is a group of analytical and physical techniques used in engineering to evaluate the **integrity, properties, composition, or geometry** of a material, component, or assembly **without causing damage** or altering its end-use serviceability. NDT allows 100% inspection of critical components, unlike destructive sampling.

In KTU 2024 Scheme, the Production Engineering Lab (PCPEL408) treats NDT as a **pillar of Quality Assurance (QA)**, where components manufactured via casting, forging, welding, or machining are screened for hidden defects before being placed in service.

### 1.2 The Two NDT Methods in This Experiment

| # | Method | Core Detection Principle | Defect Type Revealed |
|---|--------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Dye Penetrant Test (DPT)** | Capillary action of a liquid dye into surface-breaking discontinuities | **Surface-breaking** defects only |
| 2 | **Magnetic Particle Test (MPT)** | Distortion of magnetic flux lines at defect boundaries | **Surface & near-surface (subsurface)** defects |

> [!NOTE]
> **Defect vs. Discontinuity**: A *discontinuity* is any interruption in the physical structure of a material (may be intentional, e.g., a keyway). A *defect* is a discontinuity that **degrades the intended function** of the component. NDT detects discontinuities; engineering judgment classifies them as defects.

---

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

#### Analogy A — Dye Penetrant Test (DPT)
> Imagine you spill a drop of **bright red ink** on a piece of paper that has a hairline cut on its surface. The ink **slowly seeps into the cut** and stains it from inside. When you wipe the surface, the cut remains **dyed red**, making it visible. This is exactly how DPT works: the penetrant (ink) is drawn into a defect (cut) by capillary action, and the developer (paper/white background) reveals the stain.

#### Analogy B — Magnetic Particle Test (MPT)
> Imagine a **magnetic field** as an evenly flowing stream of water in a smooth, straight canal. If a **rock (defect)** lies in the canal, the water flows *around* the rock and creates **ripples and turbulence**. In MPT, iron particles (visual tracers) cluster at the boundaries of the defect, just like foam collects at the edges of the rock — making the hidden flaw **visible to the naked eye**.

---

### 1.4 Standard Reference Codes (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always cite the **ASTM / IS standard** in your record while writing the lab observation. The examiner expects the standard code along with the procedure.

| Method | Indian Standard | International Standard |
|--------|-----------------|------------------------|
| Dye Penetrant Test | **IS 3658** | ASTM E1417 |
| Magnetic Particle Test | **IS 5334** | ASTM E1444 |
| NDT — General Vocabulary | IS 13816 | ASTM E1316 |

---

### 1.5 Why These Two Methods Are Taught Together in PCPEL408

- Both are **low-cost, portable, fast** screening methods.
- They cover complementary defect geometries: DPT for **non-magnetic** materials (austenitic SS, aluminum, brass); MPT for **ferromagnetic** materials (carbon steel, low-alloy steel, cast iron).
- Together they teach the student the **physical principles** of defect detection: *capillarity* vs. *magnetic permeability contrast*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
