## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Dye Penetrant Test (DPT) — Operating Principle

DPT exploits **capillary action** (also called *wicking*). When a low-viscosity, high-wetting liquid (penetrant) is applied to a clean surface, it is drawn into surface-breaking discontinuities by the pressure differential created in the small capillary volume of the crack.

The governing physical relationship is the **Jurin's Law** of capillary rise:

$$h = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

Where:
- $h$ = height of liquid column risen into the crack (depth of penetrant penetration)
- $\gamma$ = surface tension of the penetrant liquid (N/m)
- $\theta$ = contact angle between penetrant and the solid surface
- $\rho$ = density of the penetrant (kg/m³)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $r$ = effective radius of the defect (m)

> [!NOTE]
> For maximum penetration depth $h$, the penetrant should have **high $\gamma$** (surface tension) and **low $\theta$** (good wetting — i.e., $\cos\theta \to 1$). This is why dye penetrants are formulated with low surface tension and contain surfactants to wet even oily surfaces.

---

### 2.2 DPT — Six Sequential Stages (KTU Board Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners will award full marks **only** if all six stages are mentioned in the correct order with dwell times. Memorize the order: **Clean → Apply → Dwell → Remove → Develop → Inspect**.

| Step | Stage | Function | Typical Parameter |
|------|-------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Pre-Cleaning** | Remove oil, dirt, paint, scale so penetrant can wet the bare surface | Solvent wipe, alkaline cleaning, vapor degreasing |
| 2 | **Penetrant Application** | Coat the surface evenly with penetrant | Spray, brush, dip, or electrostatic |
| 3 | **Dwell Time (Penetration)** | Allow capillary action to fill defects | **≥ 10 minutes** (typically 10–30 min) |
| 4 | **Excess Penetrant Removal** | Remove surface penetrant without removing penetrant trapped in defects | Water spray, solvent wipe, or emulsifier + water |
| 5 | **Developer Application** | Apply a dry or wet developer that draws penetrant *out* of defects by reverse capillary action (bleed-out) | Dry powder, aqueous, or non-aqueous (solvent-suspended) |
| 6 | **Inspection & Evaluation** | Examine under white light (visible) or UV-A light (fluorescent) | Magnification, comparison with ASTM E433 reference photographs |

---

### 2.3 DPT — Classification of Penetrant Systems

| Family | Removal Mechanism | Sensitivity | Typical Use |
|--------|-------------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Water-Washable** | Direct water rinse | Low–Medium | Field inspection, rough surfaces |
| **Post-Emulsifiable** | Emulsifier + water rinse | **High** | Aerospace, critical welds |
| **Solvent-Removable** | Solvent wipe | Medium | Spot checks, in-situ repair shops |

| Type | Light Source | Sensitivity | Background |
|------|--------------|-------------|------------|
| **Visible (Red Dye)** | White light (~500 lux) | Lower | White developer needed |
| **Fluorescent** | UV-A (365 nm, 1000 µW/cm²) | **Highest** (5–10× visible) | Dark booth required |

> [!TIP]
> Fluorescent penetrant under UV-A is **5 to 10 times more sensitive** than visible red dye. KTU board questions often ask: "Which is more sensitive — visible or fluorescent?" The answer is **fluorescent**.

---

### 2.4 Magnetic Particle Test (MPT) — Operating Principle

When a ferromagnetic component is magnetized, magnetic flux lines travel through the metal. If a defect (crack, void, inclusion) interrupts the flux path:

1. The flux lines **leak out** into the air around the defect (flux leakage).
2. The leakage field exerts a **magnetic force** on the applied iron particles.
3. Iron particles **accumulate** at the defect edges, forming a visible indication.

> The principle of flux leakage is identical to how a **seismograph** detects underground voids — the wave/flux *bends* around the obstacle, and the boundary becomes detectable.

---

### 2.5 MPT — Five Sequential Stages (KTU Board Definition)

| Step | Stage | Function |
|------|-------|----------|
| 1 | **Pre-Cleaning** | Remove oil, paint, scale, plating so defects are open to surface |
| 2 | **Magnetization** | Apply magnetic field using a yoke, head shot, coil, or central conductor |
| 3 | **Application of Particles** | Apply dry powder or wet suspension while current is flowing (continuous method) or after current is cut (residual method) |
| 4 | **Inspection** | Observe indications under white light (visible) or UV-A (fluorescent particles); interpret pattern (linear, round, clustering) |
| 5 | **Demagnetization** | Use AC decaying field or reversing DC to bring residual magnetism to near zero |

---

### 2.6 MPT — Two Magnetization Methods

| Method | When Particles Are Applied | Material Condition | Best For |
|--------|----------------------------|---------------------|----------|
| **Continuous Method** | **While** current is flowing | Material must be at full saturation | Surface defects, low-permeability parts |
| **Residual Method** | **After** current is cut | Material must have high retentivity (e.g., hardened steel) | Subsurface defects, batch inspection |

---

### 2.7 MPT — Two Directional Magnetization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> To detect a defect, the **magnetic flux must be perpendicular (90°) to the defect's longest axis**. Hence, a single inspection requires **two magnetization directions** — longitudinal and circular — to avoid missing defects oriented parallel to the field.

| Direction | Field Geometry | Created By | Detects Defects Oriented |
|-----------|----------------|------------|--------------------------|
| **Longitudinal** | Flux runs along the length of the part | Coil (solenoid) wrap | Transverse cracks (circumferential) |
| **Circular** | Flux loops around the part | Central conductor or current shot | Longitudinal cracks (axial) |

---

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula & Parameter Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every key relationship, parameter, and ASTM/IS threshold a KTU 2024 lab student must know for viva and record.

| # | Parameter / Formula | Symbol / Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---------------------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Jurin's Law (capillary rise) | $h = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ | m — depth of penetrant penetration |
| 2 | DPT Dwell Time | $t_d \geq 10$ | minutes (typically 10–30 min) |
| 3 | DPT Inspection Temperature | $15$ to $52$ | °C (per ASTM E1417) |
| 4 | DPT Developer Drying Time | $\leq 10$ | minutes (after wet developer) |
| 5 | UV-A Wavelength for Fluorescent DPT | $365$ | nm (peak intensity) |
| 6 | UV-A Intensity at Surface | $\geq 1000$ | µW/cm² |
| 7 | Magnetic Flux Density (operating) | $B \geq 0.7$ to $0.8$ | Tesla (typical saturation range for carbon steel) |
| 8 | MPT Magnetizing Current (circular shot) | $I = \frac{H \cdot d}{N}$ | A — for prod spacing $d$ |
| 9 | Tangential Field Strength | $H = \frac{NI}{\pi d}$ | A/m — for coil magnetization |
| 10 | Sensitivity Enhancement Factor (fluorescent vs visible) | $5 \text{ to } 10$ | $\times$ — relative brightness |
| 11 | Standard sensitivity test block | ASTM E433 / ASME Sec V Art 7 | Reference for DPT |
| 12 | Standard reference block for MPT | MTU 1, MTU 2, MTU 3 (Kobe or AISI ring) | Reference for MPT |

---

### 2.9 Comparative Decision Matrix — DPT vs MPT

| Criterion | DPT | MPT |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Material Applicability | **Any non-porous** material (ferrous, non-ferrous, non-magnetic) | **Only ferromagnetic** materials |
| Defect Type | Surface-breaking only (open to surface) | Surface **and** shallow subsurface (≤ 6 mm depth) |
| Sensitivity to Fine Cracks | **Very high** (esp. fluorescent) | High for ferromagnetic, but masked by surface coatings |
| Pre-Cleaning Requirement | **Critical** (oil blocks penetrant) | Critical (oil traps particles) |
| Equipment Cost | Low (spray can, UV lamp) | Moderate (yoke, prods, demagnetizer) |
| Demagnetization Needed | No | **Yes — mandatory** for parts going back into service |
| Portability | Highly portable | Portable with car battery or yoke |
| Operator Skill | Low–medium | Medium (must interpret flux patterns) |
| KTU Viva Question: "Which is more sensitive?" | **Fluorescent DPT is most sensitive** for surface defects | — |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since PCPEL408 is a **laboratory course**, the "derivations" here are the **logical procedures, inspection logic, and decision trees** that the student must write in their lab record. These replace the heavy mathematical derivations of theory courses and are equally mark-yielding in the KTU board evaluation.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why Dwell Time is the Most Critical Variable in DPT

**Starting Premise (Jurin's Law):**

$$h(t) = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r} \cdot \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)$$

Where $\tau$ is the **capillary time constant** of the system, depending on the viscosity $\mu$ of the penetrant:

$$\tau = \frac{8 \mu L}{\rho g r^2}$$

**Step 1 — Define the Problem:**
The depth of penetration $h(t)$ of penetrant into a defect of effective radius $r$ grows over time. The student must explain *why* a minimum dwell time is mandated by ASTM E1417.

**Step 2 — Steady-State Depth:**
As $t \to \infty$, the exponential term $e^{-t/\tau} \to 0$, so:

$$h_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} h(t) = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

This is the **maximum possible penetration depth** for a defect of radius $r$.

**Step 3 — Time to Reach 95% Penetration:**
For practical inspection, we want $h(t) \geq 0.95 \, h_{ss}$:

$$0.95 \, h_{ss} = h_{ss}\left(1 - e^{-t_{95}/\tau}\right) \implies e^{-t_{95}/\tau} = 0.05$$

$$t_{95} = -\tau \ln(0.05) = 2.996 \, \tau \approx 3\tau$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Decision Rule:**
> **The dwell time must be at least $3\tau$** to ensure 95% of the maximum depth is filled with penetrant. ASTM E1417 mandates a minimum of **10 minutes** to safely cover the typical $\tau$ values of commercial penetrants on defects of engineering interest.

**Step 5 — Engineering Consequence:**
If dwell time is too short, penetrant only partially fills the defect. During bleed-out, the indication is **weak or absent**, leading to a **false negative** — a critical safety failure in NDT.

**Summary Equation:**

$$t_{\text{dwell, min}} \geq 3\tau \quad \text{where} \quad \tau = \frac{8\mu L}{\rho g r^2}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Why Demagnetization is Mandatory After MPT

**Step 1 — Physical Setup:**
After magnetization, the ferromagnetic component retains a **residual magnetic field** $B_r$ equal to:

$$B_r = \mu_0 (H_{applied} - N \cdot M)$$

Where $N$ is the demagnetization factor (geometry-dependent) and $M$ is the magnetization.

**Step 2 — Service Consequence:**
- Residual magnetism attracts **ferrous chips** → bearing failure, instrument error.
- Welding arcs (HIG) deflect → poor weld geometry.
- Sensitive electronics (avionics, gyros) malfunction.

**Step 3 — Demagnetization Principle:**
A decaying alternating field $H(t) = H_0 e^{-t/\tau_d}$ is applied while the part is slowly withdrawn from the coil. The hysteresis loop is traced repeatedly with shrinking amplitude, ending at the origin of the $B$–$H$ curve:

$$\oint H \, dB \to 0 \quad \text{as} \quad H_0 \to 0$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Rule:**
> Per ASTM E1444, residual field after demagnetization must be **$\leq 3$ Gauss (0.3 mT)** for general parts, and **$\leq 1$ Gauss** for instrument-grade parts.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Defect Interpretation

| Indication Pattern | Probable Defect | Method Most Likely |
|--------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Single, sharp, **linear** red/fluorescent line | **Crack** (fatigue, grinding, stress) | DPT or MPT |
| Cluster of small round spots | **Porosity / gas pockets** | DPT |
| Aligned round indications in a row | **Stringer of inclusions** | MPT |
| Sharp indication perpendicular to weld axis | **Undercut, crater crack** | DPT |
| Indication only on one magnetization direction | **Defect oriented parallel to one field** | MPT — need second direction |
| Broad, fuzzy indication | **Subsurface defect** (≥ 1 mm deep) | MPT only |
| Faint indication that disappears after re-cleaning | **Residual penetrant (false indication)** | DPT — improve cleaning |
| Indication visible at corners of a weld | **Lack of fusion, porosity at root** | DPT |

---

### 3.4 Procedural Decision Tree — Selecting DPT vs MPT

**Question: Is the part magnetic?**
- **No (austenitic SS, aluminum, brass, titanium, plastic)** → Use **DPT only**. MPT is impossible.
- **Yes (carbon steel, low-alloy steel, cast iron, martensitic SS)** → Continue.

**Question: Is the defect expected to be only on the surface?**
- **Yes, open crack** → Both DPT and MPT work; **DPT is simpler**.
- **Possibly subsurface** → Use **MPT** (residual magnetization method).

**Question: Is the part in service and must not be contaminated?**
- **Yes (aerospace, nuclear)** → Use **fluorescent DPT** with low-sulfur, low-halogen penetrant.

**Question: Is the geometry complex (recesses, blind holes)?**
- **Yes** → Use **post-emulsifiable DPT** to avoid penetrant entrapment.

---

### 3.5 Practical Limitations of Both Methods (KTU Board Frequently Tested)

| Limitation | DPT | MPT |
|------------|-----|-----|
| Cannot detect **subsurface** defects beyond ~ 1–2 mm | ✗ | Partially (up to ~ 6 mm with high current) |
| Cannot detect defects **not open to surface** | ✗ | ✗ |
| Surface coatings (paint, plating) must be **removed first** | Required | Required |
| **Rough or porous** surfaces cause false indications | Major problem | Minor problem |
| Inspection of **parts in service** at high temperature | Not feasible | Not feasible |
| Operator must be **certified (Level I/II/III)** per IS 13816 | Required | Required |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Flow Diagram — Six Stages of Dye Penetrant Test (DPT)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Component Received] --> B[Pre-Cleaning - Remove Oil, Dirt, Scale]
    B --> C[Apply Penetrant - Spray or Dip]
    C --> D[Dwell Time - 10 to 30 minutes - Capillary Action Fills Defects]
    D --> E[Excess Penetrant Removal - Water Spray or Solvent Wipe]
    E --> F[Apply Developer - White Powder or Spray]
    F --> G[Drying of Developer - 1 to 10 minutes]
    G --> H[Inspection - White Light or UV-A 365 nm]
    H --> I{Defect Indication Found?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Record Indication - Type, Size, Location]
    I -->|No| K[Mark Component as Accepted]
    J --> L[Post-Cleaning - Remove Developer]
    K --> L
    L --> M[End: Report Generation per ASTM E1417 or IS 3658]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Flow Diagram — Magnetic Particle Test (MPT)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Ferromagnetic Component] --> B[Pre-Cleaning - Degrease and Descale]
    B --> C[Stage 1: Magnetize in Direction 1 - Longitudinal or Circular]
    C --> D[Apply Magnetic Particles While Field is ON - Continuous Method]
    D --> E[Inspect Indications - White or UV Light]
    E --> F[Demagnetize Field 1]
    F --> G[Stage 2: Magnetize in Direction 2 - Perpendicular to Direction 1]
    G --> H[Apply Magnetic Particles Again]
    H --> I[Inspect Indications]
    I --> J[Final Demagnetization - AC Decay to less than 3 Gauss]
    J --> K[Record and Report per ASTM E1444 or IS 5334]
```

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Choice of NDT Method Based on Defect Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Component to be Inspected] --> Q1{Is the Material Ferromagnetic?}
    Q1 -->|No - Al, Austenitic SS, Plastic| DPT[Use DPT Only]
    Q1 -->|Yes - Carbon Steel, Cast Iron, Low Alloy SS| Q2{Is the Defect Surface Breaking or Subsurface?}
    Q2 -->|Surface Breaking Only| Both[Both DPT and MPT Applicable - Prefer DPT for Simplicity]
    Q2 -->|Possibly Subsurface| MPT[Use MPT with Residual Method]
    Q2 -->|Unknown - First Time Inspection| Both2[Apply Both Methods in Sequence]
    DPT --> Result1[Indication: Linear Sharp Line equals Crack, Round Cluster equals Porosity]
    Both --> Result1
    MPT --> Result2[Indication: Pattern Shows Defect Orientation and Depth]
    Both2 --> Result1
    Both2 --> Result2
```

---

### 4.4 Schematic — Capillary Action Inside a Crack (DPT Principle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Crack Opening at Surface] --> B[Penetrant Liquid on Top Surface]
    B --> C[Capillary Force F equals 2 gamma cos theta divided by r]
    C --> D[Liquid Rises Into Crack - Depth h over Dwell Time t]
    D --> E[After Excess Removal - Crack Contains Trapped Penetrant]
    E --> F[Developer Applied - Reverse Capillary Action Draws Penetrant Out]
    F --> G[Visible or Fluorescent Indication on White Developer Background]
```

---

### 4.5 Schematic — Magnetic Flux Leakage at a Defect (MPT Principle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Magnetized Ferromagnetic Part] --> B[Uniform Flux Lines Inside Part]
    B --> C[Defect Present - Crack, Void, Inclusion]
    C --> D[Flux Lines Bypass the Defect]
    D --> E[Flux Leakage Field Emerges From Defect Edges]
    E --> F[Iron Particles Applied - Drift Toward Leakage Field]
    F --> G[Particle Accumulation Forms Visible Indication Outline of Defect]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often write **only 4 stages** in DPT (forgetting Developer Drying and Post-Cleaning). This is a **2-mark deduction** in the record.
> 2. Students confuse **longitudinal vs circular magnetization** — remember: **Coil = Longitudinal, Prods = Circular**.
> 3. Many students wrongly claim MPT works on **stainless steel** — only **ferromagnetic** materials. Austenitic SS is **non-magnetic** and is rejected by MPT.
> 4. Forgetting to mention **demagnetization** in MPT procedure costs marks — it is **mandatory** by ASTM E1444.
> 5. UV-A wavelength of **365 nm** is a KTU viva favorite — memorize it.

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Define Dye Penetrant Testing. List the six stages of DPT in correct order.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT) is a non-destructive testing method used to detect **surface-breaking discontinuities** in non-porous materials. It works on the principle of **capillary action**, where a low-viscosity liquid (penetrant) is drawn into surface defects and later revealed by a developer.

The six stages are:
1. **Pre-cleaning** of the test surface
2. **Application** of penetrant
3. **Dwell time** (penetration, ≥ 10 minutes)
4. **Excess penetrant removal**
5. **Application of developer**
6. **Inspection and evaluation** under white or UV-A light

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is the principle of Magnetic Particle Testing? State any two limitations of MPT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Principle:** When a ferromagnetic component is magnetized, magnetic flux lines travel through the metal. If a defect is present, the flux lines **leak out** at the defect boundaries, creating a leakage field. Iron particles applied to the surface are attracted to this leakage field and **accumulate at the defect edges**, forming a visible indication.

**Limitations (any two):**
1. Applicable **only to ferromagnetic materials** (e.g., carbon steel, cast iron); cannot inspect austenitic stainless steel, aluminum, or non-metals.
2. **Surface preparation is critical** — paint, oil, plating, or scale must be removed before inspection.
3. Demagnetization after inspection is **mandatory**; otherwise residual magnetism interferes with service performance.
4. Cannot detect defects that are **not open to the surface** if they are too deep (> 6 mm).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Explain the procedure of Dye Penetrant Testing with a neat flowchart. Discuss the difference between **visible** and **fluorescent** penetrant systems. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** What is Magnetic Particle Testing? Explain **longitudinal** and **circular** magnetization with diagrams. State the importance of demagnetization. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q3(a) [7 marks]**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
DPT is a surface NDT method that uses a liquid penetrant to detect open-to-surface defects by capillary action. It is governed by ASTM E1417 / IS 3658.

**2. Procedure with Flowchart (3 marks):**

```
Pre-cleaning → Apply penetrant → Dwell (10–30 min) →
Remove excess → Apply developer → Inspect → Post-clean
```

Each stage's function:

| Stage | Function |
|-------|----------|
| Pre-cleaning | Remove contaminants; allow penetrant to wet the surface |
| Penetrant application | Cover entire surface uniformly |
| Dwell time | Allow capillary action to fill defects |
| Removal | Remove surface penetrant without disturbing defect-trapped penetrant |
| Developer | Reverse capillary action draws penetrant back out (bleed-out) |
| Inspection | Visualize indications under appropriate light |

**3. Visible vs Fluorescent Penetrant (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Visible (Red Dye) | Fluorescent |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------|
| Color | Bright red | Yellow-green under UV-A |
| Light Source | White light (~ 500 lux) | UV-A (365 nm, ≥ 1000 µW/cm²) |
| Sensitivity | Lower | **5–10× higher** |
| Background needed | White developer | Dark booth, low ambient light |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Inspection speed | Faster setup | Slower (dark adaptation) |
| Typical use | Field, repair shops | Aerospace, nuclear, critical welds |

---

#### **Model Solution — Q3(b) [7 marks]**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
MPT is an NDT method for detecting **surface and shallow subsurface defects** in ferromagnetic materials. It uses the principle of **magnetic flux leakage** at defect boundaries.

**2. Longitudinal Magnetization (2 marks):**
In longitudinal magnetization, the magnetic flux runs **along the length** of the component. This is achieved by placing the part inside a **coil (solenoid)** and passing current through it. The flux lines enter at one end and exit at the other. **Defects oriented perpendicular to the axis** (i.e., transverse or circumferential cracks) produce flux leakage and are detected.

**3. Circular Magnetization (2 marks):**
In circular magnetization, the flux lines form **closed loops around the circumference** of the part. This is achieved by passing current **through the part itself** (central conductor) or by placing prods on the surface and passing current between them. **Defects oriented parallel to the axis** (longitudinal or axial cracks) are detected.

> **Rule:** The defect must lie **perpendicular to the flux direction** to be detected. Hence, two perpendicular magnetization directions are required for complete inspection.

**4. Importance of Demagnetization (2 marks):**
After MPT, the part retains **residual magnetism** that can:
- Attract ferrous debris to bearing surfaces → wear and failure.
- Interfere with **arc blow** during subsequent welding.
- Disturb sensitive instruments (compasses, gyros, electronics).

Demagnetization is performed using an **AC decaying field**; the part is slowly withdrawn from a coil carrying a current that decays sinusoidally to zero. ASTM E1444 specifies residual field **≤ 3 Gauss** for general parts and **≤ 1 Gauss** for instrument-grade parts.

---

### 5.3 PART B — Alternative Choice (Question B)

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** List the different types of penetrant systems used in DPT. Compare **water-washable**, **post-emulsifiable**, and **solvent-removable** penetrants. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compare Dye Penetrant Testing and Magnetic Particle Testing on the basis of **material applicability, defect type detected, sensitivity, equipment, and necessity of demagnetization**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Q4(a) [7 marks]**

**Three Types of Penetrant Systems (1 mark each, total 3 marks):**

1. **Water-Washable Penetrants** — Direct water rinsing removes excess. Simplest; suitable for rough surfaces and field use.
2. **Post-Emulsifiable Penetrants** — An emulsifier must be applied before water rinse. Provides **highest sensitivity** by preventing over-washing. Used in aerospace.
3. **Solvent-Removable Penetrants** — Removed with a solvent wipe. Best for **spot checks** and portable in-situ inspection.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Property | Water-Washable | Post-Emulsifiable | Solvent-Removable |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Removal | Direct water | Emulsifier + water | Solvent wipe |
| Sensitivity | Low | **High** | Medium |
| Over-wash risk | High (may wash out defect penetrant) | Low (controlled) | Low |
| Cost | Lowest | Highest | Medium |
| Operator skill | Low | High (emulsifier timing) | Low |
| Typical application | Field, rough forgings | Aerospace, nuclear welds | Spot checks, repair |
| Standards | ASTM E1417 Type I/II | ASTM E1417 Type III/IV | ASTM E1417 Type I/II |

---

#### **Model Solution — Q4(b) [7 marks]**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Criterion | DPT | MPT |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Material applicability | Any non-porous (ferrous, non-ferrous, plastic) | **Only ferromagnetic** |
| Defect type | Surface-breaking only | Surface + subsurface (≤ 6 mm) |
| Sensitivity (surface) | Very high (fluorescent = highest) | High |
| Sensitivity (subsurface) | None | Moderate |
| Equipment | Penetrant, developer, UV lamp | Yoke/prods, magnetizer, demagnetizer |
| Pre-cleaning | Critical | Critical |
| Demagnetization | **Not required** | **Mandatory** |
| Operator certification | Level I/II (IS 13816) | Level I/II (IS 13816) |
| Cost | Low | Moderate |

**Conclusion (2 marks):**
- DPT is the **only choice** for non-magnetic materials (austenitic SS, aluminum).
- MPT is **more efficient** when the material is magnetic and the part is in production batches.
- For critical components, **both methods may be applied sequentially** to maximize defect detection confidence.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullet Points — Memorize Before the Lab Exam**

- [ ] **NDT** = inspection without damaging the part. Goal: 100% screening of critical components.
- [ ] **DPT** detects only **surface-breaking** defects. **MPT** detects **surface + shallow subsurface** defects.
- [ ] DPT works on the **principle of capillary action** (Jurin's Law: $h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$).
- [ ] MPT works on the **principle of magnetic flux leakage** at defect boundaries.
- [ ] DPT procedure: **Clean → Apply → Dwell (10–30 min) → Remove → Develop → Inspect** (6 stages).
- [ ] DPT minimum dwell time = **10 minutes**; reason = 95% penetration in ~ $3\tau$ (capillary time constant).
- [ ] **Fluorescent DPT** uses UV-A at **365 nm**, intensity **≥ 1000 µW/cm²**, in a **dark booth**.
- [ ] Fluorescent is **5–10× more sensitive** than visible red dye.
- [ ] Three penetrant families: **Water-washable, Post-emulsifiable, Solvent-removable** (last is most sensitive).
- [ ] MPT procedure: **Clean → Magnetize → Apply particles → Inspect → Demagnetize** (5 stages).
- [ ] MPT requires **two perpendicular magnetization directions** to detect all defect orientations.
- [ ] **Coil = longitudinal magnetization** (flux along length); **Prods = circular magnetization** (flux around part).
- [ ] **Continuous method**: particles applied while current is ON. **Residual method**: particles applied AFTER current is OFF; requires high retentivity.
- [ ] **Demagnetization** is **mandatory** in MPT; use **AC decaying field** to ≤ 3 Gauss (general) or ≤ 1 Gauss (instruments).
- [ ] DPT works on **all non-porous** materials. MPT works **only on ferromagnetic** materials.
- [ ] Austenitic stainless steel (e.g., 304, 316) is **non-magnetic** → use **DPT, not MPT**.
- [ ] ASTM standards: **E1417** for DPT, **E1444** for MPT, **E1316** for NDT terminology.
- [ ] IS standards: **IS 3658** for DPT, **IS 5334** for MPT, **IS 13816** for qualification.
- [ ] **ASTM E433** is the standard reference for evaluating DPT indications (line, dot, porosity patterns).
- [ ] The three classical NDT methods besides DPT and MPT are **Ultrasonic Testing (UT), Radiographic Testing (RT), and Eddy Current Testing (ET)**.
- [ ] In lab record, always cite the **standard code** alongside the procedure — this is a frequently awarded 1 mark.
- [ ] Common defect patterns: **Linear line = crack**, **Cluster of round spots = porosity**, **Aligned round spots = stringer of inclusions**, **Broad fuzzy = subsurface defect**.
- [ ] For MPT, the **defect must be perpendicular to the flux direction** to be detected; if not, increase current or rotate the part.
- [ ] Developer in DPT works by **reverse capillary action** (bleed-out) — drawing the trapped penetrant *out* of the defect onto the white background.
- [ ] Operator certification per **ASNT SNT-TC-1A** (international) or **IS 13816** (India) is mandatory for industrial NDT personnel.
- [ ] DPT is **not applicable** to porous materials (sintered parts, concrete) because the penetrant absorbs into the bulk, generating false indications.
- [ ] MPT **cannot detect** defects that are **parallel to the flux lines** and **too deep** (> 6 mm) below the surface.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acceptance Test of Machine tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL408 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Acceptance Test of Machine tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Acceptance Test** of a machine tool is a systematic, standardized procedure conducted after a machine tool is newly purchased, overhauled, or relocated, in order to verify that the machine conforms to the manufacturer's guaranteed accuracy specifications and the relevant national/international standards (primarily **ISO 230** series and **BIS IS 2063 / IS 7246**). The test evaluates three principal categories of machine behavior:

1. **Geometric Accuracy (Static Tests):** Squareness, parallelism, straightness, flatness, and roundness of machine elements when the machine is unloaded and idle.
2. **Positioning & Repeatability Accuracy:** The ability of slides and spindles to move to and return from commanded positions under no-load conditions.
3. **Working / Dynamic Accuracy (Cutting Tests):** The actual performance of the machine while producing a test piece — measuring surface finish, dimensional accuracy, and chatter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 scheme, Module Experiment 2 on Acceptance Tests emphasizes the *practical implementation* of these tests on a lathe, drilling machine, or milling machine, with calculations based on dial indicator readings, autocollimator readings, and test-bar readings.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you buy a brand-new car from a showroom. Before you drive it home, the dealer offers you a *test drive* and an *inspection*. You check:
- Are the headlights aligned? (Geometric check)
- Does the steering wheel come back to center accurately? (Repeatability)
- Does the engine run smoothly on a highway? (Performance under load)

An **Acceptance Test of a Machine Tool is exactly this — but for an industrial machine.**

- The **dial indicator** is the "speedometer" measuring tiny deviations (often in micrometers).
- The **cylindrical square / test mandrel** is the "alignment tool" used to detect squareness and parallelism.
- The **autocollimator** is the "laser eye" measuring angular deviations of slides.
- The **test piece / cut test** is the actual "test drive" — making a real cut and inspecting the workpiece.

If the machine fails any test beyond the **tolerance band** specified by the manufacturer, it is *rejected at acceptance*, and the supplier must rectify the defect before final payment is released. This protects the buyer (industry) from receiving a defective asset.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standards Cited:**
> - **ISO 230-1** (2022) — Geometric accuracy of machine tools under no-load conditions.
> - **ISO 230-2** — Determination of accuracy and repeatability of NC machine tool axes.
> - **ISO 1708** — Acceptance conditions for horizontal spindle horizontal axis milling machines.
> - **BIS IS 2063** — Acceptance tests for general purpose machine tools (India).
> - **Typical alignment tolerance:** **0.01 mm per 1000 mm** for precision machine tools.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Acceptance Tests

The KTU lab curriculum organizes acceptance tests into a strict hierarchy. Understanding the *order* of testing is crucial for board examinations.

### 2.1.1 Stage 1 — Pre-Installation Tests
- Visual inspection for damage in transit.
- Verification of accessories, manuals, lubrication units, and electricals.
- Foundation check (level, vibration isolation).

### 2.1.2 Stage 2 — Geometric / Static Accuracy Tests
Conducted **without load**, with the machine warmed up to operating temperature. These are the *most heavily tested* in the lab.

- **Test 1:** Flatness of the machine table / bed.
- **Test 2:** Straightness of guideways (in horizontal and vertical planes).
- **Test 3:** Squareness of the spindle axis to the table (X-Y plane).
- **Test 4:** Parallelism of spindle axis to table travel.
- **Test 5:** Spindle runout (radial and axial) — measured at the taper.
- **Test 6:** Concentricity of tailstock with spindle axis (lathe).

### 2.1.3 Stage 3 — Positioning & Repeatability Tests (NC machines)
- **Bi-directional Repeatability:** $R \uparrow = \vert x_{i\uparrow} - \bar{x}_{\uparrow} \vert$ and $R \downarrow = \vert x_{i\downarrow} - \bar{x}_{\downarrow} \vert$.
- **Unidirectional Repeatability:** $R = 4 \cdot s$, where $s$ is the standard deviation of unidirectional positions.
- **Axis Accuracy:** $A = \max(\bar{x}_{\uparrow} - \bar{x}_{\downarrow})$ over the travel.

### 2.1.4 Stage 4 — Working / Dynamic Accuracy (Cutting Test)
- A standard test piece is machined (e.g., a stepped bar on a lathe, or a square pocket on a milling machine).
- Inspect for: dimensional tolerance, surface roughness $R_a$ in **µm**, parallelism of cuts, absence of chatter.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Test

- **Flatness of bed** → If the bed is twisted or warped, the workpiece will geometrically inherit the error multiplied by its distance from the source.
- **Spindle runout** → Runout of 0.05 mm at the nose translates to ~0.10 mm diameter error at a tool 50 mm away — unacceptable for finishing.
- **Squareness of spindle to table** → A non-square spindle causes taper in drilled holes and misalignment in milling.
- **Squareness of tailstock to spindle (lathe)** → Affects taper turning of long shafts between centers.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|------|
| 1 | Total Indicated Reading (Runout) | $\text{TIR} = R_{\max} - R_{\min}$ | $R_{\max}, R_{\min}$ : max, min dial indicator reading | mm |
| 2 | Squareness error (using cylindrical square) | $\Delta = (R_{90} - R_{0}) / L$ | $R_{0}, R_{90}$ : readings at $0°$ and $90°$; $L$ : length of square | mm/mm |
| 3 | Angular squareness | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta L_{\text{actual}}}{L_{\text{reference}}}\right)$ | $\theta$ : deviation angle from 90° | rad / arc-sec |
| 4 | Parallelism error | $\delta_p = R_{\max} - R_{\min}$ over travel | $\delta_p$ : parallelism deviation | mm |
| 5 | Unidirectional repeatability | $R = 4s$ | $s$ : sample standard deviation | mm |
| 6 | Bi-directional backlash | $B = \bar{x}_{\uparrow} - \bar{x}_{\downarrow}$ | Mean position reversal error | mm |
| 7 | Straightness error (per unit length) | $e_s = \dfrac{\Delta h}{L}$ | $\Delta h$ : height deviation | mm/m |
| 8 | Surface roughness of test cut | $R_a = \dfrac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L}\vert y \vert\, dx$ | $y$ : deviation from mean line | µm |
| 9 | Level deviation (foundation) | $\theta_l = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2}{D}$ | $h_1, h_2$ : heights at two points; $D$ : distance | mm/m |
| 10 | Geometric Tolerance per ISO 230 | Permissible deviation $\leq$ $k \cdot L$ | $k$ : tolerance factor (e.g., 0.01); $L$ : travel length | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical note for tables:** When a numerical value or mathematical expression would normally contain a vertical bar (e.g., absolute value, set notation), the LaTeX commands `\vert` or `\mid` are used to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Acceptance testing is not merely academic — it is a **legal and commercial requirement** in the Indian engineering industry. Under BIS and ISO regulations:

- A **lathe manufacturer** in Coimbatore or Rajkot must guarantee **±0.01 mm/300 mm** parallelism of carriage to bed.
- A **CNC machining center** must demonstrate **positioning accuracy of ±5 µm** and **repeatability of ±2 µm** under ISO 230-2.
- **Aerospace and defense suppliers** (HAL, ISRO, DRDO vendors) require acceptance test certificates before any machine can produce flight-critical components.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Squareness Error Between Spindle Axis and Machine Table Using a Cylindrical Square

### 3.1.1 Apparatus and Setup
A precision **cylindrical square** (a hardened, ground cylindrical bar with two perpendicular reference faces $F_1$ and $F_2$) is clamped on the machine table. A **dial indicator** (least count **0.01 mm**) is mounted on the spindle such that its plunger rides against the cylindrical face of the square.

### 3.1.2 Experimental Procedure
1. The cylindrical square is set on the table and aligned approximately with the table edges.
2. The dial indicator plunger is made to contact the cylinder at point A, with the table at the **0° position** (initial).
3. The spindle is then *lowered* (vertical machine) or the table is *rotated 90°* about a vertical axis (horizontal machine) — record the reading $R_{90°}$.
4. Repeat for positions $180°$ and $270°$ to obtain $R_{180°}$ and $R_{270°}$.

### 3.1.3 Mathematical Derivation

Let the true perpendicular distance from the spindle axis to the table reference plane be $d_{\text{true}}$. If the spindle axis is *not* perpendicular to the table, then at the rotated angle $\phi$ from the reference, the distance measured by the dial indicator is:

$$
d(\phi) = d_{\text{true}} + L \cdot \sin(\theta) \cdot \cos(\phi - \alpha)
$$

where:
- $\theta$ is the **angular squareness error** (the angle by which the spindle deviates from true perpendicularity),
- $\alpha$ is the azimuth of the error axis,
- $L$ is the length from the spindle to the table reference.

At $\phi = 0°$ and $\phi = 90°$, the indicator readings $R_0$ and $R_{90}$ are proportional to $d(0)$ and $d(90)$. Therefore:

$$
R_{90} - R_0 = L \cdot \sin(\theta) \cdot \left[\cos(90° - \alpha) - \cos(\alpha)\right]
$$

Using the identity $\cos(90° - \alpha) = \sin(\alpha)$ and $\cos(\alpha) = \cos(\alpha)$:

$$
R_{90} - R_0 = L \cdot \sin(\theta) \cdot \left[\sin(\alpha) - \cos(\alpha)\right]
$$

By **symmetry of testing at $0°$ and $90°$**, the worst-case difference becomes:

$$
\Delta R_{\max} = \sqrt{2} \cdot L \cdot \sin(\theta)
$$

For small angles (machine tools are designed for $\theta$ very small, typically $< 0.001$ rad), $\sin(\theta) \approx \theta$ in radians, so:

$$
\theta = \dfrac{\Delta R_{\max}}{\sqrt{2} \cdot L}
$$

The **linear squareness error over a reference length $L$** is:

$$
\delta_{\text{sq}} = L \cdot \theta = \dfrac{\Delta R_{\max}}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

### 3.1.4 Simplified KTU Formula (Most Commonly Used in Lab)

In KTU practical records, the simplified form is universally adopted:

$$
\text{Squareness Error} = \dfrac{\vert R_{0°} - R_{90°} \vert + \vert R_{180°} - R_{270°} \vert}{2}
$$

expressed in mm per unit length of the cylindrical square.

### 3.1.5 Numerical Worked Example

> A cylindrical square of length $L = 300 \text{ mm}$ is used to test the squareness of a vertical spindle. Dial indicator readings are: $R_0 = 0.000$ mm, $R_{90} = 0.024$ mm, $R_{180} = 0.002$ mm, $R_{270} = 0.026$ mm. Find the angular and linear squareness errors.

**Step 1 — Compute the differences:**

$$
\Delta_1 = R_{90} - R_0 = 0.024 - 0.000 = 0.024 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\Delta_2 = R_{270} - R_{180} = 0.026 - 0.002 = 0.024 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Average the two differences (since they should ideally match if the error axis is aligned with the test direction):**

$$
\overline{\Delta} = \dfrac{0.024 + 0.024}{2} = 0.024 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Linear squareness error (over the length of the square):**

$$
\delta_{\text{sq}} = \overline{\Delta} = 0.024 \text{ mm per } 300 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Express per unit length:**

$$
\delta_{\text{sq}}^* = \dfrac{0.024}{300} = 8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mm/mm} = 0.08 \text{ mm/m}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to angular form:**

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{0.024}{300}\right) = \tan^{-1}(8 \times 10^{-5}) \approx 16.4 \text{ arc-seconds}
$$

**Step 6 — Acceptance verdict:** The manufacturer-specified tolerance for a medium-precision drilling machine is typically **0.03 mm / 300 mm**. The measured value (0.024 mm / 300 mm) is within tolerance → **ACCEPTED**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Spindle Radial Runout (Total Indicated Reading)

### 3.2.1 Setup
A short **test mandrel** is inserted into the spindle taper. A dial indicator is positioned such that its plunger touches the mandrel surface, perpendicular to the spindle axis. The spindle is rotated slowly by hand through 360° in steps of 30° or 45°.

### 3.2.2 Reading Table (Sample)

| Spindle Angle (°) | 0 | 45 | 90 | 135 | 180 | 225 | 270 | 315 | 360 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reading (mm) | 0.000 | 0.018 | 0.030 | 0.022 | 0.005 | 0.020 | 0.032 | 0.024 | 0.000 |

### 3.2.3 Calculation

**Step 1 — Identify max and min readings:**

$$
R_{\max} = 0.032 \text{ mm} \quad \text{at } 270°
$$

$$
R_{\min} = 0.000 \text{ mm} \quad \text{at } 0° \text{ and } 360°
$$

**Step 2 — Total Indicated Reading (TIR) = Runout:**

$$
\text{TIR} = R_{\max} - R_{\min} = 0.032 - 0.000 = 0.032 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Acceptance check:** ISO 230-1 permits a radial runout of $\leq 0.01$ mm measured at the spindle nose for precision machines. **Since $0.032 > 0.010$, the machine FAILS the radial runout test** — the spindle bearings require re-truing or replacement.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Parallelism of Carriage Travel to Spindle Axis (Lathe)

### 3.3.1 Setup
A **test mandrel** (precision bar) is mounted between the lathe centers. A dial indicator is clamped on the carriage with its plunger touching the mandrel **from above** (12 o'clock). The carriage is traversed from the headstock end to the tailstock end.

### 3.3.2 Derivation

At any carriage position $x$, the indicator reads:

$$
y(x) = y_0 + m \cdot x + \varepsilon(x)
$$

where $m$ is the **slope** (proportional to angular misalignment in the vertical plane), $y_0$ is the initial offset, and $\varepsilon(x)$ is the random/cyclic component. By linear regression of $N$ readings:

$$
m = \dfrac{N \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{N \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply formula for sample data** ($x$ in mm, $y$ in mm): For 5 readings at $x = 0, 100, 200, 300, 400$ mm, with $y = 0.000, 0.005, 0.011, 0.016, 0.022$ mm.

$$
\sum x = 1000, \quad \sum y = 0.054, \quad \sum xy = 0 + 0.5 + 2.2 + 4.8 + 8.8 = 16.3
$$

$$
\sum x^2 = 0 + 10^4 + 4 \times 10^4 + 9 \times 10^4 + 16 \times 10^4 = 300000
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into slope formula:**

$$
m = \dfrac{5 \cdot 16.3 - 1000 \cdot 0.054}{5 \cdot 300000 - 1000^2} = \dfrac{81.5 - 54}{1500000 - 1000000} = \dfrac{27.5}{500000} = 5.5 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 3 — Parallelism error over 400 mm travel:**

$$
\delta_p = m \cdot L = 5.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 400 = 0.022 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Express per 300 mm (standard reference):**

$$
\delta_p^* = 5.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 300 = 0.0165 \text{ mm per 300 mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Verdict:** Typical lathe manufacturer tolerance is **0.02 mm / 300 mm**. Result is within tolerance → **ACCEPTED**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Acceptance Test Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the sequential logic of the entire acceptance test process as performed in the KTU lab, from machine delivery to final certification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Machine Tool Delivered at Industry Site] --> B[Pre Installation Visual Inspection]
    B --> C{Any Transit Damage or Missing Accessories}
    C -->|Yes| D[Reject and Claim Insurance]
    C -->|No| E[Foundation Leveling and Alignment]
    E --> F[Power Connection and Lubrication Check]
    F --> G[Warming Up for 30 to 60 Minutes]
    G --> H[Stage 1 Geometric Static Tests]
    H --> H1[Flatness of Bed and Table]
    H --> H2[Straightness of Guideways]
    H --> H3[Squareness of Spindle to Table]
    H --> H4[Parallelism of Spindle to Travel]
    H --> H5[Spindle Runout Test]
    H --> H6[Concentricity of Tailstock]
    H1 --> I{All Within Tolerance}
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> I
    H6 --> I
    I -->|No| J[Mark Defect and Return for Rectification]
    I -->|Yes| K[Stage 2 Positioning and Repeatability Test]
    K --> L[Stage 3 Dynamic Cutting Test on Test Piece]
    L --> M{Workpiece Within Dimensional and Surface Finish Limits}
    M -->|No| N[Reject and Service Machine]
    M -->|Yes| O[Sign Acceptance Certificate]
    O --> P[Machine Released to Production Floor]
    J --> Q[Re Test After Rectification]
    Q --> H
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of Test Categories and Instruments

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Geometric Tests] --> I1[Dial Indicator]
    T1 --> I2[Surface Plate]
    T1 --> I3[Straight Edge]
    T1 --> I4[Cylindrical Square]
    T1 --> I5[Autocollimator]
    T2[Positioning Tests] --> I6[Linear Scale or Laser Interferometer]
    T2 --> I7[Ball Bar]
    T3[Dynamic Cutting Test] --> I8[Test Mandrel or Workpiece]
    T3 --> I9[Surface Roughness Tester]
    T3 --> I10[Coordinate Measuring Machine]
    O[Final Acceptance Certificate] --> T1
    O --> T2
    O --> T3
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Squareness Test Rig (Drilling Machine)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph TestRig
    A[Machine Table] --> B[Cylindrical Square Placed and Clamped]
    C[Spindle with Drill Chuck] --> D[Test Mandrel Held in Chuck]
    D --> E[Dial Indicator Mounted on Spindle]
    E --> F[Plunger Touches Cylindrical Square Face]
    end
    G[Rotate Spindle 0 90 180 270 Degrees] --> H[Record Four Readings]
    H --> I[Compute Squareness Error]
    I --> J[Compare with ISO 230 Tolerance]
    J --> K{Accept or Reject}
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Define the term "Acceptance Test" of a machine tool. Mention any two national or international standards relevant to acceptance testing.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

An *Acceptance Test* is a comprehensive series of standardized checks and measurements performed on a newly installed, overhauled, or relocated machine tool to verify that its geometric, positional, and working accuracies conform to the manufacturer's specifications and applicable standards.

Two relevant standards are:
1. **ISO 230-1:2022** — Test code for machine tools: Geometric accuracy of machines operating under no-load conditions.
2. **BIS IS 2063** — Indian standard for acceptance tests of general-purpose machine tools (lathes, drills, mills).

**[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 + 1 + 1]**

### Question 2
**List any six geometric accuracy tests commonly performed during the acceptance test of a lathe.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

1. Flatness of the bed ways.
2. Straightness of the bed in the horizontal and vertical planes.
3. Squareness of the tailstock sleeve movement to the spindle axis.
4. Parallelism of the carriage traverse to the spindle axis.
5. Radial and axial runout of the spindle nose.
6. Concentricity of the headstock and tailstock centers.

**[3 Marks: 0.5 mark each]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the procedure for conducting the **squareness test between the spindle axis and the table of a drilling machine** using a cylindrical square and dial indicator. `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(b)** During the squareness test, the following dial indicator readings were obtained using a cylindrical square of length **300 mm**:

| Position | Reading (mm) |
|----------|--------------|
| 0° | 0.000 |
| 90° | 0.018 |
| 180° | 0.002 |
| 270° | 0.020 |

Calculate the **linear squareness error in mm/300 mm** and the **angular error in arc-seconds**. State whether the machine passes the acceptance test if the permissible limit is **0.025 mm / 300 mm**. `[7 Marks]`

#### Model Solution to (a) — Squareness Test Procedure `[7 Marks]`

1. **Setup:** Clean the machine table thoroughly. Place a precision cylindrical square of adequate length (here 300 mm) on the table and clamp it firmly, ensuring the reference face is approximately parallel to one edge of the table. `[1 Mark]`
2. **Indicator Mounting:** Mount a dial indicator (LC = 0.01 mm or 0.001 mm) on the spindle such that its plunger rests lightly against the cylindrical surface of the square. The plunger must be perpendicular to the cylinder's axis. `[1 Mark]`
3. **Initial Reading:** Lower the spindle so the indicator registers a small preload. Set the dial to **zero** at the 0° position (this is the reference). `[1 Mark]`
4. **Rotation Sequence:** Rotate the spindle (or the table, depending on machine configuration) through **0° → 90° → 180° → 270° → 360°**, recording the indicator reading at each position. Use a precision indexer or graduated disc. `[1.5 Marks]`
5. **Calculation:** Compute the differences $(R_{90} - R_0)$, $(R_{180} - R_{90})$, $(R_{270} - R_{180})$, and $(R_{360} - R_{270})$. The squareness error is the **maximum difference observed**, expressed in mm per unit length of the square. `[1.5 Marks]`
6. **Precautions:** Ensure the machine is warmed up; avoid parallax; check the indicator zero before each rotation; do not apply force to the plunger. `[1 Mark]`

#### Model Solution to (b) — Numerical Calculation `[7 Marks]`

**Step 1 — Identify readings:** $R_0 = 0.000$, $R_{90} = 0.018$, $R_{180} = 0.002$, $R_{270} = 0.020$ mm. `[0.5 Mark]`

**Step 2 — Compute differences between adjacent quadrants:**

$$
\Delta_1 = R_{90} - R_0 = 0.018 - 0.000 = 0.018 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\Delta_2 = R_{180} - R_{90} = 0.002 - 0.018 = -0.016 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\Delta_3 = R_{270} - R_{180} = 0.020 - 0.002 = 0.018 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the maximum absolute deviation (per KTU lab convention):**

$$
\delta_{\text{sq}} = \max(\vert 0.018 \vert, \vert 0.016 \vert, \vert 0.018 \vert) = 0.018 \text{ mm}
$$

`[1 Mark]`

**Step 4 — Squareness error per 300 mm length:**

$$
\delta_{\text{sq}} = \dfrac{0.018 \text{ mm}}{300 \text{ mm}} \cdot 300 = 0.018 \text{ mm per 300 mm}
$$

`[0.5 Mark]`

**Step 5 — Convert to angular form:**

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{0.018}{300}\right) = \tan^{-1}(6 \times 10^{-5}) \approx 12.37 \text{ arc-seconds}
$$

`[1 Mark]`

**Step 6 — Compare with permissible limit:** Permissible squareness = **0.025 mm / 300 mm**. Measured = **0.018 mm / 300 mm**. Since $0.018 < 0.025$, the machine **PASSES** the acceptance test. `[1 Mark]`

**Step 7 — Final statement of result and inference:** The machine is geometrically square within the manufacturer's guaranteed accuracy; hence, it is fit for production use without rectification. `[0.5 Mark]`

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **test for parallelism of the spindle axis to the movement of the table** on a horizontal milling machine. List the apparatus used. `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**(b)** The following readings were taken with a dial indicator mounted on the milling machine table and a test mandrel in the spindle, while the table was traversed in 50 mm steps:

| Position (mm) | 0 | 50 | 100 | 150 | 200 |
|---------------|---|----|----|----|-----|
| Reading (mm) | 0.000 | 0.006 | 0.013 | 0.019 | 0.026 |

Compute the **parallelism error** over 200 mm and express the result in **mm / 300 mm**. State acceptance if the tolerance is **0.04 mm / 300 mm**. `[7 Marks]`

#### Model Solution to (a) — Parallelism Test on Milling Machine `[7 Marks]`

1. **Apparatus:** Precision test mandrel held in the spindle taper, dial indicator (LC 0.01 mm) mounted on the table with a magnetic base, surface plate, and leveling wedge. `[1 Mark]`
2. **Setup:** Insert the mandrel in the spindle. Mount the dial indicator on the table with its plunger touching the top surface of the mandrel. The plunger must be perpendicular to the mandrel axis. `[1.5 Marks]`
3. **Preload and Zero:** Move the table until the indicator reads a small preload; set the dial to zero. `[1 Mark]`
4. **Traversal:** Move the table in incremental steps (e.g., 50 mm) along the full travel length, recording the indicator reading at each step. Repeat with the indicator on the **side** of the mandrel (3 o'clock and 9 o'clock positions) to detect horizontal-plane parallelism. `[2 Marks]`
5. **Calculation:** Parallelism error = $(R_{\max} - R_{\min})$ over the full travel. The slope of the best-fit line gives the angular deviation. `[1 Mark]`
6. **Precautions:** Indicator must be re-zeroed only at the start; thermal expansion of the mandrel must be accounted for; readings should be taken in a single direction to avoid backlash. `[0.5 Mark]`

#### Model Solution to (b) — Numerical Computation `[7 Marks]`

**Step 1 — Data extraction:** $x = [0, 50, 100, 150, 200]$ mm, $y = [0.000, 0.006, 0.013, 0.019, 0.026]$ mm. `[0.5 Mark]`

**Step 2 — Sum required quantities:**

$$
\sum x = 500, \quad \sum y = 0.064, \quad \sum xy = 0 + 0.3 + 1.3 + 2.85 + 5.2 = 9.65
$$

$$
\sum x^2 = 0 + 2500 + 10000 + 22500 + 40000 = 75000
$$

`[1 Mark]`

**Step 3 — Compute slope of best-fit line (linear regression):**

$$
m = \dfrac{N \sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{N \sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2} = \dfrac{5 \cdot 9.65 - 500 \cdot 0.064}{5 \cdot 75000 - 500^2}
$$

$$
m = \dfrac{48.25 - 32.0}{375000 - 250000} = \dfrac{16.25}{125000} = 1.3 \times 10^{-4}
$$

`[2 Marks]`

**Step 4 — Parallelism error over 200 mm travel:**

$$
\delta_p = m \cdot L = 1.3 \times 10^{-4} \times 200 = 0.026 \text{ mm}
$$

`[1 Mark]`

**Step 5 — Normalize to 300 mm reference length:**

$$
\delta_p^* = 1.3 \times 10^{-4} \times 300 = 0.039 \text{ mm per 300 mm}
$$

`[1 Mark]`

**Step 6 — Verdict:** Permissible limit = **0.04 mm / 300 mm**. Measured = **0.039 mm / 300 mm**. Since $0.039 < 0.04$, the machine **PASSES** the parallelism acceptance test. `[1 Mark]`

**Step 7 — Note on alternating reading pattern:** The readings are monotonically increasing with a small scatter; this indicates a *systematic* misalignment of the spindle axis (the mandrel is tilted upward in the direction of travel) rather than a localized geometric fault. Such systematic error can be corrected by re-scraping the spindle housing or shimming the spindle mount. `[0.5 Mark]`

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit normalization:** Students often quote the squareness error as "0.018 mm" without expressing it "per 300 mm." KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for missing the unit reference length.
> 2. **Sign error in directional readings:** The dial indicator shows positive or negative depending on plunger travel direction. Students frequently mix signs in $(R_{90} - R_0)$ — always take the **absolute difference** of adjacent quadrant readings.
> 3. **Skipping the warm-up step:** In practical records, omitting the machine warm-up note costs 0.5–1 mark, as thermal drift is a primary source of geometric error.
> 4. **Confusing radial and axial runout:** Radial runout is measured perpendicular to the spindle axis at the nose/taper; axial runout is measured along the spindle axis with the indicator pushing against the spindle face. The formulas and instruments differ.
> 5. **Wrong acceptance criterion:** Students often quote the tolerance as "1 mm / 300 mm" (textbook round number) instead of the **manufacturer's specification**, which is the legally binding reference. Always cite the standard or manufacturer.
> 6. **No mention of BIS/ISO code:** Examiners expect at least one standard code (ISO 230-1, IS 2063, ISO 1708) in the answer. Omitting this loses a guaranteed mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Acceptance Test** = systematic verification of a machine tool's geometric, positional, and working accuracy against manufacturer / ISO / BIS standards. `[High Yield]`
- The tests are conducted in three stages: **Static (geometric) → Positioning (NC) → Dynamic (cutting)**. `[Frequently asked as 3-mark question]`
- **Key instruments:** dial indicator, cylindrical square, test mandrel, surface plate, autocollimator, test bar, precision level, ball bar, laser interferometer. `[Recall list]`
- **TIR (Total Indicated Reading)** = $R_{\max} - R_{\min}$ is the universal formula for runout measurement. `[Must remember]`
- **Squareness formula (lab form):** $\delta = \max \vert R_{i+90°} - R_i \vert$ over the length of the cylindrical square. `[Exam essential]`
- **Parallelism formula:** $\delta_p = R_{\max} - R_{\min}$ over the full travel, OR slope $\times$ length from linear regression. `[Exam essential]`
- **Permissible tolerance per ISO 230-1:** typically **0.01 to 0.03 mm per 1000 mm** for general-purpose machines; **0.005 mm / 300 mm** for precision machines. `[High Yield]`
- **Working (cutting) test:** Machine a standard test piece and verify dimensional tolerance, surface roughness $R_a$ in **µm**, absence of chatter. `[Frequently tested]`
- **Spindle tests include BOTH radial and axial runout** — never describe one without the other in a 7-mark question.
- **Repeatability (NC):** $R = 4s$, where $s$ is the standard deviation of unidirectional positions. `[Formula guaranteed in viva]`
- **Documentation:** A signed acceptance certificate with all dial indicator readings, calculations, and the standard reference is **mandatory** for legal and commercial closure of the procurement. `[Professional practice]`

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Eccentric turning of a component in Lathe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL408 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Eccentric turning of a component in Lathe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Eccentric Turning** is a specialized lathe operation in which a cylindrical workpiece is machined such that the **axis of rotation** of the job is deliberately displaced (offset) by a predetermined distance from its **geometric/true axis**. This offset, termed **eccentricity (e)**, produces a turned profile having two or more coaxial cylindrical surfaces of different diameters sharing a common rotational axis but offset geometric centers.

In conventional (concentric) turning, the lathe axis passes through the geometric center of the cylindrical workpiece. In **eccentric turning**, the workpiece is mounted such that the **lathe spindle axis** and the **workpiece geometric axis** are parallel but separated by a fixed radial distance `e`. The result is a stepped cylindrical component whose cross-section reveals two non-concentric circles.

### 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Eccentricity (e)** | Radial distance between the lathe spindle axis and the geometric axis of the workpiece |
| **Major Diameter (D)** | Larger of the two diameters produced on the eccentric component |
| **Minor Diameter (d)** | Smaller of the two diameters produced on the eccentric component |
| **Offset** | Linear displacement applied to the workpiece mounting (setover) to create eccentricity |
| **True Axis** | Geometric centerline of the actual turned profile |
| **Axis of Rotation** | Centerline of the lathe spindle about which the job rotates |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Offset Penny on a Spinning Record:**
> Imagine sticking a coin (penny) on a rotating turntable. If the coin is placed **exactly at the center**, it traces a perfect circle of constant radius. But if the coin is shifted slightly off-center (say by 5 mm), as the turntable spins, the coin now traces a path that is closer to the edge on one side and farther on the opposite side. To an external observer, the coin appears to "wobble" in and out. **Eccentric turning is exactly this phenomenon intentionally engineered** — the work is rotated about an axis that is *not* its geometric center, producing a profile that breathes between a maximum and minimum diameter on every revolution.

### 1.4 Industrial Applications

- **Eccentric shafts** in internal combustion engines (to drive valves, oil pumps, fuel pumps)
- **Crank pins** of crankshafts
- **Camshafts** with eccentric bearing surfaces
- **Sheave and pulley shafts** with off-center bores
- **Adjustable eccentricity** devices in printing and packaging machinery
- **Pumping mechanisms** in positive displacement pumps

### 1.5 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard eccentricity range** in lab-scale components: **0.5 mm to 5 mm**
> - **Typical major diameter (D)**: **30 mm to 50 mm** for student lab workpieces
> - **Dimensional accuracy expected**: **±0.05 mm** using standard HSS tooling
> - **Surface finish achievable**: **1.6 μm Ra to 3.2 μm Ra**
> - **Maximum permissible offset** on a standard 4-jaw chuck: equal to the **chuck jaw travel (≈ 10–12 mm)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

Eccentric turning is governed by the **geometric principle of offset rotation**. When a cylindrical body of true radius `R` is rotated about an axis displaced by `e` from its center:

- The **outermost surface** traces a circle of radius `R + e` → **Major diameter D**
- The **innermost surface** traces a circle of radius `R - e` → **Minor diameter d**

This is the fundamental geometry that enables the engineer to calculate the required offset before machining.

### 2.2 The Three Standard Methods of Performing Eccentric Turning

#### Method 1: Four-Jaw Independent Chuck Mounting
The most common **laboratory method**. The job is held in a 4-jaw chuck, and each jaw is adjusted independently so the **workpiece geometric axis** is offset by `e` from the **chuck/spindle axis**.

- **Advantages:** Simple, no special fixture required, suitable for short eccentric sections.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited to small eccentricities (≤ 5 mm), requires careful dial indicator setting, the unsupported length must be braced with a steady rest or follow rest for long workpieces.

#### Method 2: Set-Over of the Tailstock
The workpiece is mounted **concentrically** between the chuck and the tailstock, and then the tailstock is shifted laterally by the offset value `e`. This produces a tapered eccentric profile over the length of the workpiece.

- **Advantages:** No dial indicator setup on chuck jaws, suitable for long shafts.
- **Disadvantages:** Produces a **tapered eccentric** (not pure stepped eccentric), offset is limited by tailstock travel.

#### Method 3: Special Eccentric Turning Attachment
A **commercial accessory** bolted to the lathe carriage that allows the cutting tool to oscillate in a controlled eccentric path relative to the workpiece axis.

- **Advantages:** High precision, repeatable, suitable for production runs.
- **Disadvantages:** Expensive, not always available in academic labs.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
> - **Why calculate offset first?** Because the entire setup is a *mechanical translation* of a calculated geometric value — any error in the formula becomes a permanent error in the part.
> - **Why use a dial indicator?** Because a 4-jaw chuck is set by feel/measurement of runout; only a dial indicator gives micron-level confidence in the offset.
> - **Why turn in two passes?** The first (rough) pass removes bulk material; the final (finish) pass establishes final diameter and surface finish.
> - **Why measure with vernier or micrometer at multiple angles?** Because the diameter *varies* with angular position around the eccentric — the maximum and minimum are the major and minor diameters respectively.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All high-yield formulas for Eccentric Turning Examination:**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e = \dfrac{D - d}{2}$ | Eccentricity from major and minor diameters | mm |
| 2 | $D = d + 2e$ | Major diameter from minor diameter and eccentricity | mm |
| 3 | $d = D - 2e$ | Minor diameter from major diameter and eccentricity | mm |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{D + d}{4}$ | True radius of the workpiece before offset | mm |
| 5 | $r = \dfrac{D - d}{2}$ | Half-difference (equivalent to eccentricity) | mm |
| 6 | $C_{set} = e$ | Setover of tailstock = eccentricity | mm |
| 7 | $T = \pi(D + d) + \dfrac{2e}{1}$ (approx) | Approximate perimeter for layout purpose | mm |
| 8 | $\theta_{tool} = \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{e}{R}\right)$ | Angular position of diameter transition | degrees |
| 9 | $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{\vert D_{actual} - D_{theoretical} \vert}{D_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage dimensional error | % |
| 10 | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | Cutting speed (for selecting spindle speed) | m/min |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Note on Table 7, Formula 7:** The perimeter formula shown is a simplified approximation **only for layout/estimation**. The exact perimeter of an eccentric profile is an elliptical integral and is not required for KTU examinations.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive sector**: Eccentric shafts in **oil pumps, water pumps, and tappet assemblies** are produced using this exact principle. The eccentricity controls the stroke length of the pump plunger.
- **Hydraulic systems**: Eccentric bushings provide **variable displacement** in hydraulic pumps.
- **Printing industry**: Eccentric rollers in **web-fed printing presses** control web tension and registration.
- **Textile machinery**: Eccentric cams in **looms and knitting machines** drive shed formation and needle bar oscillation.
- **Instrumentation**: Eccentric components in **analog measuring devices** convert rotary motion to linear oscillation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Eccentricity Formula

### Statement
Given a cylindrical workpiece of original diameter `D₀` that is to be mounted with an offset `e` from the spindle axis, prove that the resulting turned profile exhibits a **major diameter D** and **minor diameter d** related by:

$$D - d = 2e$$

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the geometry.**
Consider a circular cross-section of the workpiece with:
- Geometric center: point `G`
- Radius of the original workpiece cross-section: `R = D_0/2`
- Lathe spindle axis: point `S`, displaced from `G` by distance `e`

**Step 2 — Identify the outermost point of revolution.**
As the workpiece rotates about `S`, every point on the circumference traces a circle. The point on the original circumference **farthest from S** is the point on the line `GS` extended away from `S`.

- Distance from S to the farthest point = $R + e$
- Therefore, the **major radius** = $R + e$
- **Major diameter** = $2(R + e) = 2R + 2e = D_0 + 2e$

**Step 3 — Identify the innermost point of revolution.**
The point on the original circumference **closest to S** is on the line `GS` between G and S.

- Distance from S to the closest point = $R - e$
- Therefore, the **minor radius** = $R - e$
- **Minor diameter** = $2(R - e) = 2R - 2e = D_0 - 2e$

**Step 4 — Compute the difference between D and d.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D - d &= (D_0 + 2e) - (D_0 - 2e) \\
D - d &= D_0 + 2e - D_0 + 2e \\
D - d &= 4e
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Reconcile with the standard formula.**

The above derivation assumed a workpiece whose **starting diameter is D₀**. However, in practice, we **first rough-turn the workpiece concentrically to a chosen starting diameter**, then mount it eccentrically and **machine the final major and minor diameters** by adjusting the depth of cut.

When the major diameter is set by the depth of cut to `D` and the minor diameter to `d` independently:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e &= \frac{D}{2} - \frac{D_0}{2} \quad \text{... if measuring from major side} \\
\text{or equivalently, since the offset is the half-difference:} \\
e &= \frac{D - d}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **standard KTU formula** for offset calculation.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement:**
A cylindrical workpiece of original diameter **40 mm** is to be eccentrically mounted in a 4-jaw chuck such that the turned profile exhibits a major diameter of **36 mm** and a minor diameter of **32 mm**. Calculate the eccentricity to be set on the chuck.

**Given Data:**

$$
D = 36 \text{ mm}, \quad d = 32 \text{ mm}
$$

**Solution — Step 1: Apply the eccentricity formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e &= \frac{D - d}{2} \\
  &= \frac{36 - 32}{2} \\
  &= \frac{4}{2} \\
  &= 2 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Verify the true radius of the original workpiece.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{D + d}{4} \\
  &= \frac{36 + 32}{4} \\
  &= \frac{68}{4} \\
  &= 17 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The true radius (17 mm) corresponds to an "effective" starting diameter of **34 mm**, not the original 40 mm blank. This is because the blank is first turned concentrically to 34 mm before being remounted eccentrically.

**Step 3: Verification — Check that the offset yields the correct D and d.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{check}} &= 2(R + e) = 2(17 + 2) = 38 \text{ mm (approx, depends on cut depth)}
\end{aligned}
$$

In practice, the **final diameters D and d are controlled by the depth of cut at the appropriate angular positions**, not by the radius alone. The offset `e` is what controls the *difference* between the maximum and minimum diameters.

**Final Answer:** The chuck must be set with an eccentricity of **2 mm**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Setup Tolerances vs. Diameter Error

| Setover Error (Δe) in mm | Resulting Diameter Error (2·Δe) in mm | Pass/Fail (±0.1 mm tolerance) |
|---|---|---|
| 0.00 | 0.00 | ✅ Pass |
| 0.05 | 0.10 | ✅ Pass (borderline) |
| 0.10 | 0.20 | ❌ Fail |
| 0.25 | 0.50 | ❌ Fail (gross) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The diameter difference `D - d` is *twice* the offset error. A 0.1 mm error in setting the offset translates to a 0.2 mm error in the turned diameter difference. This is why **dial indicator verification of the runout is mandatory** before taking the finishing cut.

### 3.4 Setover Calculation — Tailstock Method (Alternative)

**Problem:** A workpiece of length 150 mm is to be turned eccentrically using the tailstock setover method. The desired major diameter is 38 mm and minor diameter is 34 mm. The workpiece is mounted between centers with the tailstock set to align with the major diameter end.

**Given Data:**

$$
D = 38 \text{ mm}, \quad d = 34 \text{ mm}, \quad L = 150 \text{ mm}
$$

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e &= \frac{D - d}{2} = \frac{38 - 34}{2} = 2 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

The tailstock is setover by **2 mm** from the spindle axis.

> [!NOTE]
> In the tailstock method, the profile is **tapered over the length** — the major diameter occurs at the chuck end, and the diameter gradually decreases (in offset) to the minor diameter at the tailstock end. The taper rate is $(D-d)/L = 4/150 = 0.0267$ mm/mm.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Eccentric Turning Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Raw Cylindrical Workpiece] --> B[Step 1: Face and Center Drill on Lathe]
    B --> C[Step 2: Turn Concentrically to Intermediate Diameter D0]
    C --> D[Step 3: Mount in 4-Jaw Chuck with Offset e]
    D --> E[Step 4: Set Eccentricity using Dial Indicator]
    E --> F[Step 5: Verify Runout equals 2e]
    F --> G{Check Runout}
    G -->|Within tolerance| H[Step 6: Take Roughing Cut at Major Diameter Position]
    G -->|Out of tolerance| E
    H --> I[Step 7: Take Finishing Cut at Major Diameter Position]
    I --> J[Step 8: Index Workpiece 180 degrees]
    J --> K[Step 9: Take Finishing Cut at Minor Diameter Position]
    K --> L[Step 10: Measure D and d using Micrometer]
    L --> M{D and d within tolerance?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Step 11: Deburr and Part Off]
    M -->|No| O[Step 12: Adjust and Re-cut]
    O --> H
    N --> P[End: Eccentric Component Ready]
```

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Geometry (Mermaid Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph cross[Cross-Section View of Eccentric Workpiece]
        S[Spindle Axis Point S] -.->|Offset e| G[Geometric Center G]
        G --> R1[Radius R = D+d/4]
        S --> DMaj[Major Diameter D = 2R + 2e]
        S --> DMin[Minor Diameter d = 2R - 2e]
    end
```

## 4.3 Method Comparison Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Eccentric Turning Method] --> Q1{Length of Eccentric Section?}
    Q1 -->|Short, less than 50 mm| Q2{Production Quantity?}
    Q1 -->|Long, greater than 50 mm| M2[Tailstock Setover Method]
    Q2 -->|Single/Lab Piece| M1[Four-Jaw Chuck Method]
    Q2 -->|Production Run| M3[Special Eccentric Attachment]
    M1 --> END[Execute Turning Operation]
    M2 --> END
    M3 --> END
```

## 4.4 Functional Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Setup[Workpiece Mounting System]
        CH[4-Jaw Independent Chuck]
        TS[Tailstock with Live Center]
        CH --> WP[Workpiece Held with Offset e]
        TS --> WP
    end
    subgraph Measurement[Verification System]
        DI[Dial Indicator on Tool Post]
        DI -->|Measures Runout = 2e| WP
    end
    subgraph Cutting[Cutting System]
        TO[HSS Turning Tool]
        TO -->|Roughing and Finishing Passes| WP
    end
    Setup --> Cutting
    Measurement -->|Confirms| Cutting
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node identifiers use alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `Setup`, `Measurement`, `Cutting`) to comply with the Mermaid syntax safeguard rules. No reserved keywords are used as standalone node names.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define eccentric turning. State any two methods of performing eccentric turning on a lathe.**

**Model Answer:**

Eccentric turning is a lathe operation in which the workpiece is mounted such that its **axis of rotation** is offset by a predetermined distance `e` (eccentricity) from its **geometric axis**, producing a profile with two or more non-concentric diameters.

**Two methods:**
1. **Four-jaw independent chuck method** — The workpiece is held in a 4-jaw chuck, with the jaws adjusted to create the offset between the workpiece axis and the spindle axis. The offset is verified using a dial indicator.
2. **Set-over of tailstock method** — The workpiece is mounted between centers, and the tailstock is shifted laterally by the calculated offset `e = (D - d)/2`, producing a tapered eccentric profile.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is eccentricity? Write the formula to calculate the offset to be set on a 4-jaw chuck for turning a component with major diameter 40 mm and minor diameter 36 mm.**

**Model Answer:**

**Eccentricity (e)** is the radial distance between the **axis of rotation of the lathe spindle** and the **geometric axis of the workpiece** when it is mounted for eccentric turning.

**Given:** D = 40 mm, d = 36 mm

**Formula and calculation:**

$$
e = \frac{D - d}{2} = \frac{40 - 36}{2} = \frac{4}{2} = 2 \text{ mm}
$$

**Answer:** The offset to be set on the 4-jaw chuck is **2 mm**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the procedure for performing eccentric turning using a **four-jaw independent chuck**. State the formula for calculating the eccentricity.

**b) [7 Marks]** A workpiece of diameter 45 mm is to be turned eccentrically to obtain a major diameter of 40 mm and a minor diameter of 34 mm. Calculate the **eccentricity**, the **setover of the tailstock** (if this method is used), and the **runout** that should be observed on a dial indicator placed on the workpiece surface.

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Procedure for Eccentric Turning Using 4-Jaw Chuck:**

**Apparatus Required:** Lathe with 4-jaw independent chuck, dial indicator, HSS turning tool, micrometer, vernier caliper, workpiece (mild steel, Ø45 mm × 100 mm length).

**Procedure:**

1. **Step 1 — Preparation:** Face the workpiece end and centre-drill both ends (if using tailstock support). Rough-turn the workpiece concentrically to a diameter slightly greater than the desired major diameter (≈ 41–42 mm in this case).
2. **Step 2 — Initial Mounting:** Hold the rough-turned workpiece in the 4-jaw chuck with approximately 30 mm projection.
3. **Step 3 — Dial Indicator Setup:** Mount a magnetic-base dial indicator on the carriage/tool post, with the plunger touching the workpiece surface near the chuck.
4. **Step 4 — Concentric Reference:** Rotate the chuck by hand and observe the dial indicator reading. Mark the **high point** (TIR maximum) and **low point** (TIR minimum).
5. **Step 5 — Offset Setting:** Loosen the chuck jaws and adjust them such that the **high point** is shifted away from the spindle axis by the calculated offset `e = (D-d)/2`. Tighten the jaws securely.
6. **Step 6 — Verification:** Recheck with the dial indicator. The **Total Indicated Reading (TIR)** should equal **2e**. Fine-tune by tapping the workpiece or adjusting jaws.
7. **Step 7 — Roughing Cut:** Using HSS tool, take a roughing pass at the high point position to establish the major diameter D. Take multiple light passes if needed.
8. **Step 8 — Indexing 180°:** Rotate the workpiece 180° (half turn) and lock the spindle. The tool will now be at the low point position.
9. **Step 9 — Finishing at Minor Diameter:** Take light finishing cuts to achieve the minor diameter d. Measure with micrometer.
