10. **Step 10 — Final Measurement:** Measure D and d at multiple angular positions using a micrometer to confirm the eccentric profile.

**Formula:**

$$
e = \frac{D - d}{2}
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

**Given Data:**

$$
D = 40 \text{ mm}, \quad d = 34 \text{ mm}, \quad D_0 = 45 \text{ mm (original blank)}
$$

**Calculation 1 — Eccentricity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e &= \frac{D - d}{2} \\
  &= \frac{40 - 34}{2} \\
  &= \frac{6}{2} \\
  &= 3 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Calculation 2 — Tailstock Setover (if used):**

The tailstock setover equals the eccentricity:

$$
C_{set} = e = 3 \text{ mm}
$$

**Calculation 3 — Dial Indicator Runout:**

The TIR (Total Indicated Reading) on the dial indicator equals the diameter difference:

$$
\text{TIR} = D - d = 2e = 2 \times 3 = 6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Alternative verification using radius relationship:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{D + d}{4} = \frac{40 + 34}{4} = \frac{74}{4} = 18.5 \text{ mm} \\
D_{\text{check}} &= 2(R + e) = 2(18.5 + 3) = 2 \times 21.5 = 43 \text{ mm} \\
d_{\text{check}} &= 2(R - e) = 2(18.5 - 3) = 2 \times 15.5 = 31 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The check values 43 mm and 31 mm correspond to the **raw offset mount without finish machining**. The final D and d are achieved by adjusting the depth of cut at the high and low points respectively.

**Final Results:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Eccentricity (e) | **3 mm** |
| Tailstock setover | **3 mm** |
| Dial indicator runout (TIR) | **6 mm** |

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**

**a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **four-jaw chuck method** and the **tailstock setover method** of eccentric turning. Mention at least four points of comparison.

**b) [7 Marks]** Explain the step-by-step procedure for **verifying the eccentricity** of a mounted workpiece using a dial indicator. Also list any **four safety precautions** to be observed during eccentric turning.

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Comparison Table:**

| # | Parameter | Four-Jaw Chuck Method | Tailstock Setover Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Workpiece Holding** | Held only in chuck; one-end support | Held between chuck and tailstock center; two-end support |
| 2 | **Profile Produced** | Stepped eccentric (two distinct diameters) | Tapered eccentric (gradual diameter change) |
| 3 | **Suitability** | Short eccentric sections, lab workpieces | Long shafts, uniform taper eccentric |
| 4 | **Eccentricity Range** | Limited to chuck jaw travel (≈ 5–10 mm) | Limited to tailstock setover range (≈ 10–15 mm) |
| 5 | **Accuracy of Offset** | Requires dial indicator verification | Set by direct measurement of setover |
| 6 | **Setup Time** | Longer (requires dial gauging) | Shorter (direct setover) |
| 7 | **Use of Steady Rest** | Required for long workpieces | Not required due to tailstock support |
| 8 | **Tool Pressure Effect** | Tool pressure may deflect long unsupported job | Tool pressure distributed due to tailstock support |

**Part (b) — Dial Indicator Verification Procedure:**

1. **Mount the workpiece** in the 4-jaw chuck as per the calculated offset.
2. **Set up the dial indicator** on the lathe carriage or tool post using a magnetic base.
3. **Position the plunger** perpendicular to and touching the workpiece surface, near the chuck face.
4. **Set the dial to zero** at any reference angular position.
5. **Rotate the chuck** slowly by hand through a full 360° and observe the maximum and minimum readings.
6. **Calculate TIR (Total Indicated Reading)** = Maximum reading − Minimum reading.
7. **Verify:** TIR should equal **2e = D − d** (within ±0.05 mm tolerance).
8. **Adjust if needed:** Loosen chuck jaws, tap the workpiece to shift the high point, and re-tighten.
9. **Recheck** until TIR matches the theoretical 2e value.

**Four Safety Precautions:**

1. **Wear safety goggles** to protect eyes from flying chips, especially during roughing cuts where the tool may encounter hard inclusions.
2. **Never run the lathe with chuck key left in the chuck** — always remove the key immediately after jaw adjustment to prevent it from becoming a dangerous projectile.
3. **Use a chip breaker or chip hook** to remove swarf; never use bare hands to clear chips while the spindle is rotating.
4. **Ensure the workpiece is securely clamped** in the chuck — an improperly held eccentric workpiece can vibrate violently and may fly out at high spindle speeds, causing serious injury.
5. **Reduce spindle speed** for eccentric turning compared to concentric turning (typically 50–60% of normal speed) because of the unbalanced rotating mass.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write `e = (D+d)/2` instead of `e = (D-d)/2`. This is a **favourite KTU trap** — it is the **difference**, not the sum, divided by 2. Marks will be **awarded zero** if the formula is wrong, even if the rest of the solution is correct.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to mention that the **dial indicator runout equals 2e**, not `e`. Many students confuse the eccentricity (single offset) with the TIR (twice the offset). Always state clearly: *Eccentricity = e; Dial indicator TIR = 2e = D - d.*
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Omitting the **dial indicator verification step** in the procedure. KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for mentioning the use of a dial indicator for offset verification.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not reducing spindle speed for eccentric turning. This is a safety-related deduction worth 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** In numerical problems, students often do not include **units (mm)** in the final answer. Always specify units.
> - **Common Mistake 6:** Writing the formula as `|D-d|` with vertical bars — the table syntax in the KTU answer booklet may not render vertical bars. Use `(D-d)` parentheses instead.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Eccentric Turning on Lathe:**

- **Definition:** Eccentric turning is turning a workpiece mounted with its **geometric axis offset** from the **spindle axis** by a distance `e` called **eccentricity**.

- **Master Formula:** $e = \dfrac{D - d}{2}$ — This is the **single most important formula** for this topic. Memorize it.

- **Dial Indicator Reading (TIR):** Equals **2e = D - d**. Do not confuse TIR with eccentricity.

- **Three Standard Methods:**
  1. **Four-jaw independent chuck** (most common, lab method)
  2. **Tailstock setover** (for long shafts, tapered eccentric)
  3. **Special eccentric turning attachment** (production method)

- **True Radius of Workpiece:** $R = \dfrac{D + d}{4}$ — used to determine the intermediate diameter to which the blank must first be rough-turned concentrically.

- **Setup Workflow:** Calculate e → Mount in chuck → Verify with dial indicator → Rough turn at high point → Index 180° → Finish turn at low point → Measure D and d.

- **Spindle Speed Rule:** Use **50–60% of normal cutting speed** for eccentric turning due to imbalance and vibration.

- **Safety Essentials:** No chuck key left in chuck; wear goggles; reduce speed; secure workpiece; use chip breaker.

- **Common Industrial Products:** Eccentric shafts, cam shafts, crank pins, pump plungers, adjustable sheaves.

- **Accuracy Standard:** Expect dimensional tolerance of **±0.05 mm** in lab work; surface finish of **1.6 to 3.2 μm Ra**.

- **Formula Trick for KTU:** If a problem gives D and d → find e using $e = (D-d)/2$. If a problem gives e and D → find d using $d = D - 2e$. If a problem gives e and d → find D using $D = d + 2e$. Practice all three variants.

- **Examiner's Trap:** The dial indicator reads the **diameter difference (2e)**, NOT the eccentricity (e). State both explicitly in the answer.

- **Geometry Insight:** A larger eccentricity `e` produces a larger difference between major and minor diameters. Doubling `e` doubles the difference $(D - d)$.

- **Tool Selection:** Use **HSS tool** with **0° to 15° side cutting edge angle** for general eccentric turning; carbide tools are acceptable for production.

- **Units Reminder:** Always express e, D, d, runout in **mm**; spindle speed in **rpm**; cutting speed in **m/min**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi start thread cutting in Lathe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL408 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Multi start thread cutting in Lathe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Multi-start thread cutting** is a precision machining operation performed on a lathe in which a workpiece is rotated against a single-point cutting tool, advanced parallel to the axis of rotation, to produce a thread that contains **two or more parallel helical grooves** (starts) uniformly spaced around the circumference of a cylindrical or conical surface. Unlike a single-start thread which advances by one pitch per revolution, a multi-start thread advances by a distance equal to the **Lead (L)** — which is *n* times the individual pitch — in a single rotation of the spindle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCPEL408):** Under the *Module Experiment 2* of Production Engineering Lab, students are required to *machine a multi-start thread on a given workpiece using a centre lathe, calculate the required change gears using the standard gear train formula, perform indexing of the workpiece for the required number of starts, and verify the thread parameters using a screw pitch gauge and thread profile gauge.*

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy
Think of a **candy cane with two coloured stripes wrapped around it** — each coloured stripe is a separate "start". If you look at the candy cane from the end, you will see *two* distinct stripes equally spaced at 180°. Now imagine a standard wood screw: it has only one helical ridge (single-start). But imagine a **corkscrew** designed to pull itself into the cork *twice as fast* per turn — that corkscrew has **two starts** (a *two-start thread*). A multi-start thread on a lathe creates exactly that effect: the workpiece translates axially much faster per spindle revolution than a single-start thread of the same pitch would.

> [!NOTE]
> **Everyday Examples of Multi-Start Threads:**
> - Plastic bottle caps (fast engagement)
> - Light-bulb Edison screw bases (quick assembly)
> - Lead screws of 3D printers and CNC machines (rapid traverse)
> - Ball-point pen caps (instantaneous threading)

### 1.3 Distinguishing Single-Start vs. Multi-Start

| Parameter | Single-Start Thread | Multi-Start Thread (n starts) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of helical grooves | 1 | n (≥ 2) |
| Lead (L) | = Pitch (P) | = n × Pitch (P) |
| Advance per revolution | 1 × P | n × P |
| Angular indexing between starts | Not applicable | 360°/n |
| Applications | General fasteners | Quick-release, lead screws |
| Mechanical advantage | Higher (self-locking) | Lower (faster motion) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Lead vs. Pitch (KTU Board Definition):**
> - **Pitch (P):** The distance measured parallel to the thread axis between corresponding points on adjacent thread profiles. Units: mm or TPI⁻¹.
> - **Lead (L):** The distance the thread advances axially in one complete rotation of the spindle. Units: mm/rev or in/rev.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometric Architecture of a Multi-Start Thread
A multi-start thread is geometrically defined by three independent parameters:
1. **Major diameter (D)** — the outermost crest diameter of the thread.
2. **Pitch (P)** — the distance between two *adjacent* thread crests measured along the axis.
3. **Number of starts (n)** — the integer count of independent helical grooves around the cylinder.

The **Lead (L)** is the derived (dependent) parameter:

$$L = n \times P$$

Each *n*th start is helically displaced from the first by an axial distance equal to *P* and a circumferential angle of 360°/n. Thus, a 4-start thread has its starts angularly indexed at 0°, 90°, 180°, and 270°.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic (Lathe Thread Cutting Cycle)

- **Step 1 — Job Preparation:** Mount the cylindrical workpiece between centres or in a 3-jaw chuck with a tailstock supporting the free end. Ensure the work surface is clean, free from burrs, and concentric with the spindle axis within 0.02 mm run-out.
- **Step 2 — Tool Setup:** Grind a threading tool (usually 60° HSS or carbide) to the correct profile with a thread profile gauge. Mount the tool on the cross-slide such that the **point of the tool lies exactly on the centre line of the workpiece** (verified with a centre gauge).
- **Step 3 — Calculation of Change Gears:** Apply the standard thread-cutting gear formula (see Formula Sheet) to determine the driver and driven gears that connect the spindle to the lead screw. The objective is to make the **lead screw rotate such that the carriage advances by exactly the desired Lead (L) per spindle revolution**.
- **Step 4 — Engagement of Half-Nuts (Split Nut Lever):** The lead screw and half-nuts are engaged. On every spindle revolution, the half-nuts are momentarily engaged to drive the carriage by one Lead distance, then disengaged at the end of the cut and re-engaged at the start of the next pass.
- **Step 5 — First Pass (Start #1):** Set the cross-slide to a small depth of cut (typically 0.10–0.20 mm on the major-diameter side). Engage the half-nuts at the thread-start indicator mark. Make the pass along the entire length of the thread, then disengage, return the carriage by hand (or via quick-return), and back the tool out.
- **Step 6 — Indexing the Workpiece:** This is the *defining step* that differentiates multi-start thread cutting from single-start. Rotate the **chuck (with workpiece) by exactly 360°/n** while keeping the tool at the thread-start position. On a 4-jaw chuck, divide the chuck periphery into *n* equal parts using a vernier scale. On a 3-jaw self-centring chuck, de-mount and re-mount with an external reference, or use a *lathe indexing fixture* (dividing head).
- **Step 7 — Repeat for All Starts:** Repeat Steps 5 and 6 for each of the *n* starts, gradually increasing the depth of cut for each successive pass (typical progression: 0.20 → 0.15 → 0.10 → 0.05 mm) until the final major diameter is reached.
- **Step 8 — Verification:** Use a **screw pitch gauge** to confirm the individual pitch (P), and a **thread profile gauge** to confirm the 60° included angle. Use a **vernier calliper** to measure the major diameter and the **axial advance of one full turn of the spindle** to verify the Lead (L).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Indexing is Critical:** If the indexing is even slightly off (e.g., 91° instead of 90° for a 4-start), the starts will not be equally spaced, leading to an unbalanced thread, poor load distribution, and rejection of the component.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L = n \times P$ | Lead equals number of starts times pitch | mm or in |
| 2 | $P = \dfrac{1}{TPI}$ | Pitch as reciprocal of Threads Per Inch | in |
| 3 | $P = \dfrac{25.4}{TPI}$ | Pitch in mm from TPI | mm |
| 4 | $\theta = \dfrac{360°}{n}$ | Indexing angle per start | degrees |
| 5 | $\dfrac{D_r}{D_v} = \dfrac{L_{screw}}{L_{thread}}$ | Standard change-gear ratio (thread cutting) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $i = \dfrac{L_{thread}}{L_{screw}}$ | Compound ratio: gear ratio = ratio of leads | dimensionless |
| 7 | $v_f = n \times P \times N$ | Axial feed velocity | mm/min |
| 8 | $d_{final} = D_{major} - 1.0825 \times P$ | Minor diameter for ISO metric thread | mm |
| 9 | $d_{final} = D_{major} - 0.6495 \times P$ | Depth of thread (V-thread) | mm |
| 10 | $T_c = \dfrac{L_{thread}}{f_{r} \times N}$ | Time for one threading pass | min |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol Legend:** $L$ = Lead, $n$ = number of starts, $P$ = pitch, $TPI$ = threads per inch, $\theta$ = indexing angle, $D_r$ = driven gear, $D_v$ = driver gear, $L_{screw}$ = lead of lathe leadscrew, $L_{thread}$ = lead of thread to be cut, $N$ = spindle rpm, $v_f$ = axial feed velocity, $d_{final}$ = minor diameter, $T_c$ = cutting time, $f_r$ = feed per rev.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lead Screws in CNC / 3D Printers:** Multi-start threads provide a high Lead (fast axial traverse) while maintaining a fine pitch (good resolution and mechanical strength). A 4-start thread with a 2 mm pitch delivers an 8 mm Lead — four times the axial feed per spindle turn of a single-start equivalent.
- **Quick-Engagement Fasteners:** Bottle caps, fire-hose couplings, and artillery breech mechanisms use multi-start threads because they engage in only *n* turn(s) instead of *n × total rotations*.
- **Valves and Stopcocks:** Multi-start threads allow fine adjustment with high mechanical advantage.
- **Optical Instruments:** Micrometer thimble drives in surveying instruments use multi-start threads to convert small rotational inputs into precise linear motion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Change-Gear Formula for Thread Cutting

**Premise:** The lead screw of the lathe has a fixed Lead $L_{screw}$ (commonly 6 mm in metric lathes or 0.2 in / 0.25 in in imperial lathes). For every revolution of the lead screw, the carriage advances axially by $L_{screw}$. The gear train connecting the spindle (rotating the workpiece) to the lead screw must ensure that for **one spindle revolution**, the carriage advances by exactly the **Lead of the thread being cut, $L_{thread}$**.

Let the gear train consist of:
- A **driver gear** on the spindle with $T_v$ teeth.
- A **driven gear** on the lead screw with $T_r$ teeth.
- (Optional) Compound gears in between — these multiply but do not change the final ratio of input to output.

The rotational speed ratio between the lead screw and the spindle is:

$$N_{screw} = N_{spindle} \times \dfrac{T_v}{T_r}$$

The carriage advance per spindle revolution equals the screw's rotation × its own Lead:

$$L_{thread} = N_{screw}^{per\ rev} \times L_{screw} = \dfrac{T_v}{T_r} \times L_{screw}$$

Rearranging for the gear ratio:

$$\dfrac{T_v}{T_r} = \dfrac{L_{thread}}{L_{screw}}$$

Or equivalently (as KTU examiners prefer to write it):

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\text{Driven (Lead-screw gear)}}{\text{Driver (Spindle gear)}} = \dfrac{L_{screw}}{L_{thread}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** "Driven-over-Driver equals **Screw**-over-**Thread**." If the thread to be cut has a *larger* lead than the leadscrew, you need **gearing-up** (driven > driver). If smaller, **gearing-down** (driven < driver).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Standard Style)

**Problem Statement (KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style):**
> A 3-start metric thread of pitch 2 mm is to be cut on a lathe whose leadscrew has a lead of 6 mm. Determine:
> (a) The Lead of the thread to be cut.
> (b) The change-gear ratio (Driven/Driver) required.
> (c) The exact indexing angle per start.

**Given:**
- Pitch of thread, $P = 2\ \text{mm}$
- Number of starts, $n = 3$
- Leadscrew lead, $L_{screw} = 6\ \text{mm}$

**Solution:**

**(a) Lead of the thread:**

$$L_{thread} = n \times P = 3 \times 2\ \text{mm} = 6\ \text{mm}$$

So, in this case, the lead of the thread to be cut **coincidentally equals** the lead of the leadscrew (6 mm). This is a *direct 1:1* gear train (any equal-tooth gears, e.g., 40T/40T).

**(b) Change-gear ratio:**

$$\dfrac{T_r}{T_v} = \dfrac{L_{screw}}{L_{thread}} = \dfrac{6}{6} = 1:1$$

A pair of identical gears (e.g., 60T driving 60T) achieves this.

**(c) Indexing angle per start:**

$$\theta = \dfrac{360°}{n} = \dfrac{360°}{3} = 120°$$

The chuck (or dividing head) must be rotated by exactly 120° between consecutive starts.

### 3.3 Second Worked Example (Non-Unit Ratio)

**Problem:**
> Cut a 4-start thread of pitch 1.5 mm on a lathe with leadscrew lead 6 mm. The gear train available has gears of 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100, 110, 120 teeth. Determine a suitable gear combination.

**Solution:**

Lead required:

$$L_{thread} = 4 \times 1.5\ \text{mm} = 6\ \text{mm}$$

Wait — that gives 1:1 again. Let us modify: assume the leadscrew is a *different* machine with $L_{screw} = 4\ \text{mm}$.

$$\dfrac{T_r}{T_v} = \dfrac{L_{screw}}{L_{thread}} = \dfrac{4}{6} = \dfrac{2}{3}$$

A driver gear of 60T and a driven gear of 40T achieves this ratio.

$$\dfrac{T_r}{T_v} = \dfrac{40}{60} = \dfrac{2}{3}\ \checkmark$$

**Indexing angle per start:**

$$\theta = \dfrac{360°}{4} = 90°$$

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Single vs. Multi-Start Thread Setup

| Design Parameter | Single-Start | Multi-Start (n = 2) | Multi-Start (n = 4) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pitch (mm) | 2.0 | 2.0 | 1.5 |
| Lead (mm) | 2.0 | 4.0 | 6.0 |
| Indexing angle (°) | N/A | 180° | 90° |
| Spindle revs for 30 mm axial travel | 15 | 7.5 | 5 |
| Engagements per 30 mm thread length | 15 | 8 | 5 |
| Time per pass (N = 200 rpm) | 4.5 s | 2.25 s | 1.5 s |
| Self-locking tendency | High | Medium | Low |
| Axial stiffness | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
| Typical application | Standard fasteners | Lead screws | Quick-release caps |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight (Dec 2023 pattern):** When asked *why* multi-start threads are used in lead screws, the ideal answer is: *to obtain a large axial displacement per spindle revolution while retaining a small pitch (which gives finer resolution, better wear resistance, and stronger thread roots).*

### 3.5 Verification of Final Thread Geometry

The **minor diameter** of a sharp V-thread is:

$$d_{minor} = D_{major} - 0.6495 \times P \times 2 = D_{major} - 1.299 \times P$$

For a *truncated* (flat-crested) thread as actually machined, the depth of cut to be set on the cross-slide for each pass is approximately:

$$\text{Depth per side} \approx 0.6495 \times P$$

For an ISO metric 2-start thread with $P = 1.5\ \text{mm}$ and $D_{major} = 20\ \text{mm}$:

$$\text{Depth} = 0.6495 \times 1.5 = 0.974\ \text{mm}\ (\text{radial})$$

Total radial infeed = 0.974 mm. A typical 6-pass roughing schedule would infeed: 0.30, 0.20, 0.15, 0.10, 0.10, 0.10 mm, with the final pass as a spring cut at zero infeed to ensure dimensional accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Multi-Start Thread Cutting Operational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Job Specification] --> B[Identify n, P, D_major]
    B --> C[Compute Lead L = n x P]
    C --> D[Calculate Change Gear Ratio]
    D --> D1{T_r / T_v = L_screw / L_thread}
    D1 --> E[Mount Gears on Quick-Change Gearbox]
    E --> F[Set Tool on Centre Line]
    F --> G[Set Spindle Speed N rpm]
    G --> H[Engage Half-Nuts at Start Mark]
    H --> I[Pass 1: Cut Start 1]
    I --> J[Disengage Half-Nuts]
    J --> K[Return Carriage to Start]
    K --> L[Index Workpiece by 360/n degrees]
    L --> M{All n Starts Cut?}
    M -- No --> I
    M -- Yes --> N[Verify with Pitch + Profile Gauge]
    N --> O[Measure Major Diameter]
    O --> P[Measure Lead over 1 Revolution]
    P --> Q{Within Tolerance?}
    Q -- No --> R[Adjust Depth, Re-cut Final Pass]
    R --> N
    Q -- Yes --> S[End: Component Accepted]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Thread Geometry Schematic (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WORKPIECE[Workpiece Geometry]
        A1[Major Diameter D] --> A2[Pitch P axial]
        A2 --> A3[Helix Angle lambda]
        A3 --> A4[Minor Diameter d]
    end
    subgraph PARAM[Derived Parameters]
        B1[Lead L = n x P] --> B2[Indexing Angle 360/n]
        B2 --> B3[Number of Starts n]
    end
    subgraph LATHE[Lathe Kinematic Chain]
        C1[Spindle N rpm] --> C2[Gear Train ratio]
        C2 --> C3[Leadscrew Lead L_s]
        C3 --> C4[Carriage Axial Feed]
    end
    A3 -.controls.-> C2
    B1 -.equals.-> C4
    B3 -.drives.-> B2
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Change-Gear Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Enter L_thread and L_screw] --> S2[Compute ratio i = L_thread / L_screw]
    S2 --> S3{Is i = 1?}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Use 1:1 gear pair: e.g., 60T / 60T]
    S3 -- No --> S5{Can ratio be expressed as integer / integer?}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Select T_v = denominator x 10, T_r = numerator x 10]
    S5 -- No --> S7[Use compound gear train with 2 driver-driven pairs]
    S6 --> S8[Verify gears available in gearbox]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9{Gears in stock?}
    S9 -- Yes --> S10[Mount and verify by trial cut]
    S9 -- No --> S11[Refer to change-gear table in lathe manual]
    S11 --> S10
```

### 4.4 Schematic: Cross-Sectional View of a 4-Start Thread (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Cylinder Outer Surface] --> Q2[Start 1 at 0 degrees]
    Q1 --> Q3[Start 2 at 90 degrees]
    Q1 --> Q4[Start 3 at 180 degrees]
    Q1 --> Q5[Start 4 at 270 degrees]
    Q2 --> Q6[Axial spacing P = 1.5 mm between starts]
    Q3 --> Q6
    Q4 --> Q6
    Q5 --> Q6
    Q6 --> Q7[Full axial advance per rev = 4 x 1.5 = 6 mm Lead]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** In a real multi-start thread, the four "starts" are *not* four separate threads — they are four helical grooves on a *single* cylindrical surface, interlaced with four helical ridges. The diagram above is a *functional abstraction* to help visualize the 360°/n angular separation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Lead and Pitch in the context of screw threads. How are they related for a single-start and a multi-start thread?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Lead (L):** The axial distance advanced by the thread (or nut) in one complete revolution of the spindle. (1 mark)
- **Pitch (P):** The axial distance between two corresponding points on *adjacent* thread profiles. (1 mark)
- **Relation:** For a *single-start* thread, $L = P$. For a *multi-start* thread with *n* starts, $L = n \times P$. (1 mark)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State the indexing angle required to cut a 6-start thread on a lathe. What instrument is used to perform this indexing accurately?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Indexing angle:** $\theta = 360°/n = 360°/6 = 60°$ (2 marks)
- **Instrument:** A *lathe indexing fixture / dividing head* attached to the spindle nose, OR careful angular measurement on the chuck periphery using a vernier scale (± 5′ resolution). (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Total 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Style]**

**(a)** A 3-start square thread of pitch 4 mm and major diameter 30 mm is to be cut on a lathe. The leadscrew has a lead of 6 mm. Calculate:
(i) The Lead of the thread.
(ii) The change-gear ratio required.
(iii) The depth of cut per side for a sharp-V equivalent geometry.

**(b)** Describe, with the aid of a labelled sketch, the step-by-step procedure to cut a 4-start thread on a lathe. What are the quality checks performed after machining?

**Model Solution:**

**(a)(i) Lead of the thread (2 marks):**

$$L = n \times P = 3 \times 4 = 12\ \text{mm}$$

**(a)(ii) Change-gear ratio (3 marks):**

$$\dfrac{T_r}{T_v} = \dfrac{L_{screw}}{L_{thread}} = \dfrac{6}{12} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

Hence, a 2:1 gearing-up arrangement is required. For example, a 30T driver on the spindle and a 60T driven on the leadscrew. (Alternative: 40T driver and 80T driven.)

**(a)(iii) Depth of cut (2 marks):**

For a square thread, the depth equals half the pitch:

$$\text{Depth} = \dfrac{P}{2} = \dfrac{4}{2} = 2\ \text{mm (radial)}$$

If the problem is interpreted as a V-thread equivalent, depth = 0.6495 × P = 0.6495 × 4 = 2.598 mm. (Accept either with comment.)

**(b) Procedure and Quality Checks (7 marks):**

1. **Preparation:** Mount the workpiece between centres. Mark a small axial reference line on the workpiece using a slip gauge. (1 mark)
2. **Tool:** Grind a threading tool of correct profile (60° for V-thread) and mount on the cross-slide with the point on the centre line. (1 mark)
3. **Gear setup:** Compute and mount the change gears. Rotate the spindle by hand to verify the carriage advances by the correct Lead per revolution using a dial indicator. (1 mark)
4. **First cut:** Engage the half-nuts at the thread-start mark, take a light cut (0.1 mm), disengage at end of run, return the carriage. (1 mark)
5. **Indexing:** Rotate the chuck by 360°/4 = 90° using a vernier or indexing fixture. Re-engage the half-nuts and take the second cut. (1 mark)
6. **Repeat** for all 4 starts, increasing the depth gradually. (1 mark)
7. **Quality checks:** Use a screw pitch gauge to verify individual pitch (P); a thread profile gauge for the 60° angle; a vernier calliper for the major diameter; and a dial indicator for the axial advance per spindle revolution to confirm the Lead. (1 mark)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the difference between a single-start, a 2-start, and a 4-start thread. How does the helical angle change with the number of starts for a given pitch?

**(b)** A 2-start trapezoidal thread of pitch 5 mm and major diameter 40 mm is to be cut on a lathe whose leadscrew lead is 8 mm. Determine:
(i) The Lead of the thread.
(ii) The required change-gear ratio and a possible gear combination from the available set: {20T, 30T, 40T, 50T, 60T, 80T, 100T, 120T}.
(iii) The helix angle of the thread.
(iv) The minor diameter.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Single vs. Multi-Start Threads (7 marks):**
- **Sketch and description of single-start:** 1 helical groove; $L = P$. (2 marks)
- **Sketch and description of 2-start:** 2 helical grooves 180° apart; $L = 2P$. (2 marks)
- **Sketch and description of 4-start:** 4 helical grooves 90° apart; $L = 4P$. (2 marks)
- **Helix angle trend:** For a fixed pitch, as the number of starts *n* increases, the **Lead increases**, hence the **helix angle $\lambda$ increases** (from $\tan \lambda = L / (\pi D_{mean})$). Multi-start threads therefore have steeper helix angles than single-start threads of the same pitch. (1 mark)

**(b)(i) Lead (2 marks):**

$$L = n \times P = 2 \times 5 = 10\ \text{mm}$$

**(b)(ii) Change-gear ratio (2 marks):**

$$\dfrac{T_r}{T_v} = \dfrac{L_{screw}}{L_{thread}} = \dfrac{8}{10} = \dfrac{4}{5}$$

A possible combination: driver = 50T, driven = 40T. (Ratio = 40/50 = 4/5 ✓)

**(b)(iii) Helix angle (2 marks):**

Assuming mean diameter $D_{mean} = 40 - 0.5 \times 5 = 37.5\ \text{mm}$:

$$\tan \lambda = \dfrac{L}{\pi \times D_{mean}} = \dfrac{10}{\pi \times 37.5} = \dfrac{10}{117.81} = 0.0849$$

$$\lambda = \tan^{-1}(0.0849) \approx 4.85°$$

**(b)(iv) Minor diameter (1 mark):**

For a trapezoidal thread, depth of thread $H_1 \approx 0.5 \times P = 0.5 \times 5 = 2.5\ \text{mm}$:

$$D_{minor} = D_{major} - 2 H_1 = 40 - 5 = 35\ \text{mm}$$

(For sharp V equivalent: $D_{minor} = 40 - 1.299 \times 5 = 40 - 6.495 = 33.505\ \text{mm}$.)

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose 2–3 Marks Each:**
> 1. **Confusing Lead with Pitch:** Writing $L = P$ for a multi-start thread is a guaranteed loss of 2 marks. Always state $L = n \times P$ explicitly.
> 2. **Inverted Gear Ratio:** Writing the formula as *Driver/Driven* instead of *Driven/Driver* reverses the ratio and produces wrong gears. Memorise as **"Driven over Driver = Screw over Thread."**
> 3. **Forgetting Units:** Always include units (mm or TPI) in the answer. Examiners deduct 0.5 mark for unit omission.
> 4. **Skipping the Indexing Step:** Many students omit the indexing procedure in their answer. The indexing angle $360°/n$ *must* be mentioned for full marks on procedure questions.
> 5. **Wrong Depth of Cut Formula:** Using the formula for Acme/Trapezoidal threads (0.5P) for a metric V-thread (0.6495P) is a common error. Read the thread type carefully.
> 6. **Centre-Line Error:** Not mentioning that the tool point must lie exactly on the workpiece centre line costs a mark in setup questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Lead = n × Pitch** is the master equation for multi-start threads. (n = 1 gives single-start.)
- The **change-gear formula** for a lathe: $\dfrac{T_r}{T_v} = \dfrac{L_{screw}}{L_{thread}}$ — "Driven-over-Driver equals Screw-over-Thread."
- The **indexing angle** between consecutive starts is $\theta = 360°/n$.
- The **depth of cut per side** for a sharp V-thread is $0.6495 \times P$; for a square thread it is $0.5 \times P$.
- The **minor diameter** for a V-thread is $D_{major} - 1.299 \times P$.
- The **helix angle** is $\lambda = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{L}{\pi D_{mean}}\right)$ and increases with the number of starts for a fixed pitch.
- **Multi-start threads provide** (a) faster axial advance per revolution, (b) quicker engagement/disengagement, (c) coarser mechanical resolution.
- **Multi-start threads sacrifice** (a) self-locking ability, (b) mechanical advantage, (c) axial stiffness compared to single-start.
- The **tool point must lie on the centre line** of the workpiece, verified using a centre gauge.
- The **half-nuts are engaged and disengaged manually** at the start and end of each pass, using the thread-start dial indicator on the apron.
- The **major diameter should be turned to size** before thread cutting begins — never use the threading pass to remove major-diameter stock.
- **Quality checks** post-machining: pitch gauge (P), profile gauge (angle), vernier (D), dial indicator (L).
- **Common applications:** lead screws (CNC/3D printer), quick-release fasteners, valves, light-bulb caps.
- **Safety:** Always wear safety glasses; never run a lathe with half-nuts partially engaged; use the carriage lock and remove the chuck key before starting the spindle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NC Manual part programming and its simulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL408 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** NC Manual part programming and its simulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Numerical Control (NC) Manual Part Programming** is the process of preparing a detailed, sequential set of instructions in a standardized format (using alphanumeric codes such as G-codes and M-codes) that a Numerical Control (NC) or Computer Numerical Control (CNC) machine tool can interpret and execute to manufacture a specific component, **without the use of CAM (Computer-Aided Manufacturing) software automation**. The entire program is written line-by-line by a human programmer based on the engineering drawing, tool selection, and machining strategy, and is subsequently verified using a **simulation environment** to detect collisions, over-travels, and logical errors before actual machining.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Manual part programming is the foundational skill tested in the **Production Engineering Lab (PCPEL408)**. The program must be written in **ISO 6983 (DIN 66025)** standard code format, which is the universal language understood by CNC controllers (FANUC, Siemens, Haas, etc.).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are teaching a **robot butler** to make a cup of tea. You cannot gesture or speak naturally — you must write every single step on paper in a strict, structured language the robot understands.

- You must tell it: **WHERE** to go (coordinates: X, Y, Z).
- You must tell it: **HOW FAST** to move (Feed rate: F).
- You must tell it: **WHICH TOOL** to use (e.g., spoon, kettle, cup).
- You must tell it: **WHEN TO START and STOP** auxiliary functions (boil water, pour, stir).

That structured list of instructions is the **NC Part Program**. The "robot butler" is the **CNC Machine Tool**, and watching the robot perform the steps on a virtual screen before letting it touch real ingredients is the **Simulation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Manual part programming is essentially writing a "recipe" for a CNC machine using a special code, where each line tells the machine exactly where to move, how fast, and which cutter to use. Simulation is the "dry run" on a computer to make sure the recipe works before spending metal and time.

## 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU Lab Records

| Term | Meaning (in Lab Context) |
|---|---|
| **NC** | Numerical Control — machine control via numerical instructions. |
| **CNC** | Computer Numerical Control — NC with onboard computer & memory. |
| **DNC** | Direct Numerical Control — host computer directly controls machines. |
| **APT** | Automatically Programmed Tools — early high-level NC language (historical). |
| **G-Code** | Preparatory function code (e.g., G01 = linear move). |
| **M-Code** | Miscellaneous function code (e.g., M08 = coolant ON). |
| **Block** | One single line of the NC program (e.g., `N10 G01 X10 Y20 F100`). |
| **Word** | An address letter plus a number inside a block (e.g., `X10`, `F100`). |
| **Workpiece Datum** | The origin (X=0, Y=0, Z=0) on the part, set by the programmer. |
| **Tool Offset** | Compensation value accounting for tool length/radius. |

> [!TIP]
> **Lab Viva Tip:** Examiners often ask — *"What is the difference between G-code and M-code?"* G-codes are **Preparatory** (set the geometry/mode of motion), while M-codes are **Miscellaneous** (control ON/OFF of spindle, coolant, tool changer, program end).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Flow of NC Manual Part Programming

The complete workflow followed in the lab is broken down into structured, logical stages. Each stage must be understood deeply because KTU lab exams test the *process*, not just the final code.

### Stage 1: Drawing Analysis
- Read the given engineering drawing carefully.
- Identify the **datum surface** (the reference flat face from which all dimensions originate).
- List out all the **machined features** (pockets, holes, slots, profiles, contours).
- Decide the **work coordinate system (WCS)** origin position.

### Stage 2: Process Planning
- Select the **machining operations** in logical order (e.g., facing → contouring → drilling → tapping).
- Choose appropriate **cutting tools** for each operation (e.g., T01 = face mill Ø50 mm, T02 = end mill Ø10 mm).
- Determine the **cutting parameters** — Spindle Speed (N) and Feed Rate (f).

### Stage 3: Tool Path Planning
- Decide the **entry point** and **exit point** of the cutter.
- Plot the cutter center path (accounting for tool radius if using **cutter compensation G41/G42**).
- Identify **rapid approach** (G00) zones vs. **cutting feed** (G01) zones.

### Stage 4: Coding the Program
- Write the program block-by-block following the structure: **Line Number → Preparatory → Coordinates → Feed/Speed → Miscellaneous → End of Block**.
- Use absolute (G90) or incremental (G91) mode as per convenience.

### Stage 5: Simulation & Verification
- Run the program in a **CNC simulator** (e.g., FANUC CNC Guide, Pro/ENGINEER, Mastercam Simulator, or any open-source simulator like CNCSimulator Pro).
- Check for: tool collisions, over-travel of machine limits, incorrect feed/speed, wrong tool selection, and proper coolant usage.
- Iterate the program until simulation shows a clean, error-free cut.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

These are the **core engineering formulas** used in manual NC programming for calculating feed, speed, and verifying tool paths.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ | $N$ in **rev/min (rpm)**, $V_c$ in **m/min**, $D$ in **mm** |
| 2 | Feed Rate | $f_r = N \cdot z \cdot f_z$ | $f_r$ in **mm/min**, $z$ = number of teeth, $f_z$ in **mm/tooth** |
| 3 | Metal Removal Rate | $MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot f_r$ | $a_p$ = depth of cut (**mm**), $a_e$ = width (**mm**), $f_r$ in **mm/min** |
| 4 | Cutting Time | $t_m = \dfrac{L}{f_r}$ | $L$ = tool path length (**mm**), $t_m$ in **min** |
| 5 | Distance between two points (2D) | $L = \sqrt{(X_2 - X_1)^2 + (Y_2 - Y_1)^2}$ | $L$ in **mm** |
| 6 | Distance between two points (3D) | $L = \sqrt{(X_2 - X_1)^2 + (Y_2 - Y_1)^2 + (Z_2 - Z_1)^2}$ | $L$ in **mm** |
| 7 | Cutter Compensation Offset | $C = R_{tool} + \delta$ | $C$ = compensation distance (**mm**), $R_{tool}$ = tool radius, $\delta$ = offset sign |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** A common mistake students make is using $N$ (rpm) where $f_r$ (mm/min) is required, or vice versa. **Spindle speed is in revolutions per minute; feed rate is in linear distance per minute.** They are *not* the same!

## 2.3 G-Code & M-Code Reference Table (Essential for Lab & Viva)

| Code | Function | Category |
|---|---|---|
| G00 | Rapid Traverse (Positioning) | Motion |
| G01 | Linear Interpolation (Cutting feed) | Motion |
| G02 | Circular Interpolation — Clockwise (CW) | Motion |
| G03 | Circular Interpolation — Counter-Clockwise (CCW) | Motion |
| G17 / G18 / G19 | Select XY / XZ / YZ plane for circular moves | Plane Selection |
| G20 / G21 | Input in inches / millimetres | Unit Selection |
| G28 | Return to Reference Point (Home) | Reference |
| G40 | Cutter Compensation Cancel | Compensation |
| G41 | Cutter Compensation Left | Compensation |
| G42 | Cutter Compensation Right | Compensation |
| G43 | Tool Length Compensation Positive | Compensation |
| G54–G59 | Select Work Coordinate System 1–6 | Coordinate |
| G80 | Cancel Canned Cycle | Canned Cycle |
| G81–G89 | Drilling / Tapping / Boring Canned Cycles | Canned Cycle |
| G90 | Absolute Programming | Mode |
| G91 | Incremental Programming | Mode |
| G94 / G95 | Feed per minute / Feed per revolution | Feed Mode |
| M00 | Program Stop | Misc. |
| M01 | Optional Stop | Misc. |
| M02 | Program End | Misc. |
| M03 | Spindle ON (CW) | Misc. |
| M04 | Spindle ON (CCW) | Misc. |
| M05 | Spindle OFF | Misc. |
| M06 | Automatic Tool Change | Misc. |
| M08 | Coolant ON (Flood) | Misc. |
| M09 | Coolant OFF | Misc. |
| M30 | Program End & Reset | Misc. |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

NC manual programming is the **backbone of precision manufacturing** in industries producing:
- **Aerospace components** (turbine blades, structural ribs) where tolerances are ±0.01 mm.
- **Automotive prototypes** and **die/mould manufacturing** where complex 3D contours are routine.
- **Medical implants** (orthopaedic parts) where manual programming ensures full control over every cutter movement.
- **Defence and tooling industries** where CAM automation is restricted due to security/IP concerns.

> [!NOTE]
> Even with advanced CAM software, every manufacturing engineer **must know** manual G-code programming. CAM output is essentially auto-generated G-code — if a CAM system fails or is unavailable, the engineer falls back on manual coding.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Since this is a *lab-oriented* topic, the analytical work focuses on **tool path distance calculations**, **machining parameter derivations**, and a **complete worked sample NC program** with line-by-line explanations.

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Cutting Time for a Rectangular Pocket

**Problem Statement (Lab Type Calculation):**
A rectangular pocket of dimensions **100 mm × 60 mm** is to be machined on a vertical milling machine using an end mill of diameter $D = 10$ mm. The depth of cut is $a_p = 5$ mm. The cutting speed is $V_c = 30$ m/min, feed per tooth $f_z = 0.05$ mm/tooth, and the end mill has $z = 2$ flutes. Calculate:
(a) Spindle speed $N$
(b) Feed rate $f_r$
(c) Total cutting time $t_m$ (assume the tool follows the full inner perimeter)

### Step 1: Convert Cutting Speed to mm/min

We know that spindle speed formula is:

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$$

Substituting $V_c = 30$ m/min and $D = 10$ mm:

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot 30}{\pi \cdot 10}$$

$$N = \frac{30000}{31.4159}$$

$$N \approx 955 \text{ rev/min (rpm)}$$

> This is the rotational speed of the spindle. In practice, this is rounded to the nearest standard available spindle speed on the machine (e.g., 1000 rpm).

### Step 2: Calculate the Feed Rate

The feed rate formula is:

$$f_r = N \cdot z \cdot f_z$$

Substituting $N = 955$ rpm, $z = 2$, $f_z = 0.05$ mm/tooth:

$$f_r = 955 \cdot 2 \cdot 0.05$$

$$f_r = 955 \cdot 0.10$$

$$f_r = 95.5 \text{ mm/min}$$

> This is the linear speed at which the table feeds the workpiece under the rotating cutter.

### Step 3: Calculate the Perimeter (Tool Path Length)

The cutter traces the **inner perimeter** of the pocket. Since the end mill is 10 mm diameter, the cutter center follows a path **5 mm (radius) inside** the actual pocket walls.

The inner dimensions of the cutter path are:
- Inner length $L_{inner} = 100 - 2 \cdot 5 = 90$ mm
- Inner width $W_{inner} = 60 - 2 \cdot 5 = 50$ mm

The perimeter of this inner rectangle is:

$$P = 2 \cdot (L_{inner} + W_{inner})$$

$$P = 2 \cdot (90 + 50)$$

$$P = 2 \cdot 140$$

$$P = 280 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Cutting Time

The cutting time formula is:

$$t_m = \frac{L}{f_r}$$

Substituting $L = 280$ mm and $f_r = 95.5$ mm/min:

$$t_m = \frac{280}{95.5}$$

$$t_m} \approx 2.93 \text{ minutes}$$

### Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tool Diameter | $D$ | 10 | mm |
| Cutting Speed | $V_c$ | 30 | m/min |
| Feed per Tooth | $f_z$ | 0.05 | mm/tooth |
| Number of Flutes | $z$ | 2 | — |
| Spindle Speed | $N$ | 955 | rpm |
| Feed Rate | $f_r$ | 95.5 | mm/min |
| Tool Path Perimeter | $P$ | 280 | mm |
| Cutting Time | $t_m$ | 2.93 | min |

## 3.2 Worked Sample: Manual NC Program for a Simple Profile

**Given:** Mill a rectangular pocket 80 mm × 50 mm, depth 3 mm, on a part whose top surface is at Z=0. The part datum is at the bottom-left corner of the pocket on the top surface. The pocket is opened from above; tool is a Ø10 mm end mill, set as T01. Use absolute programming (G90), in mm (G21), and cutter compensation (G41) to the left of the path.

### Step-by-Step Program with Explanation

```
O0001              ; Program number O0001 (mandatory header)
G21 G90 G17        ; Block 1: mm mode, absolute, XY plane
G54                ; Block 2: Activate Work Coordinate System #1
M06 T01            ; Block 3: Tool change to T01 (Ø10 end mill)
M03 S1000          ; Block 4: Spindle ON clockwise at 1000 rpm
G00 Z25.0          ; Block 5: Rapid up to safe Z height 25 mm
G00 X-15.0 Y-15.0  ; Block 6: Rapid to start position (outside the pocket)
G01 Z-3.0 F50      ; Block 7: Plunge into cut at depth 3 mm, feed 50 mm/min
G41 D01            ; Block 8: Cutter compensation LEFT, offset reg. D01
G01 X0 Y0 F100     ; Block 9: Move to corner at feed 100 mm/min
G01 X80.0 Y0       ; Block 10: Cut along +X to (80, 0)
G01 X80.0 Y50.0    ; Block 11: Cut along +Y to (80, 50)
G01 X0 Y50.0       ; Block 12: Cut along -X to (0, 50)
G01 X0 Y0          ; Block 13: Cut along -Y to (0, 0) — pocket closed
G40                ; Block 14: Cancel cutter compensation
G00 Z25.0          ; Block 15: Retract tool to safe Z
G00 X-15.0 Y-15.0  ; Block 16: Return to start position
M05                ; Block 17: Spindle OFF
M09                ; Block 18: Coolant OFF
M30                ; Block 19: Program end and rewind
```

### Explanation of Critical Lines

- **Line 1 (Header):** The `O` number identifies the program in the controller memory. KTU labs expect a valid header.
- **Line 7 (Plunge):** This is where most students make mistakes. Plunging in G00 (rapid) directly into the workpiece is **dangerous** — always use G01 for any vertical entry into material.
- **Line 8 (Compensation):** G41 D01 enables left-hand compensation using the offset value stored in register D01. Without this, the cutter would follow the programmed line, not the actual pocket edge.
- **Line 19 (M30):** Unlike M02, M30 also **rewinds the program** to the beginning, ready for the next part.

## 3.3 Verification of Circular Interpolation (G02 / G03)

**Problem:** Program a clockwise circular arc from point **A (10, 10)** to point **B (30, 30)** with a center at **C (30, 10)**. Verify the I, K (or I, J) parameters.

### Step 1: Identify Direction
Looking down the +Z axis (toward the workpiece), the arc goes from (10,10) to (30,30) sweeping through the lower-right quadrant. This is **Clockwise (CW)**, hence **G02**.

### Step 2: Calculate the Radius

$$R = \sqrt{(X_B - X_A)^2 + (Y_B - Y_A)^2}$$

$$R = \sqrt{(30-10)^2 + (30-10)^2} = \sqrt{400 + 400} = \sqrt{800} \approx 28.28 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3: Calculate I, J (Incremental Distance from Start to Center)

For **G90 absolute** mode, I and J are the **incremental distances** from the *start point* to the *arc center*:

$$I = X_C - X_A = 30 - 10 = 20 \text{ mm}$$

$$J = Y_C - Y_A = 10 - 10 = 0 \text{ mm}$$

> Note: The arc **center** is at (30, 10), not the midpoint of the chord. The radius of the arc is the distance from A to C, which is 20 mm, not 28.28 mm. (The 28.28 mm is the chord length, not the radius — a classic student trap!)

### Step 4: Final Block

```
G02 X30.0 Y30.0 I20.0 J0.0 F80
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Manual NC Part Programming Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engineering Drawing] --> B[Process Planning]
    B --> C[Tool Selection]
    B --> D[Cutting Parameters Vc fz]
    C --> E[Tool Path Sketch]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Write NC Program in ISO 6983 Codes]
    F --> G[Enter Program into Simulator]
    G --> H{Simulation Run}
    H -- Collision or Error --> I[Edit and Debug Program]
    I --> F
    H -- Clean and Correct --> J[Verify on CNC Machine]
    J --> K[Actual Machining on Workpiece]
    K --> L[Quality Inspection]
    L --> M[Final Part Accepted]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **closed feedback loop** between the simulator and the code editor is the heart of NC programming. Even experienced programmers never run code on a real machine without simulation first.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: CNC Machine Subsystem Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CTRL[Controller Unit]
        CPU[CPU and Memory]
        INT[Interpreter Decoder]
        INTF[Machine Interface]
    end
    subgraph DRV[Drive System]
        SPN[Spindle Drive]
        SX[Servo Drive X Axis]
        SY[Servo Drive Y Axis]
        SZ[Servo Drive Z Axis]
    end
    subgraph MS[Machine Structure]
        SP[Spindle with Tool]
        TB[Work Table]
        BC[Bed and Column]
    end
    subgraph AUX[Auxiliary Systems]
        CL[Coolant Pump]
        TC[Tool Changer]
        FB[Feedback Sensors Encoders]
    end
    CPU --> INT
    INT --> INTF
    INTF --> SPN
    INTF --> SX
    INTF --> SY
    INTF --> SZ
    INTF --> CL
    INTF --> TC
    SPN --> SP
    SX --> TB
    SY --> TB
    SZ --> SP
    FB --> INTF
    BC --> SP
    BC --> TB
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Choosing G90 vs G91

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Programming Mode Selection] --> Q{Is the part datum clearly defined?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Use G90 Absolute Mode]
    Q -- No or Repetitive Patterns --> S[Use G91 Incremental Mode]
    R --> T[All coordinates given from datum origin]
    S --> U[All coordinates given as distance from previous point]
    T --> V[Best for one-off parts and contours]
    U --> W[Best for subroutines and bolt circle patterns]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Simulation Software Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NC Program File] --> B[G-code Parser]
    B --> C[Interpreter Module]
    C --> D[Kinematic Model]
    D --> E[3D Virtual Machine]
    E --> F[Tool and Workpiece Rendering]
    E --> G[Collision Detection Engine]
    E --> H[Cycle Time Estimator]
    G --> I{Error Detected?}
    H --> J[Performance Report]
    I -- Yes --> K[Alarm Message and Line Number]
    I -- No --> L[Simulation Passes]
    K --> M[Programmer Edits Code]
    M --> A
    L --> N[Download to Actual CNC Machine]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**  
> **Define NC manual part programming. List any four G-codes with their functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

NC manual part programming is the method of writing part programs for a CNC machine in the standardized ISO 6983 (DIN 66025) alphanumeric code format, where every machine movement and auxiliary function is coded manually by the programmer without using CAM software.

**Four G-codes:**
1. **G00** — Rapid traverse / positioning at maximum machine feed.
2. **G01** — Linear interpolation at programmed feed rate.
3. **G02** — Circular interpolation in the clockwise direction.
4. **G03** — Circular interpolation in the counter-clockwise direction.

*(Full marks: 1 for definition + 0.5 each for the four codes = 3 marks)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**  
> **What is the role of simulation in NC part programming? Name any two CNC simulators used in industry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Simulation is the virtual execution of the NC program on a computer-based model of the CNC machine before running it on the actual machine. It serves to:
1. **Detect collisions** between the tool, workpiece, fixtures, and machine components.
2. **Verify tool paths**, depths, and tool changes for logical correctness.
3. **Estimate cycle time** and optimize feed/speed parameters.
4. **Prevent costly machine damage** and material wastage.

**Two CNC Simulators:** (i) **FANUC CNC Guide**, (ii) **CNCSimulator Pro** (or Mastercam Simulator, Pro-E, etc.).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]
> 
> **(a)** Explain the stepwise procedure for **manual part programming** for a CNC vertical milling machine. List the safety and process planning checks. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** For a milling operation with a 4-flute end mill of diameter 12 mm, cutting speed 25 m/min, and feed per tooth 0.04 mm/tooth, on mild steel:  
> (i) Calculate the spindle speed and feed rate.  
> (ii) If the tool path length is 450 mm, find the cutting time. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Stepwise Manual Part Programming Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Drawing Analysis (1 mark):** Study the part drawing, identify the datum, list all dimensions, tolerances, and surface finish requirements.
2. **Process Planning (1 mark):** Decide the sequence of operations (e.g., facing → drilling → pocket milling → profiling), fixturing method, and work-holding devices.
3. **Tool Selection (1 mark):** Choose appropriate tools (end mill, face mill, drill, tap) and assign tool numbers (T01, T02, …).
4. **Cutting Parameter Selection (1 mark):** Use machinability handbooks or standard tables to determine cutting speed $V_c$, feed per tooth $f_z$, and depth of cut $a_p$ based on the workpiece material and tool material.
5. **Tool Path Planning (1 mark):** Sketch the cutter center path, including entry/exit points, rapid and feed moves, and decide on cutter compensation (G41/G42).
6. **Program Writing (1 mark):** Write the program block-by-block using the structure: Header → Safety block → Tool change → Spindle ON → Rapid to start → Plunge → Cutting moves → Retract → Spindle OFF → Program end.
7. **Simulation and Verification (1 mark):** Run the program in a CNC simulator; check for collisions, syntax errors, and proper tool changes. Iterate until clean.

**Safety checks include:** coolant ON before cutting, dry-run at reduced feed, single-block mode for first run, and proper tool offset entry.

---

**(b) Calculations (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 12$ mm, $V_c = 25$ m/min, $f_z = 0.04$ mm/tooth, $z = 4$, $L = 450$ mm.

**(i) Spindle Speed (3.5 marks):**

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$$

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot 25}{\pi \cdot 12}$$

$$N = \frac{25000}{37.699}$$

$$N \approx 663 \text{ rpm}$$

**Feed Rate (3.5 marks):**

$$f_r = N \cdot z \cdot f_z$$

$$f_r = 663 \cdot 4 \cdot 0.04$$

$$f_r = 663 \cdot 0.16$$

$$f_r = 106.08 \text{ mm/min}$$

**(ii) Cutting Time (Alternative distribution):**

$$t_m = \frac{L}{f_r} = \frac{450}{106.08} \approx 4.24 \text{ minutes}$$

> This is now reported with the spindle speed and feed rate per part (a)/(b) — total **7 marks** distributed across these calculations.

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Internal Choice]
> 
> **(a)** Differentiate between **absolute (G90) and incremental (G91) programming** with a neat sketch. Given the following coordinates of a profile, write the same program in both modes:  
> Start: (10, 10) → P1: (50, 10) → P2: (50, 40) → P3: (10, 40) → back to Start. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Explain with a block diagram the **role of simulation in NC part programming**. Mention any **four common errors** detected during simulation. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) G90 vs G91 with Sample Program (7 marks):**

**Differences Table (3.5 marks):**

| Feature | G90 Absolute | G91 Incremental |
|---|---|---|
| Reference | All coordinates are from the part datum (origin) | All coordinates are from the previous point |
| Notation | $X, Y, Z$ are target positions | $U, V, W$ sometimes used, or $X, Y, Z$ for delta |
| Best for | Single, clearly-dimensioned parts | Bolt-hole circles, subroutines, repetitive patterns |
| Risk | Re-typing required if origin changes | Errors accumulate if a wrong block is written |

**Programs (3.5 marks):**

Assuming start at (10, 10), depth Z = -5, in absolute mode:

```
G90 G01 X50.0 Y10.0 F100   ; Move to P1
G01 X50.0 Y40.0            ; Move to P2
G01 X10.0 Y40.0            ; Move to P3
G01 X10.0 Y10.0            ; Back to Start
```

In incremental mode (distances from previous point):

```
G91 G01 X40.0 Y0 F100      ; +40 in X to P1
G01 X0 Y30.0               ; +30 in Y to P2
G01 X-40.0 Y0              ; -40 in X to P3
G01 X0 Y-30.0              ; -30 in Y back to Start
```

---

**(b) Simulation Role and Common Errors (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (3.5 marks):** A typical CNC simulator has the following modules in functional flow:

```
NC Program → G-code Parser → Interpreter → Kinematic Engine → 3D Virtual Machine
            → Collision Detector + Time Estimator → Error Report / Pass Message
```

*(A clean schematic with these five blocks and feedback to the programmer is sufficient.)*

**Four Common Errors Detected (3.5 marks):**
1. **Tool-Workpiece Collision** — the cutter crashes into the part because of an incorrect coordinate.
2. **Spindle-Workpiece Collision** — rapid traverse in Z leads to crashing the spinning tool into the surface.
3. **Over-Travel / Limit Violation** — coordinates exceed the machine's physical travel limits on any axis.
4. **Syntax / Modal Conflict** — two G-codes from the same modal group appear in the same block (e.g., G00 and G01 together).
5. *(Optional)* Wrong tool selected, or coolant path intersecting clamps.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in NC Programming questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the safe Z-retract block** before tool change. This is mandatory in any KTU-evaluated program. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 2. **Using G00 to plunge** into the workpiece instead of G01. Rapid vertical motion into material **breaks the cutter** and is considered a programming error. Marks lost: 1.
> 3. **Missing cutter compensation activation (G41/G42)** before the first cutting move, and missing **G40 cancellation** at the end. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 4. **Confusing I, J with absolute center coordinates.** In G90 mode, I and J are *incremental distances* from start point to arc center, not the absolute center coordinates. Marks lost: 2.
> 5. **Forgetting M30 (or at minimum M05 + M02)** at program end, leaving the spindle running. Marks lost: 0.5–1.
> 6. **In calculation problems, mixing units** — using $V_c$ in m/min and $D$ in mm correctly is fine, but if a student uses $V_c$ in mm/min without the 1000 conversion, the answer is off by 1000×. Marks lost: 1–2.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **NC Manual Programming** = writing G/M-code programs *by hand* using ISO 6983 standard, without CAM automation.
- **Simulation** = virtual dry-run on a computer to detect errors *before* real machining.
- **Block structure** of an NC line: `N__ G__ X__ Y__ Z__ F__ S__ T__ M__` (address letter + number = "word").
- **Three motion G-codes** to remember: **G00** (rapid), **G01** (linear), **G02/G03** (circular CW/CCW).
- **Programming modes:** **G90** absolute (from datum), **G91** incremental (from previous point).
- **Spindle control:** **M03** (CW), **M04** (CCW), **M05** (OFF). Always pair M03/M04 with **S____** for rpm.
- **Cutter compensation:** **G41** (left), **G42** (right), **G40** (cancel). Used for profile milling to offset the tool radius.
- **Spindle speed formula:** $N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$.
- **Feed rate formula:** $f_r = N \cdot z \cdot f_z$.
- **Cutting time formula:** $t_m = \dfrac{L}{f_r}$.
- **Always plunge** with **G01**, never G00, into the workpiece.
- **Always retract to safe Z** before tool changes and at program end.
- **Always end** the program with **M30** (preferred) or **M02**.
- **Simulation checks** for: collisions, over-travels, wrong tool, coolant status, modal conflicts, and cycle time.
- **For circular moves (G02/G03):** I and J are *incremental* (from start to center), R is the *radius*. Never mix these.
- **Lab record must include:** part drawing, process plan, tool list, cutting parameters, full NC program, and a simulation screenshot (mandatory for KTU viva).
